bodyMarkdown:
If you run an accountancy practice, a law firm or a consultancy in the city, your website is quietly doing more work than you realise. It is often the first thing a prospective client sees, long before they pick up the phone or walk into your office. Good websites for accountants in Leeds — and for solicitors and consultants too — all do one thing above all else: they make a stranger feel safe handing over their money, their business or their legal problem.

Leeds is one of the biggest financial and legal centres outside London, which is both a blessing and a problem. There is plenty of work here, but there are also plenty of firms chasing it. A tired, slow or confusing website quietly hands your enquiries to the practice down the road. Here is what genuinely moves the needle for professional services in the city, and where most local firms are leaving money on the table.

## Why professional websites in Leeds have to earn trust first

Walk around Park Square or East Parade and you will pass more solicitors, accountants and chartered surveyors in five minutes than most towns have in total. Around Wellington Place and the regenerated South Bank, newer consultancies and advisory firms open every month. Your prospective client knows they have choices, so the job of your website is to remove doubt, and to do it fast.

Trust online is built from small, concrete signals. Real photographs of your team and your office beat stock pictures of glass towers every time. Your professional bodies — ICAEW, ACCA, the SRA, a chartered status — belong somewhere visible, not hidden away. Named people with real credentials reassure a nervous first-time client far more than a paragraph about 'bespoke solutions'. You can see how we handle that in our [recent work](/work).

> People don't hire a firm they can't quite figure out. Clarity is the most underrated form of credibility.

## What websites for accountants in Leeds need to get right

Whether you handle year-end accounts, conveyancing or management consultancy, the fundamentals are the same. A professional site that wins work in Leeds usually gets these right:

A well-planned [website design and build](/services/website-design) covers all five without you having to become a marketing expert overnight.

- **A plain-English list of services.** Say exactly what you do — self-assessment, payroll, commercial property, employment law — not vague 'solutions'. People search for the specific thing they need.
- **Who you help.** A practice for sole traders and small businesses across West Yorkshire reads very differently to one chasing corporate clients. Be clear which you are.
- **Proof you can be trusted.** Genuine reviews, real case examples, accreditations and years in practice, placed close to your calls to action.
- **A fast site that works on a phone.** Most first visits to a local firm's website now happen on mobile, often between meetings.
- **One obvious way to get in touch.** A single clear next step on every page, not a maze of competing links.

## Make it effortless to get in touch

Professional clients are usually busy and often a little anxious. A business owner facing an HMRC deadline, or a director dealing with a dispute, wants to reach a human quickly. Bury your phone number in the footer and you will lose them.

Put your number, email and a short enquiry form where they are impossible to miss, ideally on every page. If you see clients at an office near Park Row or Greek Street, a small map and clear opening hours save everyone a phone call. And when someone does make contact, a small team that actually picks up the phone is worth more than any clever animation. We build every site with a simple admin panel, so updating your details or adding a service takes minutes rather than a support ticket.

## Show up when Leeds searches for you

A handsome website that nobody can find does not pay the bills. When someone types 'accountant near me' or 'solicitor in Leeds' into their phone, you want to be on that first screen. That is the job of [local SEO](/services/seo) and a well-kept Google Business Profile.

Getting found locally comes down to a few unglamorous things: consistent name, address and phone details across the web; a Google Business Profile with real reviews; and pages that genuinely speak to your patch. A firm in Horsforth or Chapel Allerton serving nearby LS postcodes will often do better naming those areas honestly than trying to rank for the whole of Yorkshire. Content that answers the questions clients actually ask — about tax deadlines, buying a first home, or setting up a limited company — builds quiet authority over time.

## What it costs and how long it takes

Most firms want two things from a web project: no surprises on price, and to be live before their next busy season. We work to [fixed prices](/pricing) rather than hourly billing, so you know the cost before we start, and most sites go live within three to six weeks.

You own the site, the domain and your data outright, so there is no lock-in and nobody holding your website hostage if you ever decide to move on. Every build includes thirty days of aftercare, and an optional care plan keeps everything updated, backed up and secure afterwards. If you are not sure what your current site is doing well or badly, that is exactly what a free teardown is for.

## The quick wins

- In a crowded market like Leeds, your website's first job is to make a stranger trust you.
- Say plainly what you do, who you help and why you can be trusted — vague 'solutions' language loses enquiries.
- Most first visits happen on mobile, so speed and an obvious way to make contact matter more than clever design.
- Fixed prices, most sites live in three to six weeks, and you own everything — no lock-in.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a Leeds accountancy or law firm cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the figure before we start. Prices start at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, with care plans from **£27 a month**. SEO work and redesigns are quoted per project once we understand what you need.

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live within **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly we get your content and sign-off. We will give you a clear timeline at the start so you can plan around your busy periods.

### Do I own my website and domain?

Yes. You own your site, your domain and your data outright — there is no lock-in. If you ever decide to work with someone else, everything is yours to take with you.

### Will it help me rank on Google in Leeds?

A well-built site with genuine [local SEO](/services/seo) and a tidy Google Business Profile gives you the best chance of being found when people nearby search. We can't promise specific rankings — nobody honestly can — but we build on the things Google actually rewards.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website. We walk through what is working, what is quietly costing you enquiries, and the changes that would make the biggest difference — no obligation to do anything with it.

Miss any of them and the visitor simply backs out and opens the next result — and you never even know they were there. Professional buyers are cautious by nature, so every unanswered question is a reason to leave.

## Trust is the whole job

For professional services, credibility is the product. A confident, well-built site quietly signals that you run a serious firm — and a dated or clunky one signals the opposite, whatever the truth of it is. A few things do most of the reassuring:

None of this is design for its own sake. It's about removing the small reasons a cautious visitor gives themselves to click away. A tidy [website design and build](/services/website-design) makes each of those trust signals easy to find in seconds, instead of hidden three clicks deep.

- Your qualifications and bodies — ICAEW, ACCA, ACA or SRA regulation — shown plainly, not buried in the footer.
- Real photos of your team and your office, not stock images of strangers in suits.
- A genuine Reading address and phone number, so people know you're local and reachable.
- A handful of recent reviews or client outcomes placed near your calls to action, where doubt is highest.
- Plain language about fees or how you price, so nobody fears a mystery bill.

## Make your services unmistakable

"Accountancy services" tells a visitor nothing. The contractor who wants IR35 advice, the café owner who needs their VAT sorted and the founder raising investment are three different people with three different worries. Spell your services out in their words — self-assessment, limited-company accounts, payroll, tax planning, conveyancing, employment law, whatever you genuinely do — so each person recognises their own problem on the page. Consultants have the hardest job of all: the vaguer your offer sounds, the harder you have to work to describe the exact outcome a client walks away with.

> A visitor should recognise their own problem on your page within seconds — or they assume you don't solve it.

## Local signals that help you show up in Reading

Being findable is half the battle. Most people looking for a professional nearby start on Google, usually on a phone, and many never scroll past the map results at the top. That's why a complete, active Google Business Profile and consistent contact details across the web matter as much as the site itself. Name the areas you genuinely serve — Caversham, Earley, Woodley, Tilehurst, Wokingham, Henley — because that's how people actually search, and it helps Google connect you to Reading. A focused approach to [local SEO](/services/seo) is often the difference between page one and page five in a competitive Thames Valley market.

## Make it effortless to get in touch

Once you've earned a little trust, don't make people work to act on it. A number that taps straight to a call on mobile, a short enquiry form that doesn't demand their life story, and a clear line on what happens next — each one lowers the barrier. Professional buyers here are busy; plenty will weigh up their options on the train into Paddington or between meetings, so the site has to work beautifully on a phone first. And once it's live, a bit of steady care keeps it fast, secure and up to date — which is exactly what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for.

## What it costs — and how to start

You don't need a five-figure budget to look like the safe choice. We build on [fixed prices](/pricing), so you know the cost up front — no hourly billing, no lock-in, and you own your site, domain and data. Most builds go live in three to six weeks. If you'd like to see the approach in practice first, our [recent work](/work) shows the same trust-first thinking across different local businesses. The easiest first step costs nothing: a short, screen-recorded teardown of your current site that points out exactly what's helping and what's quietly costing you enquiries.

## The quick wins

- For accountants, solicitors and consultants, trust is the product — credentials, real faces and a local Reading address do most of the selling.
- Spell out your services in your clients' own words so each visitor recognises their own problem straight away.
- A complete Google Business Profile and local SEO help you show up when Thames Valley clients search on their phones.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for an accountancy or law firm in Reading cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the figure before we start. Starter builds begin at £477, Growth at £1,170 and Complete at £2,370, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and full redesigns are quoted per project. There's no hourly billing and no surprise invoice at the end.

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how much content and how many pages you need. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you're not left on your own once it's live.

### Do I own my website and domain?

Yes. You own your site, your domain and your data outright — there's no lock-in. If you ever decide to move on, everything is yours to take with you. We're a small UK team you deal with directly, and we pick up the phone.

### Can you help me rank for local searches in Reading?

We can't promise specific rankings — nobody honestly can — but we build sites with sound local SEO and set up your Google Business Profile properly, which is what helps you show up when people nearby search for an accountant, solicitor or consultant. It's steady work, not a magic switch.

### What exactly is a free teardown?

It's a short screen-recorded review of your current website, where we walk through what's working, what's confusing a visitor and what's quietly costing you enquiries. It's genuinely free, there's no obligation, and you keep the recording whether or not you decide to work with us.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run an accountancy practice, a law firm or a consultancy in Guildford, your website has one job before all the others: make a stranger believe you're a safe pair of hands. Well-built websites for accountants in Guildford — and for solicitors and consultants — earn that trust in seconds. A dated one quietly hands the enquiry to the firm three doors down on Chertsey Street.

Professional services here compete for a discerning, well-informed audience: business owners, commuters heading to Waterloo, and founders out at the Surrey Research Park. They look you up online long before they pick up the phone. This guide covers what your site needs to do to turn that quiet research into a booked first meeting — the same thinking we apply across the [local industries](/industries) we build for.

## Why a strong website matters more in Guildford

Guildford is not a town where you can coast on a brass name plaque and a listing in an old directory. The High Street, from the Guildhall clock down to the River Wey, is lined with established firms, and the professional cluster runs well beyond the centre — out through Onslow Village and Burpham and around the Research Park. Your prospective client is almost certainly weighing up three or four practices at once, often on their phone over a lunch break.

Then there's the London factor. A lot of Guildford residents commute into the City and are used to fast, polished, reassuring websites. Held to that standard, a slow site with tiny text and a contact form that breaks on mobile sends an unfortunate signal: that the firm behind it might be a little behind the times too. Fair or not, that is the judgement people quietly make.

## What makes websites for accountants Guildford clients actually trust

Trust in professional services is built from small, concrete signals — not stock photos of handshakes. When we review a [professional services website](/services/website-design), these are the things that genuinely move the needle:

None of it is flashy. All of it quietly answers the question every visitor is really asking: can I trust these people with something that matters to me.

- Clearly named services. 'Self-assessment', 'year-end accounts', 'commercial property' — say exactly what you do, not 'bespoke solutions'.
- Visible credentials. Your ICAEW, ACCA or SRA registration, chartered status and years in practice, shown where people actually look.
- Real faces and a real address. A photo of your team and your Guildford office builds more trust than any clever tagline.
- Genuine testimonials. A few honest words from named local clients beat a wall of anonymous star ratings.
- Straight talk on how you work. What happens at the first meeting, how you charge, and how quickly you reply.

## Make getting in touch effortless

Once someone decides you might be the right fit, don't make them work for it. Most people looking up a solicitor or accountant in Guildford are doing it on a phone, often in the evening. That calls for a click-to-call button that actually dials, a short enquiry form that isn't a chore, and a clear line on how soon you'll get back to them.

Bury your phone number in the footer, or push a nervous first-time client through ten form fields, and you'll lose people who were ready to book. More often than not, the firm that wins the enquiry is simply the one that made the next step obvious.

## Getting found by people searching in Guildford

None of this counts if you don't show up when someone searches. Local search is its own craft: 'accountant near me', 'GU1 solicitor' and 'small business accountant Guildford' all surface the map pack first, and most people never scroll past it.

Two things decide whether you appear there — a properly set-up and maintained Google Business Profile, with an accurate address, categories, opening hours and recent reviews, and a website that is clearly, genuinely rooted in Guildford. Our [local SEO](/services/seo) work focuses on exactly that: helping Surrey firms rank for the searches their future clients are already typing.

> Most people looking for a local accountant never scroll past the first few map results — so simply being there is half the battle.

## Fixed prices, and the site is yours

Professional firms tend to be wary of open-ended bills, and reasonably so — you send enough of them yourselves. Our websites are **fixed-price**, so you know the cost before we start: Starter from £477, Growth from £1,170 and Complete from £2,370, with optional [care plans](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month if you'd rather we handle updates and security. The full breakdown is on our [pricing page](/pricing).

**You own your site, your domain and your data** — no lock-in, and nothing held hostage if you ever decide to move on. Most builds go live in three to six weeks, every one includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, and you deal with a small UK team directly. We pick up the phone.

## The quick wins

- For a Guildford professional firm, the website's first job is to earn trust fast — clear services, visible credentials and real faces.
- Make contact effortless: click-to-call, a short form and a clear reply time, all working properly on mobile.
- Local SEO and a well-kept Google Business Profile decide whether Guildford searchers ever find you.
- Fixed prices from £477, you own everything, and most sites go live in three to six weeks.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a Guildford accountancy or law firm cost?

Our websites are fixed-price so there are no surprises: Starter from £477, Growth from £1,170 and Complete from £2,370. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or redesigns are quoted per project once we understand what you need.

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live in three to six weeks. The timeline mainly depends on how many pages you need and how quickly you can get us your content, photos and any credentials you want featured.

### Do I actually own the website?

Yes. You own your site, your domain and your data outright — there's no lock-in and nothing held hostage. If you ever move on, everything goes with you.

### Can you help us get found for local searches in Guildford?

Yes. We handle local SEO and Google Business Profile setup so you show up for searches like 'accountant Guildford' or 'GU1 solicitor'. We won't promise guaranteed rankings — anyone who does isn't being straight with you — but we focus on the things that genuinely help.

### What is a free teardown?

It's a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out what's working, what's costing you enquiries and what we'd fix first. No obligation, and it's yours to keep.

excerpt: A straight-talking guide to what a small business website really costs in the UK, and how to avoid paying for the wrong things.

bodyMarkdown:
If you have ever asked how much a small business website costs, you already know the answer most agencies give: it depends. That is true, but it is not much help when you are trying to set a budget. So let us be more useful. In the UK, a professional small business website usually lands somewhere between a few hundred and a few thousand pounds, and the final figure comes down to a handful of things you can actually control.

Price on its own tells you very little, though. A £300 site and a £3,000 site can look almost identical in a screenshot and behave completely differently once real customers start using them. This guide breaks down the real ranges, explains what pushes the price up or down, and helps you spend on the things that actually bring in enquiries.

## The short answer: typical UK website costs

Here are the ranges we see most often for UK small businesses. Treat them as a rough map rather than a quote, because no two projects are quite the same.

Most local businesses, a plumber, a dental practice, a café or a firm of accountants, sit comfortably in the middle bands. You rarely need the most expensive option to look credible and win work.

- **DIY website builders** (Wix, Squarespace, GoDaddy): around £0 to £30 a month, plus a lot of your own time. Fine for testing an idea, limiting once you want to grow.
- **Freelancer or template site**: roughly £300 to £1,500 for a small brochure website, depending on how much is bespoke.
- **Small studio or agency**: around £1,000 to £5,000 for a properly built site with real copywriting, SEO groundwork and someone to call when you need help.
- **Larger agency or complex build**: £5,000 and up, usually for e-commerce, booking systems or bigger content sites.

## What actually drives the price

When a quote seems high, or suspiciously low, it usually comes down to these factors. Understanding them helps you compare like for like.

- **Number of pages** and how much is custom versus dropped into a template.
- **Copywriting**: whether you supply the words or the studio writes them for you.
- **Design**: an off-the-shelf theme costs less than a design built around your brand.
- **Features**: a simple contact form is cheap; online booking, payments or customer logins add time.
- **SEO groundwork**: proper page structure, speed and local setup so you can actually be found.
- **Support afterwards**: hosting, updates and someone to fix things when they break.

> Price tells you what you paid. It does not tell you what you got.

## Why the cheapest option often costs the most

A bargain website is only a bargain if it does its job. The most common regret we hear is from owners who paid a little, got a site that looked fine but never brought in enquiries, then paid again to have it rebuilt within a year or two. Cheap sites often skip the unglamorous work that actually matters: fast loading, clear structure, mobile layout and local SEO. Most local searches now happen on a phone, so a site that is awkward on mobile quietly loses you customers every week.

There is also the cost of your time. DIY builders are genuinely capable, but the hours you spend wrestling with layouts and settings are hours you are not running your business.

## What you should get for your money

A fair price should buy more than a nice-looking homepage. As a concrete example, our own [website design and build](/services/website-design) is fixed-price and starts at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for our Complete build. You can see exactly what sits in each on our [pricing](/pricing) page, with no hourly billing and no surprise invoices.

Whatever you choose and whoever you choose, look for a package that includes these basics:

Ongoing costs are normal and worth budgeting for. A [care plan](/services/care-plans) from around £27 a month covers hosting, updates and small changes, which is far cheaper than letting a neglected site drift out of date.

- A **mobile-first design** that looks right on the phones most of your customers use.
- **Clear copy** aimed at your customers, not a wall of jargon.
- The **basics of local SEO** so you can show up when people search nearby.
- A simple way to **edit the site yourself**, plus a period of aftercare once it goes live.
- Full **ownership** of your site, domain and content, so you are never locked in.

## How to spend wisely, not just less

The goal is not the cheapest website or the most expensive one. It is the site that pays for itself by turning visitors into enquiries. Before you commit, it helps to be clear on what you want the site to do and how you will know it is working.

Get those four things straight and the price question becomes far easier to answer, because you know exactly what you are buying.

- Decide on the **one action** you want most visitors to take, whether that is a call, a booking or a form.
- Ask any provider to show you **live examples** of businesses like yours.
- Check whether **ongoing SEO** is included or extra, and get it in writing. Our [local SEO](/services/seo) work, for instance, is quoted per project.
- Confirm **who owns what** and what happens if you ever move on.

## The quick wins

- Most UK small business websites cost between a few hundred and a few thousand pounds; the extremes are rarely necessary.
- Pages, copywriting, custom design, features, SEO and support are what move the price.
- The cheapest site often costs more once you rebuild it, so buy for results, not just a low headline figure.
- Fixed-price packages, like ours from £477, make budgeting far simpler than hourly billing.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a small business website cost in the UK?

For a professionally built site, most UK small businesses pay somewhere between roughly £300 and £5,000, depending on size and features. As a fixed-price example, our sites start at £477 for a Starter build, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, so you know the cost before you commit.

### How long does it take to build a small business website?

It depends on size and how quickly the content comes together, but most of our sites go live within 3 to 6 weeks. A simple brochure site is at the shorter end; sites with booking, payments or lots of pages take a little longer.

### Is it cheaper to build my own website?

DIY builders like Wix or Squarespace can cost as little as £0 to £30 a month, so the sticker price is lower. The real cost is your time and, often, a weaker result. They are great for testing an idea, but many owners move to a professionally built site once they are serious about winning work.

### Are there ongoing costs after the website is built?

Yes, and they are worth planning for. You will usually pay for hosting, a domain and updates. A care plan from around £27 a month bundles these together, keeps your site secure and up to date, and means small changes are handled for you rather than piling up.

### Do I own my website, or does the agency?

With us, you own your site, your domain and your content outright, with no lock-in. Always check this before you sign, because some cheap builders and agencies keep control of your domain or data, which makes moving on painful and expensive.

intro: If you have ever asked how much a small business website costs, you already know the answer most agencies give: it depends. That is true, but it is not much help when you are trying to set a budget. So let us be more useful. In the UK, a professional small business website usually lands somewhere between a few hundred and a few thousand pounds, and the final figure comes down to a handful of things you can actually control.

Price on its own tells you very little, though. A £300 site and a £3,000 site can look almost identical in a screenshot and behave completely differently once real customers start using them. This guide breaks down the real ranges, explains what pushes the price up or down, and helps you spend on the things that actually bring in enquiries.

sections.h2: The short answer: typical UK website costs
paragraphs: Here are the ranges we see most often for UK small businesses. Treat them as a rough map rather than a quote, because no two projects are quite the same.
Most local businesses, a plumber, a dental practice, a café or a firm of accountants, sit comfortably in the middle bands. You rarely need the most expensive option to look credible and win work.
bullets: **DIY website builders** (Wix, Squarespace, GoDaddy): around £0 to £30 a month, plus a lot of your own time. Fine for testing an idea, limiting once you want to grow.
**Freelancer or template site**: roughly £300 to £1,500 for a small brochure website, depending on how much is bespoke.
**Small studio or agency**: around £1,000 to £5,000 for a properly built site with real copywriting, SEO groundwork and someone to call when you need help.
**Larger agency or complex build**: £5,000 and up, usually for e-commerce, booking systems or bigger content sites.

sections.h2: What actually drives the price
paragraphs: When a quote seems high, or suspiciously low, it usually comes down to these factors. Understanding them helps you compare like for like.
bullets: **Number of pages** and how much is custom versus dropped into a template.
**Copywriting**: whether you supply the words or the studio writes them for you.
**Design**: an off-the-shelf theme costs less than a design built around your brand.
**Features**: a simple contact form is cheap; online booking, payments or customer logins add time.
**SEO groundwork**: proper page structure, speed and local setup so you can actually be found.
**Support afterwards**: hosting, updates and someone to fix things when they break.
sections.pullquote: Price tells you what you paid. It does not tell you what you got.

sections.h2: Why the cheapest option often costs the most
paragraphs: A bargain website is only a bargain if it does its job. The most common regret we hear is from owners who paid a little, got a site that looked fine but never brought in enquiries, then paid again to have it rebuilt within a year or two. Cheap sites often skip the unglamorous work that actually matters: fast loading, clear structure, mobile layout and local SEO. Most local searches now happen on a phone, so a site that is awkward on mobile quietly loses you customers every week.
There is also the cost of your time. DIY builders are genuinely capable, but the hours you spend wrestling with layouts and settings are hours you are not running your business.

sections.h2: What you should get for your money
paragraphs: A fair price should buy more than a nice-looking homepage. As a concrete example, our own [website design and build](/services/website-design) is fixed-price and starts at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for our Complete build. You can see exactly what sits in each on our [pricing](/pricing) page, with no hourly billing and no surprise invoices.
Whatever you choose and whoever you choose, look for a package that includes these basics:

Ongoing costs are normal and worth budgeting for. A [care plan](/services/care-plans) from around £27 a month covers hosting, updates and small changes, which is far cheaper than letting a neglected site drift out of date.
bullets: A **mobile-first design** that looks right on the phones most of your customers use.
**Clear copy** aimed at your customers, not a wall of jargon.
The **basics of local SEO** so you can show up when people search nearby.
A simple way to **edit the site yourself**, plus a period of aftercare once it goes live.
Full **ownership** of your site, domain and content, so you are never locked in.

sections.h2: How to spend wisely, not just less
paragraphs: The goal is not the cheapest website or the most expensive one. It is the site that pays for itself by turning visitors into enquiries. Before you commit, it helps to be clear on what you want the site to do and how you will know it is working.
Get those four things straight and the price question becomes far easier to answer, because you know exactly what you are buying.
bullets: Decide on the **one action** you want most visitors to take, whether that is a call, a booking or a form.
Ask any provider to show you **live examples** of businesses like yours.
Check whether **ongoing SEO** is included or extra, and get it in writing. Our [local SEO](/services/seo) work, for instance, is quoted per project.
Confirm **who owns what** and what happens if you ever move on.

keyTakeaways: Most UK small business websites cost between a few hundred and a few thousand pounds; the extremes are rarely necessary.
Pages, copywriting, custom design, features, SEO and support are what move the price.
The cheapest site often costs more once you rebuild it, so buy for results, not just a low headline figure.
Fixed-price packages, like ours from £477, make budgeting far simpler than hourly billing.

bodyMarkdown:
Ask three UK web designers for a quote and you'll get three very different numbers. One says £300, another says £3,000, and a third quietly mentions £10,000. It's enough to make you wonder whether anyone's telling the truth. So here is web design pricing explained in plain English: what you're really paying for, why the numbers swing so wildly, and how to tell a fair price from a bad one.

The short version is that a website isn't one thing. It's design, words, build, testing, and the ongoing job of keeping your site fast and found on Google. Price mostly tracks scope and skill, not a fixed shopping list. Once you can see what sits behind the number, quotes stop feeling like a lottery and you can buy with confidence.

## Why two websites can cost £500 or £5,000

A brochure site for a local plumber and a booking system for a busy dental practice are both called 'websites', but they're miles apart in work. The gap usually comes down to a few honest differences: how many pages you need, whether the design is a template or built for your brand, who writes the words, and how much thought goes into getting you found. A cheap site often skips the last two entirely. That's not a scam, it's just a smaller job. The trouble starts when the invoice looks the same but the work behind it doesn't.

There's also who you're buying from. A large agency carries account managers, offices and overheads that end up in your quote. A one-person freelancer is cheaper, but can go quiet the moment they get busy. A small studio sits in between: senior people doing the actual work, without the corporate mark-up, and close enough to pick up the phone when you call.

## Web design pricing explained: what you're really paying for

When you pay for a website, you're rarely paying for pages. You're paying for the decisions and craft that make those pages earn their keep. A fair price usually covers all of this:

Strip any of these out and the price drops. Strip too many and you end up with a good-looking site that nobody finds, or a cheap one you're quietly embarrassed by.

- **Strategy and structure** — working out what pages you need, what each should say, and the path you want visitors to take.
- **Design** — layout, colour, type and imagery that fit your brand and build trust in the first few seconds.
- **Copywriting** — clear, persuasive words that answer real customer questions, not filler text.
- **Build and testing** — clean code that loads fast, works on mobile, and doesn't break on older phones.
- **SEO foundations** — the technical groundwork and [local SEO](/services/seo) setup that give you a genuine chance of ranking.
- **Aftercare** — someone to fix things, answer questions and keep the site secure once it's live.

## Realistic UK price ranges for a small-business site

Prices vary by region and ambition, but for a typical UK small business these ballparks hold up. A DIY builder like Wix costs a few hundred pounds a year plus a lot of your own time. A freelancer might charge £500 to £2,000. A small studio usually lands between £1,000 and £5,000 for a proper brochure or lead-generating site. Full agency work, or anything with bookings, payments or memberships, climbs from there.

We keep it deliberately simple and fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly surprises. Our [website design](/services/website-design) starts at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for the Complete build, with everything set out clearly in our [transparent pricing](/pricing) before you commit. Care plans start at £27 a month if you'd like us to keep it all running. Redesigns and larger SEO projects we quote per job, because pretending otherwise would just be a made-up number.

> A fixed price isn't a cheaper price. It's an honest one: you know exactly what you're paying before a single pixel moves.

## The costs that show up after launch

The sticker price is only half the story. Hosting, domain renewal, security updates, small content changes and the occasional fix all cost either money or time. Some providers keep those fees low by locking you into their own platform, so leaving means rebuilding from scratch. We don't work that way: you own your site, your domain and your data, and you can take them elsewhere whenever you like.

This is where a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) earns its place. For a fixed monthly amount, updates, backups and small tweaks are handled, so a broken contact form or an out-of-date price doesn't sit there quietly costing you enquiries for weeks. Whether you pay someone or do it yourself, budget for the after, not just the build.

## How to compare quotes without getting stung

The cheapest quote and the most expensive one are rarely the right one. Judge on what's actually included, not the headline figure:

If you've got a quote in front of you and you're not sure it's fair, the fastest way to find out is a second opinion. A free teardown is exactly that: a short, screen-recorded review of your current site, pointing out what's working, what's costing you enquiries, and what a fix would really involve. No pressure, no jargon.

- **Ask what's included** — copywriting, SEO, revisions and training are often extras hidden below the line.
- **Check who owns what** — you should keep your domain, hosting login and files, full stop.
- **Find out who does the work** — the person you meet in the sales chat isn't always the one building your site.
- **Look past launch** — ask what changes cost and how quickly you'll actually get help.

## The quick wins

- Website prices swing because scope and skill vary, not because someone's lying to you.
- You're paying for strategy, design, words, build, SEO and aftercare, not pages.
- Fixed pricing (ours starts at £477) means no hourly surprises and no guesswork.
- Always budget for the running costs, and never accept being locked into a platform you can't leave.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a small-business website cost in the UK?

For a typical UK small business, expect anywhere from a few hundred pounds for a DIY build up to around £5,000 for a studio-built, lead-generating site. Our fixed prices start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, so you know the full cost before you commit. Larger SEO projects and redesigns are quoted per job.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we get your content and feedback. Simpler sites are faster; anything with lots of pages, bookings or custom features takes a little longer. We'll give you a clear timeline before we start.

### Why are some web designers so much cheaper than others?

Usually because they're doing less: a template instead of a custom design, no copywriting, and no SEO setup. That can be fine for a simple brochure site, but check what's actually included before you compare on price alone.

### Do I own my website, or does the designer keep control?

With us, you own your site, your domain and your data, with no lock-in. Some providers tie you to their own platform, which makes moving expensive, so always ask who holds the accounts before you sign anything.

### What is a website care plan and do I need one?

A care plan covers hosting, updates, backups and small changes for a fixed monthly fee, starting at £27 a month. You don't have to have one, but it keeps your site secure and stops small problems turning into lost enquiries.

bodyMarkdown:
A cheap website looks like a bargain right up until you add up what it truly costs you. The £4-a-month builder, the £99 marketplace special, the friend's cousin who 'does a bit of web design' — the upfront number is easy to say yes to. But the price of a website is never just the build fee. It's the enquiries that never land, the evenings you lose fiddling with a template, and the rebuild you end up paying for a year down the line.

So let's be honest about cheap website vs professional website, and the real cost of each in plain UK numbers. No scare tactics and no hard sell — just where the money actually goes, what you get for it, and how to tell a fair price from a false economy before you hand over your card.

## What a cheap website actually costs

The sticker price on a cheap website is rarely the real one. A DIY builder starts at a few pounds a month, but the true bill shows up in your own time and in the sales you quietly miss. Here's where the hidden cost hides:

- **Your hours.** A weekend building it yourself, then an evening every month keeping it patched, is time you could spend running your business.
- **Lost enquiries.** A slow, cluttered or hard-to-use site turns visitors away before they ever call. You never see those lost customers, so the cost feels invisible.
- **Add-ons that creep up.** The £5 plan becomes £20 once you add a contact form, remove the ads, connect a domain and bolt on 'premium' features.
- **The rebuild.** Cheap sites are built to sell, not to last. Most need replacing within a year or two, so you pay for the whole thing twice.
- **Lock-in.** Many budget platforms keep hold of your content. Leave, and you often can't take the site, the design or sometimes even the domain with you.

## Cheap website vs professional website: the real cost side by side

Put the two next to each other over three years, not three months, and the picture changes. A cheap website wins on day one and loses by month twelve. A professional site costs more upfront and then quietly earns its keep.

- **Cheap:** low upfront price, a generic template, your own time to build and maintain, thin or no support, and a likely rebuild within eighteen months.
- **Professional:** a higher fixed price, a site designed around how your customers actually search and buy, built to load fast and convert, with real people to call when something breaks.
- **The tie-breaker:** one extra job a month from a site that works usually covers the difference many times over. For a tradesperson or a dental practice, a single new customer can be worth more than the whole build.

## What actually drives the price of a website

Price varies wildly because 'a website' can mean five pages of clear, well-written content or a fifty-page booking system. When you get a quote, these are the things moving the number up or down:

- **Number of pages and features** — a booking form, online payments or a members' area all add real work.
- **Copywriting and photography** — words and images that sell take time; stock and filler do not, and it shows.
- **Design from scratch vs a template** — a look built around your brand costs more than a theme with your logo dropped on top.
- **[SEO](/services/seo) and local visibility** — being found on Google is a job in its own right, separate from the build.
- **Ongoing care** — hosting, updates, backups and small tweaks, usually covered by a monthly [care plan](/services/care-plans).

## Where a professional website pays for itself

A professional site isn't about looking flashy. It's about doing a job: turning the people who find you into people who contact you. Good [website design](/services/website-design) loads in a couple of seconds, reads clearly on a phone — where most local searches happen — and makes the next step obvious. That's where the return lives.

It also saves you the hidden costs above. You're not the one patching it late at night, you're not losing enquiries to a clunky form, and you're not paying to rebuild the whole thing next spring. Spend a little more once, and you stop bleeding money in ways you can't easily see.

> A cheap website saves you money once. A professional one earns it back every month it's live.

## What a fair fixed price looks like in the UK

For a UK small business, honest ranges look roughly like this. A simple, professional brochure site starts from around £477. A larger site with more pages and features starts from £1,170, and a complete build with everything in it from £2,370. Every one of these is a fixed price, so you know the full cost before we start — no hourly billing and no surprise invoice at the end. Care plans start at £27 a month, and you own your site, domain and data outright. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, and every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make small changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live. The point of a fixed price isn't to be the cheapest quote in your inbox — it's so the number you agree is the number you pay, and the site does its job long after the invoice is forgotten.

## The quick wins

- The real cost of a cheap website is the leads you lose and the rebuild you pay for later, not the price on the tin.
- A professional site is priced higher upfront but designed to convert and built to last.
- In the UK, a fair fixed price for a small-business site starts around £477, with no hourly billing.
- Ask what drives the quote — pages, copywriting, design, SEO and ongoing care all move the number.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a professional website cost in the UK?

For a small business, a professional site typically starts from around £477 for a simple brochure site, £1,170 for a larger site with more pages and features, and £2,370 for a complete build. We work to fixed prices, so you know the full cost up front with no hourly billing. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, and care plans start at £27 a month.

### Is a cheap website ever the right choice?

Sometimes. If you need a single page to hold your name and phone number while you get going, a basic builder is fine for a few months. The trouble starts when a cheap site becomes your main way of winning customers — then the lost enquiries and the eventual rebuild cost far more than doing it properly once.

### How long does it take to build a professional website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. We agree the timeline before we start, so there are no surprises along the way.

### Will I be locked into paying forever?

No. You own your site, domain and data, so you're never held hostage by a platform. A care plan for hosting, updates and backups is optional and starts at £27 a month — and you can leave and take everything with you whenever you like.

### What's the catch with a £477 website?

There isn't one. It's a fixed price for a genuinely professional starter site, not a stripped-back trial. You get a clean, fast, mobile-friendly build, a simple admin panel to make your own edits, and 30 days of aftercare. A bigger budget buys more pages, custom design and features — not a better class of service.

The £4-a-month builder, the £99 marketplace special, the friend's cousin who 'does a bit of web design' — the upfront number is easy to say yes to. But the price of a website is never just the build fee. It's the enquiries that never land, the evenings you lose fiddling with a template, and the rebuild you end up paying for a year down the line.

So let's be honest about cheap website vs professional website, and the real cost of each in plain UK numbers. No scare tactics and no hard sell — just where the money actually goes, what you get for it, and how to tell a fair price from a false economy before you hand over your card.

sections.h2: What a cheap website actually costs
paragraphs: The sticker price on a cheap website is rarely the real one. A DIY builder starts at a few pounds a month, but the true bill shows up in your own time and in the sales you quietly miss. Here's where the hidden cost hides:
bullets: **Your hours.** A weekend building it yourself, then an evening every month keeping it patched, is time you could spend running your business.
**Lost enquiries.** A slow, cluttered or hard-to-use site turns visitors away before they ever call. You never see those lost customers, so the cost feels invisible.
**Add-ons that creep up.** The £5 plan becomes £20 once you add a contact form, remove the ads, connect a domain and bolt on 'premium' features.
**The rebuild.** Cheap sites are built to sell, not to last. Most need replacing within a year or two, so you pay for the whole thing twice.
**Lock-in.** Many budget platforms keep hold of your content. Leave, and you often can't take the site, the design or sometimes even the domain with you.

sections.h2: Cheap website vs professional website: the real cost side by side
paragraphs: Put the two next to each other over three years, not three months, and the picture changes. A cheap website wins on day one and loses by month twelve. A professional site costs more upfront and then quietly earns its keep.
bullets: **Cheap:** low upfront price, a generic template, your own time to build and maintain, thin or no support, and a likely rebuild within eighteen months.
**Professional:** a higher fixed price, a site designed around how your customers actually search and buy, built to load fast and convert, with real people to call when something breaks.

**The tie-breaker:** one extra job a month from a site that works usually covers the difference many times over. For a tradesperson or a dental practice, a single new customer can be worth more than the whole build.

sections.h2: What actually drives the price of a website
paragraphs: Price varies wildly because 'a website' can mean five pages of clear, well-written content or a fifty-page booking system. When you get a quote, these are the things moving the number up or down:
bullets: **Number of pages and features** — a booking form, online payments or a members' area all add real work.
**Copywriting and photography** — words and images that sell take time; stock and filler do not, and it shows.
**Design from scratch vs a template** — a look built around your brand costs more than a theme with your logo dropped on top.
**[SEO](/services/seo) and local visibility** — being found on Google is a job in its own right, separate from the build.
**Ongoing care** — hosting, updates, backups and small tweaks, usually covered by a monthly [care plan](/services/care-plans).

sections.h2: Where a professional website pays for itself
paragraphs: A professional site isn't about looking flashy. It's about doing a job: turning the people who find you into people who contact you. Good [website design](/services/website-design) loads in a couple of seconds, reads clearly on a phone — where most local searches happen — and makes the next step obvious. That's where the return lives.

It also saves you the hidden costs above. You're not the one patching it late at night, you're not losing enquiries to a clunky form, and you're not paying to rebuild the whole thing next spring. Spend a little more once, and you stop bleeding money in ways you can't easily see.
sections.pullquote: A cheap website saves you money once. A professional one earns it back every month it's live.

sections.h2: What a fair fixed price looks like in the UK
paragraphs: For a UK small business, honest ranges look roughly like this. A simple, professional brochure site starts from around £477. A larger site with more pages and features starts from £1,170, and a complete build with everything in it from £2,370. Every one of these is a fixed price, so you know the full cost before we start — no hourly billing and no surprise invoice at the end. Care plans start at £27 a month, and you own your site, domain and data outright. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, and every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make small changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live. The point of a fixed price isn't to be the cheapest quote in your inbox — it's so the number you agree is the number you pay, and the site does its job long after the invoice is forgotten.

keyTakeaways: The real cost of a cheap website is the leads you lose and the rebuild you pay for later, not the price on the tin.
A professional site is priced higher upfront but designed to convert and built to last.
In the UK, a fair fixed price for a small-business site starts around £477, with no hourly billing.
Ask what drives the quote — pages, copywriting, design, SEO and ongoing care all move the number.

bodyMarkdown:
If you sell online, or you're about to, one question tends to come before all the others: how much does an e-commerce website cost to build? The honest answer is that it depends, but not in the vague, hand-waving way agencies often mean it. The price is driven by a handful of specific choices, and once you understand them you can tell a fair quote from an inflated one.

This guide gives you realistic UK ranges, explains what actually moves the number, and shows where a small business can spend sensibly. No jargon, no scare tactics, just figures and the reasoning behind them.

## What you're actually paying for

An online shop is far more than a few product pages. When you pay for one, you're paying for design, the store setup, secure payments, and the quiet plumbing that keeps orders flowing without you noticing. Skimp on the wrong part and it shows at the worst possible moment: the checkout.

Most [e-commerce website design](/services/website-design) breaks down into a few core pieces, and it helps to see them laid out before you compare any quotes:

- Design and branding, so your shop looks trustworthy enough for someone to hand over their card details
- Store build, meaning product pages, categories, search and a basket that works properly on a phone
- Payments and tax, with card processing, VAT, delivery rules and refunds all set up correctly
- Content, which is your product photos, descriptions and the copy that does the actual selling
- Launch and testing, so orders, emails and stock levels all behave before you go live

## How much does an e-commerce website cost to build in the UK?

As a rough guide, here is what the main routes tend to cost in 2026. Treat these as market ranges rather than quotes, because every shop is a little different and the figure moves with what you sell.

Where you land depends less on the price tag itself and more on what you're selling, and to how many people. A small shop with twenty products has very different needs to one juggling thousands of lines and daily stock updates.

- Doing it yourself on Shopify or Wix: around £20 to £60 a month for the platform, plus a good deal of your own time
- A freelancer using a template: roughly £800 to £2,500 for a straightforward shop
- A small studio building something tailored: roughly £2,500 to £10,000, depending on features
- A large agency or complex custom build: £10,000 and up

> A shop that pays for itself is a bargain at any of these figures. A cheap one that spooks people at checkout costs you every single day it's live.

## What pushes the price up (and what doesn't)

The biggest cost drivers are rarely the ones people worry about. A slightly fancier homepage is cheap. The things that genuinely add hours are less visible:

What doesn't need to cost the earth: solid [SEO](/services/seo) foundations, fast mobile pages and clean product listings. These are standard on any decent build, and they happen to be the parts that actually bring in traffic and turn browsers into buyers.

- The number of products, and whether they come in sizes, colours or other variations
- Custom features such as subscriptions, bookings, trade accounts or bespoke delivery pricing
- Integrations with stock systems, accounting software or a physical till
- One-off design work rather than a proven, tested layout

## Where small businesses overspend

Plenty of owners pay for things they will never use. The classic mistakes are buying a huge platform built for hundreds of thousands of orders, commissioning custom design for pages a template would handle beautifully, and signing open-ended contracts where the bill quietly climbs by the hour.

The other silent cost is what happens after launch. A shop needs updating, backups and the occasional fix. A simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) at a fixed monthly rate is almost always cheaper than emergency call-outs when something breaks during a busy sale, and it saves you the stress of scrambling for help when you can least afford the downtime.

## A fixed-price way to budget

We think you should know the full cost before you commit, which is why our prices are fixed rather than billed by the hour. A simple site starts at £477, our Growth build is £1,170, and our Complete build is £2,370, with care plans from £27 a month if you'd like us to look after it for you.

For most small online shops, that means a genuine, tested store without the open-ended agency bill. You can see all the [fixed-price plans](/pricing) in one place, and every build includes a simple admin panel, 30 days of aftercare, and full ownership of your site, domain and data. Most go live in three to six weeks, and you deal with a small UK team directly the whole way through.

## The quick wins

- E-commerce sites range from about £800 with a freelancer to £10,000 or more with a large agency.
- Product count, custom features and integrations drive cost far more than how the site looks.
- Fixed-price beats hourly billing when you need to budget with real confidence.
- Our builds run from £477 to £2,370, with care plans from £27 a month and most shops live in three to six weeks.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does an e-commerce website cost to build in the UK?

For a small business, a tested online shop typically costs somewhere between £800 with a freelancer and £10,000 or more with a large agency. Our fixed-price builds run from £477 for a simple site to £1,170 (Growth) and £2,370 (Complete), so you know the figure before you start rather than watching an hourly bill climb.

### How long does it take to build an online shop?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks. The timeline depends mainly on how quickly you can get us your product details and photos. The build itself moves fast once we have what we need.

### Do I have to pay a monthly fee to run an online shop?

You'll pay for hosting and your payment provider's transaction fees, which is normal for any shop. Beyond that, a monthly plan is optional. Our care plans start at £27 a month for updates, backups and support, but you own your site and can look after it yourself if you prefer.

### Is Shopify cheaper than a custom-built site?

Shopify is cheaper to start, at around £20 to £60 a month, but you trade some control and pay ongoing fees and app costs that add up over time. A tailored build costs more upfront and gives you a site you fully own, with no per-app surprises. The right choice depends on how you plan to grow.

### Can I start small and add features later?

Yes, and it's usually the smart move. Launch with a clean, fast shop that covers what you sell today, then add features like subscriptions or trade accounts once sales justify them. That way you avoid paying for things you may never use.

bodyMarkdown:
Your website is one of the few things in your business that works around the clock. It answers questions at 11pm, takes enquiries on a Sunday and helps people in your town choose you over the shop down the road. But a website is not a leaflet you print once and forget about — it needs looking after. So when owners ask about website maintenance costs and what UK businesses should budget, they deserve a straight answer rather than a vague 'it depends'.

It does depend on a handful of things, and we will walk through each one. By the end you will know the realistic monthly ranges, exactly what you are paying for, and how to avoid both neglect and overpaying.

## What website maintenance actually covers

Maintenance is the quiet work that keeps a site fast, safe and accurate. Most of it happens in the background, which is exactly why it is easy to skip — right up until a plugin update breaks your booking form or Google flags your site as insecure. A good care plan usually rolls all of it into one predictable job:

None of this is glamorous. All of it protects the money your site quietly brings in every month.

- Software, plugin and security updates so nothing goes out of date
- Reliable hosting, an SSL certificate and regular backups
- Fixing broken links, forms, images and anything that stops working
- Small content changes — prices, opening hours, new staff, seasonal offers
- Uptime and speed monitoring so problems are caught before your customers notice
- The odd new page or feature as your business grows

## Typical website maintenance costs in the UK

There is no single figure, but the market is fairly predictable. Here are the bands most UK small businesses will recognise:

For a typical local business — a dentist, a builder, a café — a plan in one of the first two bands is usually plenty. Our own [care plans](/services/care-plans) start at £27 a month, which covers the essentials without paying for hours you will never use.

Be wary of very cheap plans that turn out to be hosting in disguise. If a £10-a-month deal does not include updates, backups and a real person who will fix things, you are paying for storage, not maintenance.

- Small brochure site, light touch: £20–£50 a month
- Busy local business with regular updates: £50–£150 a month
- E-commerce or larger sites: £150–£500+ a month
- Pay-as-you-go developer time: roughly £50–£90 an hour
- Domain, hosting and SSL bought separately: £50–£200 a year

> The cheapest maintenance is the kind that stops a small problem becoming an expensive one.

## What pushes the cost up or down

Two businesses can pay very different amounts for perfectly good reasons. A few things move the needle more than the rest.

The platform matters most. A simple, well-built site is cheap to keep healthy; a WordPress site stacked with plugins needs more attention, and an online shop taking payments needs the most. How often you change things matters too — if you post offers every week you will use more time than a site that barely changes from one year to the next. Traffic and security raise the bar as well, because a busier site is a bigger target and needs tighter monitoring. Finally, whether ongoing [SEO](/services/seo) is bundled in changes the number, since optimising a site month after month is real work, not a switch you flick once and forget.

## DIY, a freelancer, or a care plan?

You have three broad options, and the right one depends on your time and your nerves. Doing it yourself is free until it isn't — a lost afternoon wrestling with a broken update is rarely worth the saving. A freelancer on an ad-hoc basis can be excellent value, as long as they actually answer when something breaks at the worst possible moment. A fixed monthly care plan costs a little more than nothing, but updates, backups and small jobs are simply handled, and you always know exactly who to call. For most owners we speak to, that predictability is worth far more than shaving off a few pounds.

## How to budget without overpaying

Start with what your site does for you. If it brings in even one or two customers a month, a plan in the £27–£150 range is a rounding error against that value — and skipping it is a false economy. Set aside a fixed monthly figure so maintenance never has to compete with your other costs, and keep a small annual buffer, say £100–£300, for the occasional new page or seasonal refresh.

Above all, avoid open-ended hourly arrangements you cannot predict. Fixed pricing is the whole point: you should know the number before the month begins. That is how we price everything — you can see our website and care plan figures on the [pricing](/pricing) page, with no hourly billing and no lock-in, because you own your site, domain and data. If you would like a second opinion on what your current site is really costing you, book a free teardown and we will record a short, honest review — no obligation. Tell us about your site on the [contact](/contact) page and we will take it from there.

## The quick wins

- Most UK small businesses budget £20–£150 a month for website maintenance.
- Maintenance covers updates, security, backups, fixes and small content changes — not just hosting.
- Cost is driven by your platform, how often you change content, your traffic, and whether SEO is included.
- Fixed monthly pricing beats open-ended hourly billing — you know the number up front.
- Our care plans start at £27 a month, with websites from £477 and no lock-in.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does website maintenance cost in the UK?

For most small businesses, expect roughly £20–£150 a month depending on how busy and complex your site is. E-commerce and larger sites run higher. Our care plans start at £27 a month, and full websites start at £477, so you can see the whole picture before you commit to anything.

### How long does it take to get a site live and onto a care plan?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, and every build includes 30 days of aftercare before a care plan begins. Moving an existing site onto a plan is usually quicker — often a week or so once we have access to it.

### Do I really need a care plan, or can I skip it?

You can skip it, but an unmaintained site quietly drifts — updates lapse, security weakens and small faults pile up until one of them costs you enquiries. A plan from £27 a month keeps it fast, safe and current. You own your site, domain and data either way, so there is never any lock-in.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website — what is working, what is quietly costing you enquiries, and what to fix first. It is free, there is no obligation, and you keep the recording whether or not you work with us.

Fixed monthly pricing beats open-ended hourly billing — you know the number up front.
Our care plans start at £27 a month, with websites from £477 and no lock-in.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a dental practice and you're collecting quotes, you've probably noticed the prices are all over the place. One agency says £500, the next says £8,000, and neither really explains why. So how much should a dental practice pay for a website? The honest answer is that it depends on what you need the site to do — not on how impressive the invoice looks.

This is a plain-English guide to real UK price ranges, what actually drives the cost, and how to avoid paying for things you don't need. No sales spin — just the numbers we'd give a friend.

## The short answer: what UK practices actually pay

For a single-location dental practice in the UK, most sensible options fall into three broad bands. A DIY builder or an off-the-shelf template costs you £0 to around £500, but you do the work and it usually shows. A freelancer or small studio typically charges £1,000 to £3,000 for a proper, custom-built site. A larger agency doing fully bespoke design, with booking systems and ongoing marketing baked in, can run from £5,000 to £15,000 or more.

Most independent practices don't need the top band. A well-built site in the £1,000 to £3,000 range does everything a busy local practice needs: it looks professional, loads fast on a phone, and turns visitors into booked appointments.

## What pushes the price up

The gap between a £500 site and a £5,000 one usually comes down to a handful of things. Some are worth paying for, some aren't — it depends on your practice.

- Custom design versus a template — a layout built around your practice costs more than a stock theme, but it won't look like every other dentist in town.
- The number of pages — a five-page site is quicker to build than a 20-page one with a page for every treatment.
- Copywriting — words that persuade a nervous patient to book take time to write well.
- Photography — professional photos of your practice and team beat stock images, but they add to the bill.
- Online booking and integrations — connecting to your practice management or booking software adds development time.
- SEO and accessibility — proper local SEO and GDPR-friendly handling of patient enquiries are worth building in from the start.

## How much should a dental practice pay for a website?

Here's our straight answer. For most UK dental practices, a fair price for a website that looks the part and brings in patients sits between **£1,000 and £2,500**. Below that, you're usually getting a template with your logo dropped in. Above it, you're paying for extras a single-site practice rarely needs on day one.

We work to fixed prices, so you know the cost before we start — no hourly billing and no surprise invoices. Our [website design](/services/website-design) packages start at £477 for a simple Starter site, £1,170 for Growth, and £2,370 for a Complete build with more pages and copywriting. You'll find the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page. Whichever you choose, you own the site, the domain and your data — there's no lock-in.

## Why the cheapest option often costs more

A £300 site feels like a bargain until you count the hidden costs. Cheap builds are often slow, hard to update, and tied to a platform you can't leave without starting again. Plenty of practices end up paying twice: once for the cheap site, then again a year later for the rebuild.

Whatever you spend, make sure you own what you're paying for. If an agency keeps your domain, your content or your data, you don't really own your website — you're renting it. Dentistry is a competitive [industry](/industries), and a site you can't control is a site you can't grow.

> The cheapest website is rarely the one that costs the least.

## What you should get for your money

Whatever your budget, a good dental website should cover the basics without you having to ask for them.

- A mobile-first design, because most people look for a dentist on their phone.
- Clear calls to book, so a nervous first-time patient never has to hunt for your number.
- Fast loading — a slow site loses patients before the page even appears.
- A simple admin panel so you can update opening hours or prices yourself.
- Aftercare — every one of our builds includes 30 days of support once you're live.

## Don't forget the running costs

The build is a one-off, but a website needs looking after. Budget a little each month for hosting, updates, security and small tweaks. Our [care plans](/services/care-plans) start at £27 a month and cover the maintenance most practices would rather not think about.

If you want to rank for searches like 'dentist near me', that's a separate, ongoing job. Good [local SEO](/services/seo) is quoted per project because every practice starts from a different place — but it's usually the best-value marketing a local practice can invest in. No honest agency will promise you the top spot on Google, so be wary of any that do.

## The quick wins

- Most UK dental practices should expect to pay £1,000–£3,000 for a well-built, custom website.
- Anything under about £500 is usually a template, not a site built around your practice.
- Fixed-price quotes protect you from surprise invoices and hourly billing.
- Budget for running costs too: care plans from £27/month, with SEO quoted per project.
- Always check you own your site, domain and data outright — no lock-in.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much should a dental practice pay for a website?

For most UK practices, a fair range is £1,000 to £3,000 for a custom, professionally built site. Our fixed-price packages start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for a Complete build, so you know the full cost before we begin.

### How long does it take to build a dental website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we get your content and photos. Simpler Starter sites usually land at the faster end of that range.

### Are there ongoing costs after the site is built?

Yes — you'll need hosting and maintenance. Our care plans start at £27 a month and cover updates, security and small changes. SEO, if you want it, is quoted separately per project.

### Will a cheap website hurt my Google rankings?

It can. Slow, poorly built sites tend to rank worse and convert fewer visitors into patients. A fast, tidy, well-structured site gives your local SEO a much stronger foundation, though no honest agency can guarantee a specific position on Google.

### Do I own my website?

With us, yes — you own your site, your domain and your data outright, with no lock-in. Before you sign with any agency, check that you'll keep control of all three.

title: What Does SEO Cost for a Small Business in the UK?
description: Honest UK SEO pricing for small businesses: real cost ranges, what drives the price, and how to spend wisely. Book a free teardown to get started.
