bodyMarkdown:
Ask ten agencies what does SEO cost for a small business in the UK and you will get ten different numbers, and a lot of shrugging. It genuinely depends on your trade, your town and the state of your website, but that is no excuse for keeping you in the dark. So here are real, honest ranges, what pushes the price up or down, and how to make sure every pound pulls its weight.

SEO is simply the work of helping people find you on Google when they search for what you sell: a new patient looking for a dentist, someone hunting an emergency plumber at 9pm, a couple comparing wedding venues. Done well, it brings in enquiries for years. Done cheaply, it can quietly drain your budget. Knowing the going rate is how you tell the two apart.

## What does SEO cost for a UK small business?

Most UK small businesses pay for SEO in one of two ways: a monthly retainer for ongoing work, or a one-off project to fix and improve things. As a rough guide to what you will typically see:

For most local shops, trades and practices, sensible spending sits somewhere between £300 and £1,200 a month, or a well-scoped one-off you can budget for in advance. Our own [SEO work](/services/seo) is quoted per project once we have actually looked at your site, never a number plucked from thin air.

- Freelancers and solo consultants: often £250–£600 a month, or a few hundred pounds for a one-off local setup.
- Small, UK-based agencies: usually £500–£1,500 a month for ongoing local SEO.
- One-off projects, like a technical fix or a batch of location pages: commonly £500–£2,500 depending on scope.
- Competitive national campaigns: £1,500 or more a month, which is usually overkill for a local business.
- Doing it yourself: mostly your time, plus £0–£100 a month for tools.

## What actually drives the price

The range above is wide for a reason. A village hairdresser and a solicitor in central Manchester are playing very different games. These are the things that move the number:

- **Competition:** ranking for 'accountant in London' takes far more work than 'accountant in Frome'.
- **Your starting point:** a fast, well-built website needs less fixing than a slow, dated one.
- **Scope:** are you targeting one town or fifteen, one service or a whole menu of them?
- **Content:** Google favours genuinely useful pages, and someone has to research and write them.
- **Ongoing versus one-off:** a single tidy-up is cheaper, but local rankings need feeding to hold their place.

## Freelancer, agency or doing it yourself?

A good freelancer is affordable and personal, but they are one person, so holidays, illness and busy spells all slow things down. A large agency has more hands, yet you can end up a small account passed to a junior. A small studio like ours aims to sit in the middle: enough people to keep things moving, few enough that you deal with us directly and we pick up the phone. Whichever route you pick, choose someone who understands your trade, because the way people search for a [dentist, builder or restaurant](/industries) is never quite the same.

You can also do the basics yourself: claim your Google Business Profile, gather honest reviews, and write clear pages about each service and area you cover. Plenty of local businesses win their first enquiries this way. Paid help earns its keep when the DIY route stalls, or when your time is simply worth more spent running the business.

## Your website is half the battle

Here is the part cheap SEO sellers rarely mention: you cannot rank a broken website. If your site is slow, awkward on a phone or thin on content, no amount of monthly SEO will save it, and most local searches happen on mobile. That is why we treat the website as the foundation rather than an afterthought.

A solid, fast [website design and build](/services/website-design) with the right pages baked in does a lot of your SEO for free. Our fixed-price builds start at £477, so you know the cost up front, and you can see the full range on our [pricing page](/pricing). Spending £600 a month optimising a site that fights you at every step is money poured into a leaky bucket.

> You cannot rank a broken website. Fix the foundation before you pay anyone to promote it.

## Why the cheapest SEO is often the dearest

When someone offers guaranteed number-one rankings for £99 a month, be careful. Nobody can guarantee rankings because Google decides those, and rock-bottom prices usually mean corners cut: spammy links that risk a penalty, thin copied content, or reports full of numbers that mean nothing. Undoing that damage costs far more than doing the job properly the first time.

- Guaranteed rankings or promises of overnight results.
- Long contracts with no clear list of what is actually being done.
- No access to your own Google Business Profile, analytics or website login.
- Reports heavy on jargon but silent on real enquiries and calls.

## How to start without overspending

If you are just beginning, you do not need a grand a month. Start small: make your website fast and clear, claim and complete your Google Business Profile, ask happy customers for reviews, and write one honest page for each main service and area. Measure enquiries and phone calls, not vanity numbers. When you are ready for more, add ongoing help; our [care plans](/services/care-plans) start at £27 a month and keep your site healthy while you grow.

The quickest way to know what your business actually needs is to have someone look at your current site. That is exactly what a free teardown is: a short, screen-recorded review of what is working, what is costing you enquiries, and what we would fix first. No pressure, and no jargon.

## The quick wins

- There is no single price: most UK local businesses sensibly spend £300–£1,200 a month, or a well-scoped one-off.
- Competition, your website's condition and the scope of work are what move the cost most.
- A fast, well-built website does a large part of your SEO for free.
- Steer clear of guaranteed-ranking offers and anyone who will not list the work they do.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does SEO cost for a small business in the UK?

There is no single price. Most UK local businesses sensibly spend between £300 and £1,200 a month for ongoing SEO, or a few hundred to a couple of thousand pounds for a well-scoped one-off project. We quote SEO per project after reviewing your site, so the figure fits your goals rather than a generic package. For context, our fixed-price websites start at £477 and care plans from £27 a month.

### How long does SEO take to work?

Honestly, months rather than days. For most local businesses you will start to see meaningful movement in three to six months, sometimes sooner for less competitive searches. Anyone promising overnight results is not being straight with you. A new website itself is quicker: most of our builds go live in three to six weeks.

### Is cheap SEO worth it?

Sometimes, and sometimes it is the most expensive mistake you can make. Cheap, honest work like gathering reviews and tidying your Google Business Profile is well worth it. Cheap, spammy work, such as bought links and copied content, can get your site penalised and cost far more to undo. Always ask exactly what you are paying for.

### Do I have to pay for SEO every month?

No. Ongoing retainers suit competitive markets, but many small businesses do well with a solid website, a strong Google Business Profile and a one-off improvement project, topped up occasionally. You own your site, domain and data with us, so you are never locked in.

### Can I do SEO myself?

Yes, the basics are very doable: claim your Google Business Profile, gather honest reviews, keep your site fast, and write clear pages for each service and area you cover. Bring in paid help when progress stalls or your time is better spent running the business.

faq.a: Yes, and that's the setup most small businesses prefer. Buy the site outright, then add an optional low-cost care plan for hosting, updates and backups. Ours start at £27 a month and you can cancel any time, so support stays a choice rather than a lease.

bodyMarkdown:
Deciding between one-off vs monthly website payment plans is one of the first choices you'll face when you commission a new site, and it isn't as simple as picking the smaller number. Both models can be sensible. Both can also quietly cost you far more than you expected, depending on what's bundled in and, crucially, who owns the site at the end.

This guide breaks down what each model really includes, the true cost over three to five years, and the questions to ask before you sign anything. No jargon, no sales spin, just what we'd tell a friend running a shop, a salon or a [trades business](/industries) here in the UK.

## One-off vs monthly website payment plans, explained

Strip away the marketing and there are two ways to pay for a website. With a one-off payment, you agree a price, the site gets built, and it belongs to you. With a monthly payment plan, you pay little or nothing upfront and a set fee every month instead, sometimes for a fixed term, sometimes for as long as the site stays live.

Neither is a scam and neither is automatically better. What matters is what sits inside the number: the design and build, hosting, a domain, updates, support, and the part people forget to check, whether you own the finished site or are effectively renting it.

## The real cost over three to five years

A one-off build for a UK small business typically lands somewhere between a few hundred and a few thousand pounds, depending on how many pages you need and whether you want features like online booking or a shop. For context, our own [fixed prices](/pricing) start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. You know the figure before we begin, and there's no hourly meter running.

Monthly website plans usually advertise a friendlier headline: perhaps £50 to £150 a month with nothing to pay upfront. That feels easier on day one. But run the maths over the real lifetime of a website, which is often four or five years:

That doesn't make monthly wrong. Spreading cost has genuine value when money is tight. It just means the cheap-looking option is often the expensive one once you add up the years.

- £99 a month for three years is £3,564, and you keep paying after that.
- £99 a month for five years is £5,940, usually with the same site you started with.
- A £1,170 one-off build works out at roughly £20 a month across five years, then nothing.

> A £99-a-month website can quietly cost you nearly £6,000 over five years, and you may still not own it at the end.

## Who actually owns the website?

This is the question that catches people out. With many monthly plans, you're leasing the site. Stop paying and it can be switched off, taking your pages, your content and sometimes your domain and email with it. You've spent thousands and have nothing to move elsewhere.

We take the opposite view: **you should own your website, your domain and your data**, full stop. No lock-in, no hostage situation if you ever decide to work with someone else. If a provider won't confirm in writing that the site is yours to keep, treat that as your answer. It's also worth checking how the [web design](/services/website-design) is scoped, because a cheap monthly template is a very different thing from a site built around your business.

## So which is better for your business?

There's no single right answer. It depends on your cash flow and how much you value ownership. As a rough guide, a one-off payment usually wins if you:

And a monthly plan can suit you if you:

- want to own your site, domain and data outright, with no ongoing contract
- would rather pay once than carry a bill you can't easily cancel
- plan to keep the site for several years
- genuinely can't free up the upfront cost right now
- want hosting, updates and support rolled into one predictable fee, and have checked exactly what happens when you stop paying

## The middle path most small businesses want

In practice, the setup that suits most local businesses is simple: buy the site outright at a fixed price, then choose whether to add a low-cost [care plan](/services/care-plans) for hosting, updates, backups and small changes. Ours start at £27 a month and you can cancel any time, because it's a service you choose to keep, not a lease you're trapped in.

You get the best of both worlds. You own the asset, your monthly outgoing is small and optional, and you're not tied to one agency just to keep your own website online. If [strong local SEO](/services/seo) matters to you, that ownership also means you keep every ranking and every piece of content you've built up.

If you're not sure which model you're on right now, or you suspect you're renting a site you thought you owned, the quickest way to find out is to have someone look. A free teardown is a short screen-recorded review of your current site, with plain-English pointers on what's working, what isn't and what it's really costing you.

## The quick wins

- One-off means you own the site; many monthly plans mean you're only renting it.
- Cheap-looking monthly fees often cost more than a one-off build once you add up four or five years.
- Always get it confirmed in writing that you own your site, domain and data.
- A fixed-price build plus an optional care plan gives you ownership without a large monthly commitment.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a small business website cost in the UK?

For a local business, expect anywhere from a few hundred to a few thousand pounds as a one-off, depending on pages and features. Our fixed prices start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with no hourly billing. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we get your content and how many pages you need. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you're not left on your own once it's live.

### Are monthly website payment plans a rip-off?

Not necessarily. Spreading the cost is genuinely helpful when cash flow is tight, and having support bundled in can be convenient. The risk is all in the small print: check whether you own the site, what happens if you stop paying, and how the total compares to a one-off build over four or five years.

### Do I own my website if I pay a one-off price?

You should. With a proper one-off build, the site, domain and data are yours to keep with no lock-in, so you can move to another provider whenever you like. Always get that confirmed in writing, whichever payment model you choose.

### Can I pay once and still get ongoing support?

Yes, and that's the setup most small businesses prefer. Buy the site outright, then add an optional low-cost care plan for hosting, updates and backups. Ours start at £27 a month and you can cancel any time, so support stays a choice rather than a lease.

title: The Hidden Costs of a Cheap Website (and How to Avoid Them)
description: A cheap website often costs more over time. Here's what really drives web design prices in the UK, and how to avoid the hidden costs. Book a free teardown.
metaTitle: Hidden Costs of a Cheap Website & How to Avoid Them
excerpt: A budget website can quietly drain your time, leads and money. Here's what really drives the cost, and how to spend wisely.
targetKeyword: the hidden costs of a cheap website and how to avoid them

bodyMarkdown:
Everyone loves a bargain, and a £99 website or a free drag-and-drop builder can feel like a sensible way to start. But understanding the hidden costs of a cheap website, and how to avoid them, is a conversation worth having before you commit, because the price on the tin is rarely the price you end up paying. A cheap site often sends you the bill later, in lost enquiries, wasted evenings and a full rebuild you never budgeted for.

This isn't a pitch for spending as much as possible. It's an honest look at where the real costs hide, what genuinely drives the price of a small-business website in the UK, and how to spend your money once instead of twice.

## What a cheap website usually looks like

Cheap comes in a few familiar shapes. There's the DIY builder you set up yourself over a weekend. There's the £99 or £149 offer that lands in your inbox. There's the freelancer on a global marketplace charging £80 for a five-page site, and there's the friend of a friend who 'does websites' on the side.

None of these are automatically bad. Plenty of businesses start on a builder and do fine. The trouble begins when a low upfront price hides work that still needs doing, and someone, usually you, ends up doing it or paying for it further down the line.

## The hidden costs of a cheap website, and how to avoid them

The sticker price only covers what you can see. Here are the costs that tend to surface later, and the simple way to sidestep each one.

- **Lost enquiries.** If your site loads slowly, looks dated or is fiddly on a phone, people leave before they ever call you. Most local searches happen on mobile, so a site that struggles on a small screen quietly costs you customers every week.
- **Rebuild fees.** A site built without a plan often has to be scrapped and started again within a year or two. You pay twice, once for the cheap version and again for the one that actually works.
- **Platform lock-in.** Some cheap builders and 'free' packages keep your domain, content and data on their terms. If you decide to leave, you can't easily take your site with you. Always check that you own your site, domain and content outright.
- **Your own time.** DIY looks free until you count the evenings spent wrestling with templates instead of running your business. Time is the cost nobody puts on the invoice.
- **Search invisibility.** A bargain site is often built with no [SEO](/services/seo) foundation at all: no proper structure, no local signals, nothing to help Google understand who you are. You end up invisible to the exact people searching for you.
- **No one to call.** When something breaks the week before a big push, a cheap package rarely comes with a person who picks up the phone.

## What actually drives the price of a website

If cheap has hidden costs, it helps to know what you're really paying for when a price is fair. A website's cost comes down to a handful of things: how many pages and features it needs, whether the design is bespoke or a stock template, who writes the words, and whether the search and technical groundwork is done properly.

As a rough guide, a simple brochure site from a UK freelancer might be a few hundred pounds, while a larger site with custom features, professional copy and proper SEO can run into several thousand. Neither is 'right', it depends on what your business genuinely needs.

We keep this honest by working to fixed prices, so you know the number before you say yes. Our websites start at £477 for a Starter build, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month if you want us to keep looking after it. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page. The point isn't the exact figure, it's that a clear, fixed price protects you from the surprises a cheap quote tends to hide.

## How to avoid paying twice

Avoiding the hidden costs isn't about spending more. It's about asking a few sharp questions before you hand over any money.

Ask who owns the site, domain and content when the work is done, and the answer should always be you. Ask whether the price is fixed or likely to creep. Ask what happens after launch: is there support, or are you on your own the moment it goes live? A build that includes aftercare and a simple way to keep the site healthy, like a straightforward [care plan](/services/care-plans), saves you far more than it costs.

Get those answers in writing and most of the hidden costs simply disappear. You'll pay a fair price once, own what you've paid for, and have someone to call when you need them.

> Cheap asks 'what's the lowest price today?' Good value asks 'what will this actually cost me over the next three years?'

## Cheap versus value: the honest bottom line

A cheap website and a good-value website can carry the same price on day one and end up worlds apart by year two. One quietly leaks leads, time and trust until you rebuild it. The other earns its keep and grows with you.

If you're not sure which one you've got, it's worth a proper look. You can see examples of sites we've built over on our [work](/work) page, or if you'd rather have your current site checked, [get in touch](/contact). Sometimes a fresh pair of eyes is all it takes to spot what's costing you.

## The quick wins

- A cheap website's low price often hides bigger costs later: lost enquiries, rebuilds and wasted time.
- Always confirm you own your site, domain and content, because lock-in is one of the most expensive hidden costs.
- A clear, fixed price and included aftercare protect you from surprises far better than the lowest quote.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is a cheap website always a bad idea?

No. If you're testing an idea or just need a simple online presence, a builder or budget site can be fine to start with. The risk is outgrowing it quickly and paying to rebuild. The trick is being honest about what you'll need in a year, not just this month.

### How much does a small-business website actually cost?

It depends on the size and features, but you shouldn't have to guess. We work to fixed prices: websites from £477 for a Starter build, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the number before you commit.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you're not left on your own once it's live.

### What's the most expensive hidden cost of a cheap website?

Usually the rebuild. A site built without a proper plan often has to be scrapped and started from scratch within a year or two, which means paying twice. Being locked into a platform you can't easily leave is a close second.

### How do I know if my current website is costing me money?

The clearest signs are a slow load, a clunky experience on mobile, and few enquiries despite decent traffic. If you're not sure, a free teardown gives you a short, honest review of what's working and what's quietly holding you back.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a small business in the UK, you have probably asked the same quiet question at least once: do I really need a website for my business in 2026, or is a busy Facebook page and a good reputation enough? It is a fair thing to wonder. Websites cost money, they take a little setting up, and plenty of businesses seem to tick along without one.

The honest answer is not a blanket yes. It depends on how your customers look for you, what you sell, and how much you are willing to leave to chance. This guide walks through it plainly, with no jargon and no scare tactics, so you can decide with a clear head.

## Do you really need a website for your business in 2026?

Here is the short version. If people search for what you do before they buy, you need somewhere they can find you that you actually control. For most local businesses — a plumber in Leeds, a hairdresser in Bristol, a café tucked off a Sheffield high street — that search happens on a phone, on Google, and often at the exact moment someone is ready to spend. If you are not there, the job quietly goes to whoever is.

A social media page helps, but you are renting that space. The platform owns the audience, sets the rules, and can change both overnight. A website is the one place online that is genuinely yours: your name, your work, your phone number, and no algorithm deciding who gets to see it.

> A website is the one part of your online presence you actually own — everything else, you are only renting.

## What a website actually does for a small business

A website is not decoration. For a small business it does real, unglamorous work, day and night.

The good news is you do not need much. A focused [website design](/services/website-design) with five or six strong pages usually does the job far better than a sprawling site nobody finishes reading.

- **Shows up when people search.** A well-built site helps you appear in local results and on Google Maps, which is where a lot of local searches end up.
- **Answers the obvious questions.** Your hours, prices, the areas you cover and the jobs you take on — all there before anyone has to ring.
- **Builds trust before you speak.** Real photos, clear pricing and a tidy layout make you the safe choice over a rival with nothing to show.
- **Works while you sleep.** A contact form or booking link keeps catching enquiries at 11pm on a Sunday, when the phone is off.
- **Gives you one place to send people.** Every van, leaflet, business card and Instagram bio can point to a single page that does the selling for you.

## When you might be fine without one (for now)

To be straight with you, not everyone needs a website tomorrow. If you are fully booked by word of mouth, sell only through a marketplace like Etsy or a food-delivery app, or you are testing an idea before spending a penny, you can wait. There is no shame in that.

But treat it as a pause, not a plan. The moment you want more control over who finds you — or you get tired of losing enquiries to competitors who simply look more established — a website stops being optional. Strong [local SEO](/services/seo), for instance, only works when there is a site for it to point at.

## How to get a website that earns its keep

If you have decided it is time, here is a sensible order to work through, whether you build it yourself or hand it over.

- **Pick the one job.** Is it phone calls, bookings, or online sales? A site with a single clear goal beats a pretty one that does nothing.
- **Sort the basics.** A domain name, a proper business email, and a handful of honest photos of your real work will carry you a long way.
- **Write for the customer, not yourself.** Say what you do, where, for whom, and what it costs, in the plain words they would actually use.
- **Keep it small and finishable.** Home, services, about, prices, contact. You can always add more once it is live and earning.
- **Plan to keep it fresh.** A site that never changes slowly slips down the rankings; a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps it fast, secure and current without you thinking about it.

## Doing it yourself vs getting help

You have two honest routes. Website builders like Squarespace or Wix are genuinely fine for a simple site, provided you have the time and patience to learn them — expect a weekend or two, and be ready to do your own words, photos and SEO. Nothing wrong with that at all.

The other route is to have someone do it properly so you can get back to running the business. That is our whole reason for existing. We build fixed-price sites [from £477](/pricing), so you know the cost up front, you own everything at the end, and most go live in three to six weeks. You deal with a small UK team directly, and we pick up the phone.

Not sure your current site is pulling its weight? The quickest way to find out is a free teardown: a short, screen-recorded review that points out what is helping and what is quietly costing you enquiries.

## The quick wins

- You need a website when customers search before they buy — which, for most local businesses, is nearly always.
- A social page is rented space; a website is the one part of your online presence you own outright.
- You can wait if you are fully booked by word of mouth or sell only through a marketplace — but treat it as a pause, not a plan.
- Keep it small: five or six clear pages beat a big, complicated site nobody finishes reading.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do I really need a website if I already have a Facebook or Instagram page?

They work best together. Social media is great for showing your work and staying in touch, but you are renting that audience and playing by the platform's rules. A website is the one place you own outright — and it is where Google sends people who are searching for what you do right now.

### How much does a small business website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the figure before we start: from **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth, and **£2,370** for Complete. Ongoing care plans start at £27 a month, and there is no hourly billing or surprise invoice.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make small changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### Can I just build it myself instead?

Yes, and for a simple site a builder like Squarespace can work well if you have the time to learn it and write your own content. The trade-off is hours and a bit of a learning curve. If you would rather stay focused on the business, that is exactly the part we take off your hands.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website (or a competitor's, if you do not have one yet). We walk through what is working, what is putting people off, and the quickest wins to fix first — no cost and no obligation to book anything after.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a small business in the UK, you have probably asked yourself the same thing more than once: website vs Facebook page, which does your business need? It is a fair question. A Facebook page is free, quick to set up, and where plenty of your customers already spend their evenings. A website costs money and takes a little planning. So is it worth having both, or will one of them do the job on its own?

The honest answer is that they do two different jobs. One helps people notice you; the other helps them trust you and take action. Which one matters most depends on how customers find you and what you are asking them to do next. This guide walks you through it in plain English — no jargon, no pressure — so you can put your time and money where they actually count.

## What a Facebook page does well

A Facebook page earns its place. It is free, it takes minutes to create, and it puts you in front of people who are already scrolling. For a café posting the day's specials, a barber showing off fresh cuts, or a florist sharing this week's arrangements, it is a brilliant way to stay visible and keep the conversation going. Customers can message you, leave reviews, and share your posts with friends — which is word of mouth doing its work at speed.

If your business runs on regular updates and a loyal local following, a Facebook page will pull its weight. The trouble starts the moment someone wants more than a quick scroll — when they are ready to compare you, check your prices, or actually book.

## Where a Facebook page holds you back

The first problem is ownership. You do not own your Facebook page — Facebook does. The rules, the reach and the layout are theirs to change whenever they choose, and you can wake up one morning to find far fewer people are seeing your posts unless you pay to boost them. You are also sitting right next to your competitors, other people's adverts, and every distraction the feed can throw at a customer.

The second problem is Google. When someone types 'emergency plumber Bristol' or 'wedding photographer near me', it is proper websites that fill the results, not Facebook pages. If you rely on a page alone, you are close to invisible to the people who are actively looking to spend money right now — and those are the customers you most want to reach.

> A Facebook page rents you an audience. A website means you own the shop.

## What a website does that a Facebook page can't

A website is the one place online that is completely yours. It works around the clock, answers the questions your customers actually have, and it is built to be found on Google. Here is what it gives you that a page never will:

- You own it outright. Your site, your domain and your data — with no landlord who can change the rules or quietly switch off your reach.
- You get found on Google. A well-built site with sensible [local SEO](/services/seo) can appear exactly when people search for what you sell.
- You look the part. A tidy, professional site tells a first-time visitor you are a real, settled business worth trusting with their money.
- You control the journey. Booking forms, clear prices, service pages and obvious next steps guide people towards enquiring or buying.
- You keep their full attention. No adverts, no rival posts, no bottomless feed — just your business and the customer.

## Website vs Facebook page: which does your business need?

Here is the honest steer. If you are testing an idea this weekend, have next to no budget, and simply want to post updates to a local crowd, start with a Facebook page. It costs nothing and gets you moving today.

But the moment you want to be found on Google, take bookings, look credible to complete strangers, or stop depending on someone else's platform, you need a website. For most established local businesses — trades, clinics, salons, restaurants and professional services — that moment has already arrived. A [professional website](/services/website-design) becomes the foundation, and the Facebook page becomes one of several ways people arrive at it.

## The smart order: build the foundation, then feed it

You do not have to pick one forever. The businesses that get this right use both, in the right order. Your website is home base — where your prices, services and booking sit, and where Google sends people who are searching. Your Facebook page is a shop window on a busy street, there to catch attention and point people back home.

So keep the page for daily posts, offers and friendly chat, and send every visitor to your site to actually book, buy or enquire. And whoever ends up looking after it, make sure the site stays fast, updated and secure — that is exactly what a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for, so nothing quietly breaks in the background while you are busy running the business.

## Not sure where to start? That is normal

If all of this feels like one more job you do not have time for, you are not alone — and it is the part we handle every day. We build fixed-price websites for UK small businesses, so you know the cost before we begin, with [clear, upfront pricing](/pricing) and no hourly surprises. Most sites go live in three to six weeks, you own everything at the end, and you deal with a small team directly rather than a faceless call centre.

If you would like a plain-English second opinion first, we are happy to record a short walkthrough of your current website or Facebook page and show you what is helping and what is holding you back. No charge and no obligation — [get in touch](/contact) and we will take a proper look.

## The quick wins

- A Facebook page helps people notice you; a website helps them trust you and take action.
- You own a website — your Facebook page belongs to Facebook, and its reach can change overnight.
- Most local searches lead to websites, not Facebook pages, so a site is what gets you found on Google.
- The strongest setup is usually both: a website as home base and a Facebook page that points to it.

## Frequently asked questions

### Can I just use a Facebook page instead of a website?

You can, and for a brand-new side project it is a sensible place to start. But a Facebook page rarely shows up on Google, you do not own it, and Facebook can change your reach at any time. Once you want to be found by people searching for what you sell, a website becomes the safer home for your business.

### Do I still need a Facebook page if I have a website?

Often, yes — they work best together. Your website is where people book, buy and enquire, and where Google sends searchers. Your Facebook page is a friendly way to stay visible, post updates and chat with regulars, all pointing back to your site.

### How much does a small business website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the figure up front with no hourly billing. They start at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, and care plans start at **£27 a month** if you would like us to keep it running smoothly. You can see exactly what each option includes on our pricing page.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. We agree the timeline before we start, so there are no surprises, and every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare.

### Will a website really bring in more customers than Facebook?

There are no guarantees, and the honest answer is that it depends on your trade and your area. What a website reliably does is make you easier to find on Google, easier to trust at a glance, and easier to book — the groundwork that gives you the best chance of turning searchers into paying customers.

title: How to Get Your Business on Google
description: Learn how to get your business on Google with a simple, free step-by-step guide — from your Business Profile to reviews. Book a free teardown to fast-track it.
metaTitle: How to Get Your Business on Google (Step by Step)
excerpt: A plain-English guide to getting your business on Google — the free listing, the website and the habits that keep you showing up.
targetKeyword: how to get your business on google

cta.heading: Want to skip the guesswork?
cta.sub: Book a free teardown and we'll show you exactly what's holding your Google visibility back — in plain English, with no obligation.

faq.q: How do I get my business on Google for free?
faq.a: Create a **Google Business Profile** at google.com/business, verify it when Google asks, then fill it in fully — name, address, phone, categories, hours, photos and services. That free listing is what puts you in the map results, and it's the single most effective free step you can take.

faq.q: How long does it take to appear on Google?
faq.a: Your Business Profile can show up within days of being verified. Ranking well in the map pack and the main results takes longer — usually a few weeks to a few months of steady work. If we build your site, most go live in 3–6 weeks, though we never promise a specific ranking or timescale, because no honest agency can.

faq.q: How much does it cost to get a business on Google?
faq.a: The Google Business Profile itself is free. A website with us is fixed-price and starts at £477 (Starter), £1,170 (Growth) or £2,370 (Complete). Care plans start at £27 a month, and ongoing SEO is quoted per project so you always know the cost up front.

faq.q: Do I need a website to appear on Google?
faq.a: No — a Google Business Profile on its own can get you into the map results. But a website helps you rank in the main blue-link results, builds trust, and gives customers somewhere to land and get in touch. The two work far better together than either does alone.

faq.q: Why isn't my business showing up on Google?
faq.a: The usual culprits are a profile that hasn't been verified, one that's only half-filled, the wrong category, or a name, address and phone number that don't match across the web. Brand-new profiles also take a little time before Google trusts them, so give it a few weeks alongside the fixes.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a café in Leeds, a plumbing round in Bristol or a hair salon in Cardiff, most of your next customers will find you the same way. They type what they need into Google and pick from the first few results. Learning how to get your business on Google is one of the highest-value things a small business can do, and the best part is that most of it is free and well within your reach.

This guide walks through it in plain English — no jargon, no expensive tools. We'll cover the free listing that puts you on the map, the website that helps you rank, and the everyday habits that keep you visible. And if you'd rather hand the whole thing over, we'll be honest about that too.

## First, understand where you can show up

When someone searches for a local service, Google shows businesses in two main free spots. There's the map pack — the little map near the top with three businesses pinned to it — and there are the ordinary blue-link results below it. Paid ads sit above both, but you don't have to buy your way in to be seen.

Most local searches happen on a phone, often with the words "near me" attached. So the person looking for you is usually nearby, ready to call or visit, and deciding in seconds. Your job is to appear in those free spots and give them an easy reason to choose you.

> You don't need to pay Google to appear on Google — the listings that earn the most trust are the free ones.

## How to get your business on Google for free

The single most important step is a Google Business Profile (you may remember it as Google My Business). It's free, it powers that map pack, and it's the fastest way to appear at all. Go to google.com/business, search for your business, then either claim the existing listing or create a new one. Google will verify that you're really you — usually by post, phone or a short video.

Once you're in, the difference between invisible and chosen is how completely you fill it out. Give Google — and your customers — everything they need:

- Your exact business name, address and phone number, written identically everywhere online
- The most accurate categories you can pick (for example "emergency plumber", not just "plumber")
- Real photos of your premises, team and finished work, rather than stock images
- Opening hours, including bank holidays and seasonal changes
- A short, honest description of what you do and the areas you cover
- Your key products or services, with prices where it helps people decide

> A half-finished profile is the most common reason a good business stays invisible. Complete listings get chosen far more often.

## Give Google a proper website to trust

Your profile gets you onto the map. A website gets you into the blue-link results and gives Google a reason to trust you over time. It doesn't need to be big — a handful of clear, well-written pages beats a bloated site every time. Aim for a simple homepage, a page for each main service, a page about the areas you cover, and contact details that match your Business Profile word for word.

Speed and mobile matter more than looks. If a page is slow or fiddly to read on a phone, people leave before they've read a word. This is exactly what good [website design](/services/website-design) and solid [local SEO](/services/seo) are for: pages that load fast, read well and tell Google clearly what you do and where you do it.

## Reviews, posts and staying visible

Getting on Google is step one. Staying near the top is the ongoing part, and it's mostly about steady habits rather than clever tricks. Reviews are the biggest lever a small business has, so ask every happy customer for one and reply to each review you receive, good or bad. It shows Google you're active and shows customers you care.

Beyond that, keep your information accurate whenever anything changes, post the occasional update or offer, and pick up a few local mentions — your trade body, a chamber of commerce, a supplier's website. None of it is dramatic. Consistency over months is what quietly moves you up, and there are no shortcuts or guaranteed rankings, whatever anyone tells you.

## Do it yourself, or let us do it for you

Everything above is genuinely doable on your own across a few evenings, and plenty of business owners get a long way with just a well-kept Business Profile. If that's you, go for it — the steps in this guide are the whole recipe.

If you'd rather not spend your evenings on it, this is exactly what we do. We're a small UK team, we work at a fixed price with no lock-in, and you own your site, domain and data. We'll build the website, set up and optimise your Google Business Profile, and look after the ongoing bits on a [care plan](/services/care-plans) if you want us to. You can see the numbers on our [pricing](/pricing) page — websites start at £477, care plans from £27 a month.

The easiest first step is a free teardown: a short, screen-recorded review of your current site and listing, showing you exactly where you're losing customers right now. No pressure and nothing to prepare — just [get in touch](/contact) and we'll take a look.

## The quick wins

- A Google Business Profile is free and the fastest way to appear — fill it in completely, not halfway.
- A small, fast, mobile-friendly website helps you rank in the main results and builds trust.
- Reviews and consistency keep you visible over time; nobody can honestly promise guaranteed rankings.

## Frequently asked questions

### How do I get my business on Google for free?

Create a **Google Business Profile** at google.com/business, verify it when Google asks, then fill it in fully — name, address, phone, categories, hours, photos and services. That free listing is what puts you in the map results, and it's the single most effective free step you can take.

### How long does it take to appear on Google?

Your Business Profile can show up within days of being verified. Ranking well in the map pack and the main results takes longer — usually a few weeks to a few months of steady work. If we build your site, most go live in 3–6 weeks, though we never promise a specific ranking or timescale, because no honest agency can.

### How much does it cost to get a business on Google?

The Google Business Profile itself is free. A website with us is fixed-price and starts at £477 (Starter), £1,170 (Growth) or £2,370 (Complete). Care plans start at £27 a month, and ongoing SEO is quoted per project so you always know the cost up front.

### Do I need a website to appear on Google?

No — a Google Business Profile on its own can get you into the map results. But a website helps you rank in the main blue-link results, builds trust, and gives customers somewhere to land and get in touch. The two work far better together than either does alone.

### Why isn't my business showing up on Google?

The usual culprits are a profile that hasn't been verified, one that's only half-filled, the wrong category, or a name, address and phone number that don't match across the web. Brand-new profiles also take a little time before Google trusts them, so give it a few weeks alongside the fixes.

intro: If you run a café in Leeds, a plumbing round in Bristol or a hair salon in Cardiff, most of your next customers will find you the same way. They type what they need into Google and pick from the first few results. Learning how to get your business on Google is one of the highest-value things a small business can do, and the best part is that most of it is free and well within your reach.

This guide walks through it in plain English — no jargon, no expensive tools. We'll cover the free listing that puts you on the map, the website that helps you rank, and the everyday habits that keep you visible. And if you'd rather hand the whole thing over, we'll be honest about that too.

sections.h2: First, understand where you can show up
paragraphs: When someone searches for a local service, Google shows businesses in two main free spots. There's the map pack — the little map near the top with three businesses pinned to it — and there are the ordinary blue-link results below it. Paid ads sit above both, but you don't have to buy your way in to be seen.

Most local searches happen on a phone, often with the words "near me" attached. So the person looking for you is usually nearby, ready to call or visit, and deciding in seconds. Your job is to appear in those free spots and give them an easy reason to choose you.
sections.pullquote: You don't need to pay Google to appear on Google — the listings that earn the most trust are the free ones.

sections.h2: How to get your business on Google for free
paragraphs: The single most important step is a Google Business Profile (you may remember it as Google My Business). It's free, it powers that map pack, and it's the fastest way to appear at all. Go to google.com/business, search for your business, then either claim the existing listing or create a new one. Google will verify that you're really you — usually by post, phone or a short video.
Once you're in, the difference between invisible and chosen is how completely you fill it out. Give Google — and your customers — everything they need:
bullets: Your exact business name, address and phone number, written identically everywhere online
The most accurate categories you can pick (for example "emergency plumber", not just "plumber")
Real photos of your premises, team and finished work, rather than stock images
Opening hours, including bank holidays and seasonal changes
A short, honest description of what you do and the areas you cover
Your key products or services, with prices where it helps people decide
sections.pullquote: A half-finished profile is the most common reason a good business stays invisible. Complete listings get chosen far more often.

sections.h2: Give Google a proper website to trust
paragraphs: Your profile gets you onto the map. A website gets you into the blue-link results and gives Google a reason to trust you over time. It doesn't need to be big — a handful of clear, well-written pages beats a bloated site every time. Aim for a simple homepage, a page for each main service, a page about the areas you cover, and contact details that match your Business Profile word for word.

Speed and mobile matter more than looks. If a page is slow or fiddly to read on a phone, people leave before they've read a word. This is exactly what good [website design](/services/website-design) and solid [local SEO](/services/seo) are for: pages that load fast, read well and tell Google clearly what you do and where you do it.

sections.h2: Reviews, posts and staying visible
paragraphs: Getting on Google is step one. Staying near the top is the ongoing part, and it's mostly about steady habits rather than clever tricks. Reviews are the biggest lever a small business has, so ask every happy customer for one and reply to each review you receive, good or bad. It shows Google you're active and shows customers you care.

Beyond that, keep your information accurate whenever anything changes, post the occasional update or offer, and pick up a few local mentions — your trade body, a chamber of commerce, a supplier's website. None of it is dramatic. Consistency over months is what quietly moves you up, and there are no shortcuts or guaranteed rankings, whatever anyone tells you.

sections.h2: Do it yourself, or let us do it for you
paragraphs: Everything above is genuinely doable on your own across a few evenings, and plenty of business owners get a long way with just a well-kept Business Profile. If that's you, go for it — the steps in this guide are the whole recipe.

If you'd rather not spend your evenings on it, this is exactly what we do. We're a small UK team, we work at a fixed price with no lock-in, and you own your site, domain and data. We'll build the website, set up and optimise your Google Business Profile, and look after the ongoing bits on a [care plan](/services/care-plans) if you want us to. You can see the numbers on our [pricing](/pricing) page — websites start at £477, care plans from £27 a month.

The easiest first step is a free teardown: a short, screen-recorded review of your current site and listing, showing you exactly where you're losing customers right now. No pressure and nothing to prepare — just [get in touch](/contact) and we'll take a look.

keyTakeaways: A Google Business Profile is free and the fastest way to appear — fill it in completely, not halfway.
A small, fast, mobile-friendly website helps you rank in the main results and builds trust.
Reviews and consistency keep you visible over time; nobody can honestly promise guaranteed rankings.

bodyMarkdown:
Ask ten people what makes a good small business website and you'll get ten answers, usually about colours and logos. The version that actually wins you work is quieter than that. It loads fast, it says who you help in the first few seconds, and it makes getting in touch effortless. Whether you run a plumbing firm in Leeds or a café on a Bristol high street, your site has one real job: turn a curious visitor into a phone call, a booking or an enquiry.

The good news is you don't need a big budget or a design degree to get this right. You need a handful of fundamentals done well. Here's what separates a small business website that earns its keep from one that just sits there looking pretty.

## Fast, and Built for the Phone First

Most people will find you on their phone, often on patchy mobile data while they're out and about. If your homepage takes more than a few seconds to appear, a good number of them leave before they've read a word. Speed is not a nice-to-have — it is the front door to everything else.

A mobile-first [website design](/services/website-design) means text you can read without pinching, buttons big enough for a thumb, and images that appear quickly rather than crawling in one strip at a time. Test your own site honestly: open it on your phone, on mobile data rather than the office wifi, and time how long it takes before it is actually useful.

## Say What You Do, and Where, Straight Away

A visitor should grasp three things within seconds of landing: what you offer, who it is for, and how to get it. A joiner in Sheffield should not make people scroll and guess what he actually does. 'Fitted wardrobes and bespoke kitchens across South Yorkshire' beats 'Welcome to our website' every single time. Put that headline where people see it first, before they have to think.

Vague, clever wording feels safe, but it costs you enquiries. Plain and specific always wins.

- What you do, in plain words, high up on the page
- The towns and areas you actually cover
- A clear next step: call, book or send a message
- A reason or two to choose you over the firm down the road

## Earn Trust Before You've Said a Word

People hire businesses they believe will turn up and do a good job. Your website has to earn that belief before you have spoken a single word. Real photos of your own work or premises beat stock images of strangers in hard hats. Honest reviews, a proper address, and a phone number a human actually answers all quietly signal that you are a real, settled business — not one that is here today and gone tomorrow. These are the things a nervous first-time customer is scanning for, even if they could not tell you so.

> Trust is built in small details: a real photo, a local phone number, a price you are not hiding.

## Point Every Visitor to One Clear Action

Every page should gently nudge towards the same next step. When someone is ready to get in touch, do not make them hunt for how. A phone number in the header, a short contact form, an enquiry button that is always within reach — these remove the tiny frictions that quietly lose you work. A visitor who has to dig for your number will often just tap back and try the next firm on the list. Decide on the one thing you most want people to do, then make it the easiest thing on the page. One clear action beats five competing ones.

## Built to Be Found, and Kept Alive

A beautiful website nobody can find is a poster in a locked room. Getting the basics of [local SEO](/services/seo) right — a tidy Google Business Profile, pages that match what people genuinely search for, honest page titles — helps you appear when someone nearby types 'emergency electrician near me'. Most local searches happen on a mobile, often with real urgency behind them.

A site also needs to stay current. Hours change, services grow, offers come and go. A simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps yours fast, secure and up to date, so it does not quietly rot a year after launch and start turning people away.

## So, What Makes a Good Small Business Website?

Pull it together and the answer is refreshingly unglamorous. A good small business website loads fast, says what you do and where, earns trust with real detail, points to one clear action, and can be found and kept current. Nail those five and the design tends to look after itself.

You can absolutely do this yourself. But if you would rather spend your hours running your business than wrestling with page speed and headlines, this is exactly what we do — fixed-price, UK-based, and yours to keep. Either way, the fundamentals above are the bar to aim for.

## The quick wins

- Speed and mobile-friendliness come first — most visitors arrive on a phone.
- Say what you do, who it is for and where, within seconds of landing.
- Real photos, honest reviews and clear contact details build trust before you speak.
- Point every page towards one clear action, usually a call or enquiry.
- Be findable with local SEO, then keep the site current so it keeps working.

## Frequently asked questions

### What makes a small business website 'good' rather than just nice-looking?

Results, not decoration. A good site loads fast on a phone, makes it obvious what you do and where, earns trust with real photos and reviews, and points every visitor towards one clear action. Looks matter, but they come after those fundamentals, not before them.

### How much does a small business website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. Prices start at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, while care plans start at **£27 a month**. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. You can see exactly what each tier includes on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

### How long does it take to build?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending mainly on how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you are not left stranded the moment it goes live.

### Do I really need a designer, or can I build it myself?

You can build a solid site yourself if you follow the fundamentals — fast loading, a clear message, real trust signals and one obvious next step. Plenty of small businesses do. The real question is whether your time is better spent on that or on the work you are actually paid for. If you would rather it was handled, that is what we are here for, and you own the finished site, domain and data outright.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website. We walk through what is working, what is quietly costing you enquiries, and the quickest wins to fix — in plain English, with no jargon and no obligation. It is the easiest way to see where your site really stands.

bodyMarkdown:
Your website is often the first real impression someone gets of your business — long before they ring you, walk through the door or hand over any money. So it has a job to do, and doing it badly is expensive. This guide walks through the 10 things every small business website needs, in plain English, so you can open your own site tonight and spot exactly what is missing.

The good news is that you do not need to be technical to fix most of it. Whether you run a café in Leeds, a plumbing round in Bristol or a two-person accountancy practice in Surrey, the same short list of essentials decides whether a visitor turns into a customer or quietly clicks away to a competitor. It is the same list whether you have ten customers a week or a hundred.

## The 10 things every small business website needs

Here is the full checklist. Read it with your own site open in another tab. If you are missing more than two or three of these, that is usually the reason the phone is not ringing as often as it should — and most of them are quicker to fix than you think.

- A clear headline that says exactly what you do and who you do it for
- An obvious way to get in touch on every single page
- A phone number that taps straight to a call on mobile
- Fast loading, especially over a patchy phone signal
- Proof you can be trusted — reviews, real photos and credentials
- Your services explained plainly, with honest pricing signals
- A Google Business Profile linked to your website
- Local SEO so nearby customers can actually find you
- A mobile-first layout that works with one thumb
- Security, backups and someone keeping it updated

## Make it effortless to get in touch

The single most common mistake we see is hiding the contact details. Put your phone number in the header of every page, make it **tap-to-call** on mobile, and add a short contact form that asks for the bare minimum: a name, a number, and a line about what they need. Bury it, and people will simply ring someone else.

Add your address and opening hours as well. It sounds obvious, but it tells a nervous first-time visitor that you are a real, local business rather than a faceless website — and that reassurance is often what tips them into making contact.

> Most people decide within seconds whether to stay — so never make them dig for your phone number.

## Earn trust before the first phone call

People buy from businesses they believe in, and your website has to earn that belief before anyone calls. Real photos of your team and your finished work beat stock images every time. Add genuine reviews, any trade accreditations or professional memberships, and a straightforward page that says who you are and why you do this. Nobody expects a small business to look like a national brand, but they do expect it to look cared for.

Then lay your services out clearly and be honest about pricing. Even a 'from' figure or a typical range helps people self-qualify before they get in touch. When your [web design](/services/website-design) does the reassuring for you, the enquiries that land are warmer, better matched and far easier to win.

## Help local customers find you

Most local searches happen on a phone, and a huge share of them include the words **near me** or a town name. To show up for those, claim and fully complete your Google Business Profile, link it to your site, and make sure your pages actually name the towns, neighbourhoods and areas you serve.

Strong [local SEO](/services/seo) is the difference between being found by someone who is ready to buy today and being invisible while the business down the road takes the call. It builds up over months rather than overnight, so the sooner you start, the more you will have banked when it matters.

## Keep it fast, safe and up to date

A slow website loses visitors before it has even finished loading. Compress your images, keep the design light, and test it on a real phone over mobile data rather than your office wi-fi. Speed is not a nice-to-have; it quietly decides how many people stick around.

You also need the basics of housekeeping: HTTPS so browsers show the padlock, regular backups, and someone keeping software and plugins patched. An out-of-date site is one of the most common ways small businesses get hacked, so if your site was built years ago and nobody has touched it since, assume this is where the risk is hiding. A simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) covers exactly this — the dull-but-vital maintenance handled quietly in the background so you can get on with the actual work.

## Prefer to have it done for you?

You can absolutely work through this list yourself, and plenty of owners do a fine job of it. But if you would rather it was built properly the first time, without the late nights, that is what we are here for.

We build **fixed-price** websites — [prices start at £477](/pricing) — that you own outright, with no lock-in and no jargon. Most go live in three to six weeks, and every build comes with a simple admin panel so you can make small edits yourself. If you are not sure where your current site is letting you down, [send us the address](/contact) and we will take a proper look.

## The quick wins

- A clear message and an obvious way to make contact matter more than any flashy design.
- Trust signals — real photos, honest reviews and clear pricing — turn visitors into enquiries.
- Local SEO and a linked Google Business Profile are how nearby customers actually find you.
- Speed, HTTPS and regular maintenance keep your site working, safe and worth trusting.

## Frequently asked questions

### What is the most important thing a small business website needs?

A clear message and an easy way to make contact. If a visitor can tell what you do, who you do it for, and how to reach you within a few seconds, you are already ahead of most local competitors. Everything else on the list supports those two things.

### How much does a small business website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. Plans start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with optional care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes 30 days of aftercare once you are live, so you are not left on your own the moment it launches.

### Do I need to be technical to run the website myself?

No. Every site we build comes with a simple admin panel for the small changes — updating text, prices or photos — and you own your site, domain and data outright. If you would rather not touch it at all, a care plan means we handle updates, backups and security for you.

### Should I build the website myself or hire someone?

If you have the time and enjoy the process, the checklist above will get you a long way. Most owners hire help because their time is better spent running the business, and because a professional build tends to get the trust and local-SEO details right the first time. A free teardown is a no-pressure way to see which route makes sense for you.

paragraphs: People buy from businesses they believe in, and your website has to earn that belief before anyone calls. Real photos of your team and your finished work beat stock images every time. Add genuine reviews, any trade accreditations or professional memberships, and a straightforward page that says who you are and why you do this. Nobody expects a small business to look like a national brand, but they do expect it to look cared for.

Then lay your services out clearly and be honest about pricing. Even a 'from' figure or a typical range helps people self-qualify before they get in touch. When your [web design](/services/website-design) does the reassuring for you, the enquiries that land are warmer, better matched and far easier to win.

sections.h2: Help local customers find you
paragraphs: Most local searches happen on a phone, and a huge share of them include the words **near me** or a town name. To show up for those, claim and fully complete your Google Business Profile, link it to your site, and make sure your pages actually name the towns, neighbourhoods and areas you serve.

Strong [local SEO](/services/seo) is the difference between being found by someone who is ready to buy today and being invisible while the business down the road takes the call. It builds up over months rather than overnight, so the sooner you start, the more you will have banked when it matters.

sections.h2: Keep it fast, safe and up to date
paragraphs: A slow website loses visitors before it has even finished loading. Compress your images, keep the design light, and test it on a real phone over mobile data rather than your office wi-fi. Speed is not a nice-to-have; it quietly decides how many people stick around.

You also need the basics of housekeeping: HTTPS so browsers show the padlock, regular backups, and someone keeping software and plugins patched. An out-of-date site is one of the most common ways small businesses get hacked, so if your site was built years ago and nobody has touched it since, assume this is where the risk is hiding. A simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) covers exactly this — the dull-but-vital maintenance handled quietly in the background so you can get on with the actual work.

sections.h2: Prefer to have it done for you?
paragraphs: You can absolutely work through this list yourself, and plenty of owners do a fine job of it. But if you would rather it was built properly the first time, without the late nights, that is what we are here for.

We build **fixed-price** websites — [prices start at £477](/pricing) — that you own outright, with no lock-in and no jargon. Most go live in three to six weeks, and every build comes with a simple admin panel so you can make small edits yourself. If you are not sure where your current site is letting you down, [send us the address](/contact) and we will take a proper look.

keyTakeaways: A clear message and an obvious way to make contact matter more than any flashy design.
Trust signals — real photos, honest reviews and clear pricing — turn visitors into enquiries.
Local SEO and a linked Google Business Profile are how nearby customers actually find you.
Speed, HTTPS and regular maintenance keep your site working, safe and worth trusting.

bodyMarkdown:
Choosing the wrong web design agency costs you twice: once when you pay for the site, and again when you pay someone else to fix it. This guide on how to choose a web design agency is a plain UK checklist for busy small-business owners — no jargon, no sales pressure.

If you have never hired for this before, the market can feel like a fog of vague quotes and promises that sound too good to be true. Below are the questions to ask, the warning signs to walk away from, and an honest note on when it makes sense to do it yourself.

## Get clear on what you need first

Before you speak to a single agency, spend twenty minutes writing down what the site is actually for. A dentist in Leeds who wants more implant enquiries needs something different from a plumber in Bristol who mainly wants the phone to ring. Your goal shapes everything — the pages, the copy, the calls to action, even the platform.

You do not need a technical spec. You need clarity on three things: the one action you want visitors to take, the handful of pages you genuinely need, and a rough budget. An agency worth hiring will take that plain brief and ask sharper questions, not bury you in acronyms. If you want a steer on what a small-business site should include, our [website design service](/services/website-design) lays it out without the tech-speak.

## Your UK checklist for choosing a web design agency

Once you have a shortlist of two or three agencies, ask every one of them the same questions. Asking the same things is the only way to compare like for like, because a polished sales pitch can hide a very ordinary service.

- **Who owns the site, domain and content when it is finished?** The answer should always be you — no exceptions.
- **Is the price fixed, or billed by the hour?** Fixed pricing means you know the full cost before any work begins.
- **Who will I actually deal with, and can I ring them?** A small team that answers the phone beats a faceless ticket queue.
- **What happens after launch?** Ask about training, aftercare, and whether you can update the site yourself.
- **Can I see real work for businesses like mine?** Ask for live UK sites you can click through, not just screenshots.
- **How long until it goes live, and what do you need from me to hit that date?**

> The single most important question is the simplest: when this is finished, do I own everything?

## Red flags that should make you walk away

Some warning signs are worth walking away from even when the price looks tempting. Be wary of anyone who promises to get you ranked number one on Google — no honest agency can guarantee that, and Google itself says so.

Watch for vague pricing that only firms up after you have signed, contracts that quietly lock your domain or content to the agency, and portfolios full of identical templates with only the town name swapped in. If they cannot show you a live site and a real person who worked on it, keep looking. Good [local SEO](/services/seo) is a long game built on honest work, not a switch someone flips for a monthly fee.

## What a fair price actually looks like

Price is where most small-business owners feel out of their depth, so it helps to know the shape of the market. In the UK, a simple, well-built small-business website typically runs from a few hundred pounds for a starter site to a few thousand for something larger with custom design and SEO built in.

What matters more than the headline number is what sits behind it. Ask whether the quote is fixed or an estimate that can creep. Ask what is included — copywriting, images, training, aftercare — and what costs extra later. Our own [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and published for exactly this reason: websites start at £477, with no hourly meter running in the background. Ongoing [care plans](/services/care-plans) start at £27 a month if you would rather someone kept the site updated and secure for you.

## Do it yourself, or hand it over?

You can absolutely build a small site yourself with a website builder, and for a brand-new venture testing an idea, that is often the sensible first step. The honest trade-off is time. A DIY site can eat your evenings for weeks and still look like a DIY site to the customer deciding whether to trust you.

Hiring an agency buys you speed, a professional finish, and someone to call when something breaks. If you would like to see how we approach it, our [recent work](/work) shows the kind of sites we build for UK trades, clinics and hospitality. When you are ready to sense-check your shortlist, the easiest next step is a free teardown — a short screen-recorded review of your current site with the three or four things we would fix first.

## The quick wins

- Get clear on your one goal and rough budget before you shortlist anyone.
- Insist on owning your site, domain and content — and on fixed pricing you can see up front.
- Walk away from guaranteed-ranking promises and template portfolios with the town name swapped in.
- Compare two or three agencies on the same questions, then book a teardown to sense-check your choice.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a small-business website cost in the UK?

It varies with size and complexity. As a guide, our fixed-price websites start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. The key is to agree a fixed price up front so the cost cannot creep once work begins.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most well-run small-business sites go live in three to six weeks. The timeline depends less on the agency and more on how quickly you can supply content, photos and feedback. Every build we do includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you are not left stranded at launch.

### Should I choose a local agency or a national one?

Neither is automatically better. What matters is whether they understand UK small businesses, answer the phone, and can show you live local sites. A small UK-based team you deal with directly often beats a large agency where you become a ticket number.

### What questions should I ask a web design agency?

Ask who owns the finished site, whether the price is fixed or hourly, who you will actually deal with, what happens after launch, and to see live work for businesses like yours. Ask the same questions of every agency so you can compare fairly.

### Do I really own my website afterwards?

Yes, and you always should. With us you own your site, domain and data with no lock-in, so if you ever move on, everything comes with you. If an agency will not confirm that in writing, treat it as a red flag.
