bodyMarkdown:
Most small-business websites go live in three to six weeks. But the real question is how long does it take to build a website for your business, and the honest answer is that it depends less on the design than on you. The biggest delays almost never come from the code. They come from waiting on content, decisions and sign-off.

If you run a busy plumbing firm, a dental practice or a corner café, that's actually good news, because it means the timeline is largely in your hands. Here's where the weeks really go, what tends to hold a project up, and how to get a site live without letting it drag on for months.

## So how long does it take to build a website?

For a typical local-business site — a handful of pages, a contact form, maybe a booking link and a gallery of your work — three to six weeks from kickoff to launch is realistic. A single-page site can be quicker. A bigger site with lots of services, an online shop or custom features will take longer.

That range assumes one important thing: the words and pictures are ready, or someone is writing them for you. A five-page site can be built in a matter of days. It's rarely the building that takes six weeks; it's everything that feeds into it.

> It's rarely the building that takes six weeks — it's everything that feeds into it.

## A realistic week-by-week timeline

Here's how a straightforward local-business build tends to play out. Yours may vary, but the shape is usually similar:

- **Week 1 — Planning.** We agree the pages, the structure and what each one needs to say. You send over your logo, photos and any text you already have.
- **Weeks 2–3 — Design and build.** We design the homepage, get your sign-off, then build the rest to match. You watch it take shape on a private link.
- **Week 4 — Content and review.** Real words, real images, your feedback, and the small tweaks that make it feel like yours.
- **Weeks 5–6 — Testing and launch.** We check it on phones and tablets, connect your domain, set up the basics of [local SEO](/services/seo), then go live.

## What actually slows a website down

When a project runs late, it's almost never the design tool's fault. Four things cause most of the delay:

- **Content.** Writing the words, gathering photos, digging the logo out of an old email. This is the number-one hold-up, every single time.
- **Decisions.** Sign-off on the design, the wording, the colours. One decision-maker moves fast; a committee does not.
- **Third parties.** Your domain registrar, your old host, a booking or payment tool that needs connecting. These add days you didn't plan for.
- **Scope creep.** The "while we're at it, can we also..." requests. Each idea is reasonable on its own, but they stack up quickly.

> Only some of these are about design. Most are about content and decisions — which is why a clear brief up front saves weeks at the end.

## When it takes longer than six weeks

Some projects genuinely need more time, and it's worth knowing which. An online shop with products, stock and payments is a bigger job than a brochure site. A site in several languages, a members' area or heavy custom design will each add weeks. So will a full rebrand happening at the same time — new logo, new photography, new name — because the website has to wait on all of it.

None of that is a problem as long as it's planned. The trouble starts when a quick five-page site quietly grows into something twice the size halfway through. Agreeing the scope honestly at the start is what keeps the finish line where you can see it.

## How to get your website live faster

Since most delays come from your side of the fence — and I mean that kindly — a handful of habits get a site live sooner:

Fixed pricing is part of it, too. When you can see [what a site costs](/pricing) before anyone starts, the scope is agreed up front and the project doesn't stall while the budget gets debated.

- **Gather your content first.** A short paragraph on each service, ten good photos, your logo and a few reviews, ready before day one.
- **Name one decision-maker.** Someone who can say yes without a three-week email chain.
- **Keep the first version tight.** Launch with what you need and add the nice-to-haves later. A live site winning enquiries beats a perfect one that never ships.
- **Choose fixed pricing.** When the cost and scope are settled at the start, there's no mid-project renegotiation to stall things.

## Do it yourself, or have it built

You can build a small site yourself on a template builder over a weekend or two, and plenty of owners do. The trade-off is your time and the fiddly bits: mobile layouts, page speed, contact forms that actually send, and getting found on Google. Those are the parts that quietly eat your evenings.

If you'd rather not, that's our job. We handle the [website design and build](/services/website-design) end to end, you deal with a small UK team directly — we pick up the phone — and you own your site, domain and data with no lock-in. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, most sites go live inside that three-to-six-week window, and our fixed prices start at £477. A [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month keeps it updated and secure once you're live.

## The quick wins

- Most small-business websites go live in three to six weeks — the build itself is rarely the slow part.
- Content and decisions cause the biggest delays, not design or code.
- Having your words, photos and logo ready before day one is the fastest way to launch sooner.
- Bigger jobs — online shops, member areas, a rebrand at the same time — add weeks, so agree the scope honestly at the start.

## Frequently asked questions

### How long does it take to build a website?

For a typical local-business site, three to six weeks from kickoff to launch is realistic. Simple one-page sites can be quicker, and larger sites with an online shop or lots of custom features take longer. The single biggest factor is how quickly your content and decisions come together.

### How much does it cost to build a website?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front: Starter from £477, Growth from £1,170 and Complete from £2,370. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. No hourly billing and no surprise invoices.

### What slows a website project down the most?

Almost always content and decisions, not the build itself. Waiting on photos, wording, logos and sign-off is what stretches a two-week job into two months. Having your content ready before we start is the fastest way to launch sooner.

### Can you build my website faster than six weeks?

Often, yes. If your content is ready and the scope is tight, a small site can go live in a couple of weeks. We'll give you an honest timeline at the start rather than a hopeful one, and we can't promise a date if we're waiting on materials from your side.

### Will I be able to update the website myself afterwards?

Yes. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can change text and images without touching any code, plus 30 days of aftercare after launch. If you'd rather we handle the updates, a care plan covers that too.

bodyMarkdown:
Buying your first web address can feel oddly high-stakes. It's cheap, it takes five minutes, and yet it's the name your customers will type, say out loud and trust for years. So let's have domain names explained properly, and walk through how to choose the right one for your business without second-guessing yourself.

A domain name is simply your address on the web, the bit people type into the browser, like yourbakery.co.uk. You rent it, usually a year at a time, and while you can technically change it later, it's far better to get it right the first time. Here's how.

## Domain names explained, in plain English

Your domain name is your address on the web. When someone types yourbakery.co.uk into their browser, or Google shows it in the results, that's your domain. It has two parts: the name you choose (yourbakery) and the ending (.co.uk), sometimes called the extension.

You don't buy a domain outright the way you'd buy a van. You register it, usually for a year or two at a time, and renew it to keep it. As long as you keep renewing, it stays yours, and nobody else can take it.

## Start with the name people already know

The safest starting point is the name your customers already use for you. If your sign says Marlow Family Dental, then marlowfamilydental.co.uk is instantly recognisable and nobody has to guess. Matching your domain to your trading name keeps everything joined up across your van, your cards and your Google listing.

If the exact name is taken, don't panic. Adding your town or your trade often does the trick, and can even help locally: think marlowdental.co.uk or marlowfamilydentist.co.uk. What you want to avoid is drifting so far from your real name that people who have heard of you can't find you.

## Pick the right ending: .co.uk or .com?

For most UK local businesses, .co.uk is the natural choice. It tells people at a glance that you're British and that you serve the UK, which is exactly what a customer in Leeds or Cardiff wants to see. A .com tends to suit businesses with national or wider ambitions, or brands that want to look bigger than one town.

You'll also see newer endings like .uk, .london, or trade-specific ones like .dental. They can work, but tread carefully, as many customers still instinctively type .co.uk and may end up somewhere else. If you can, register both the .co.uk and the .com to protect your name, and point one at the other.

## The rules for a name that actually works

A good domain is one a customer can hear once and type correctly. Before you commit, run your shortlist past a few simple checks:

- Keep it short. Fewer letters means fewer typos and easier word-of-mouth.
- Make it easy to say out loud. If you have to spell it out twice on the phone, think again.
- Skip hyphens and numbers. Is it plumbers-24-7 or plumbers24seven? Nobody remembers, so drop them.
- Don't stuff in keywords. Something like cheap-emergency-plumber-london.co.uk looks like spam and puts people off.
- Check how it reads run together. Odd combinations appear when two innocent words collide, so read it as one string first.
- Test it on someone else. Say it aloud and ask them to write it down. If they get it right, you're onto a winner.

> The best domain names are boring in the best way: clear, short and impossible to get wrong.

## Buy it yourself, and make sure you own it

Here's the part people wish they'd known sooner. Whoever registers the domain, and holds the login, effectively controls it. Register it in your own name or your company's name, using an email address you'll keep, and store the login somewhere safe. It costs very little, often around a tenner a year, and that small step keeps you firmly in charge.

Be wary of anyone who registers your domain for you and won't hand over access. At Nexus Lab Systems we do the opposite: when we [build your website](/services/website-design), you own the site, the domain and the data, with no lock-in. It's your name, so it should sit in your account, not ours.

## When in doubt, we'll help you get it right

Choosing a domain isn't hard, but it is one of those decisions that's awkward to undo, so it's worth a few minutes of thought. Get the name right, keep the login safe, and you've got a solid foundation for everything that follows, from your website to your [local SEO](/services/seo).

If you'd rather not do it alone, that's what we're here for. We register domains, build the site and set up the essentials as part of every project, with clear fixed prices and no surprises. You can see exactly what's included on our [pricing](/pricing) page, whatever [trade you're in](/industries).

## The quick wins

- Match your domain to the business name people already know, and add your town or trade if it's taken.
- For most UK local businesses .co.uk is the safe pick; choose .com for national ambitions.
- Keep it short, easy to say, and free of hyphens and numbers.
- Register it in your own name so you always own your domain and data.

## Frequently asked questions

### Should I choose .co.uk or .com for my business?

For most UK local businesses, .co.uk is the safe choice because it clearly signals you're British and serving UK customers. Pick .com if you have national or wider ambitions, and if you can, register both to protect your name.

### Can I change my domain name later?

Yes, but it's more hassle than it sounds. You'd need to redirect the old address, update your listings, print new cards and give search engines time to catch up. It's much easier to choose a name you're happy with from the start.

### How much does a domain and website cost?

A domain itself is only around a tenner a year. Our websites are fixed-price and start at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, with care plans from **£27 a month**. We register and set up the domain as part of the build, so there are no hidden extras.

### How long does it take to get a website live?

Most of our sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you're up and running.

### Do I need to buy more than one domain?

Usually one is plenty. That said, many businesses register the matching .com alongside their .co.uk, and sometimes a common misspelling, then point the spares at their main address. It's cheap insurance against someone else grabbing them.

bodyMarkdown:
If you've ever tried to get a website off the ground, one question tends to stop you in your tracks: what is web hosting and which type do you need? It sounds technical, but the idea behind it is genuinely simple. Web hosting is the space you rent on a powerful computer, called a server, that keeps your website switched on around the clock — so anyone who types in your address sees your business rather than an error message.

The confusing bit isn't what hosting does. It's the menu you get handed: shared, VPS, cloud, dedicated, managed. Providers often make it sound more complicated than it is, and small businesses end up paying for power they will never use. For most UK firms — a plumber in Leeds, a café off a Bristol high street, a two-person accountancy practice — the right choice is usually simpler and cheaper than the sales page suggests. Let's clear it up.

## Think of hosting as renting premises online

The easiest way to picture web hosting is to compare it to a shop. Your domain name (something like yourbusiness.co.uk) is the address people use to find you. Your website is the shop itself — the shelves, the signage, the products. Hosting is the physical premises that everything sits inside. Rent that space, and your shop is open to the public. Stop paying for it, and the doors close.

A host gives you a few practical things: storage for your pages, text and images; bandwidth, which is the amount of visitor traffic you can handle; and uptime, the promise that your server stays online. Good hosting is quiet and invisible — pages load quickly, nothing falls over when you get busy, and you rarely think about it. Bad hosting shows up as slow pages and downtime, both of which cost you customers and, over time, hurt your [search rankings](/services/seo).

## The main types of web hosting explained

Almost every plan you will see is a version of one of these five. Here is what each one really means, in plain terms.

- **Shared hosting** — Your site sits on one server alongside many others, sharing the cost and the resources. It's the cheapest option and perfectly good for a standard small-business or brochure site. The trade-off is that a very busy neighbour can occasionally slow things down.
- **VPS (virtual private server)** — Still one physical server, but partitioned so you get a guaranteed slice of power that's yours alone. A sensible step up if your site grows busy or runs a small online shop.
- **Dedicated hosting** — A whole server to yourself. Powerful, expensive, and almost always overkill for a local business. This is for large sites with heavy traffic.
- **Cloud hosting** — Your site runs across a network of servers, so it can scale up when traffic spikes and you pay for what you use. Flexible and reliable, though the pricing can be less predictable.
- **Managed WordPress hosting** — Hosting tuned specifically for WordPress, with updates, security and backups handled for you. Costs a little more, but saves you the technical upkeep.

## Which type of web hosting do you need?

Here is the honest answer most small businesses never hear: you almost certainly need shared or managed hosting, not the pricey tiers. If you're running a website that shows what you do, builds trust and brings in enquiries — the kind of site most tradespeople, clinics, shops and professional firms need — shared hosting comfortably handles it. Choose managed hosting instead if your site is built on WordPress and you would rather not touch the technical side.

You only need to think about VPS or cloud hosting once you're doing serious volume: a busy online shop, thousands of daily visitors, or an app-like site. And dedicated hosting? Unless you're a large organisation with an IT budget to match, you can safely ignore it.

> For most UK small businesses, the right hosting is cheaper and simpler than the sales page wants you to believe.

## Hosting, domain and email are three different things

This trips people up constantly, so it's worth stating plainly. Your domain is your address, and you rent it separately, usually for £10 to £20 a year. Your hosting is the space your website lives in. And business email — like hello@yourbusiness.co.uk — is a separate service again, though it's often bought from the same provider. Keeping them clear matters, because you want to own all three yourself rather than have them locked inside someone else's account. If you ever change designers, you should be able to take your website, domain and data with you. That's the whole point of ownership, and it's how we build every site.

## What to look for in a host

If you do decide to sort hosting yourself, a few things separate a decent host from a frustrating one. Run down this quick checklist before you buy.

- **UK or nearby servers** — physically closer servers mean faster load times for your UK visitors.
- **A free SSL certificate** — the padlock in the browser bar; standard now, and something Google expects.
- **Daily backups** — so a mistake or a hack doesn't wipe out months of work.
- **Real support** — a human you can reach when something breaks, ideally by phone.
- **Room to grow** — an easy path to upgrade if your traffic climbs, without rebuilding everything.

## Or hand the whole thing to someone else

Here is the honest bit. You can absolutely set hosting up yourself — plenty of business owners do. But it means comparing plans, pointing your domain, installing certificates and keeping everything updated, all before you have written a single word about your actual business. For a lot of people, that's an afternoon better spent elsewhere.

When we [build a website](/services/website-design), hosting is simply part of the job. We pick the right type for your traffic, set it up properly and keep it maintained on a [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month — so you deal with one small, UK-based team rather than a stack of logins and invoices. Our sites are fixed-price from £477, you can see exactly what's included on the [pricing](/pricing) page, and you own the lot at the end. Prefer a second opinion on your current setup first? We're happy to [take a proper look](/contact).

## The quick wins

- Web hosting is the rented space that keeps your website online — no hosting, no website.
- Most UK small businesses need shared or managed hosting, not expensive VPS, cloud or dedicated plans.
- Hosting, your domain name and business email are three separate things, and you should own all three.

## Frequently asked questions

### What's the difference between web hosting and a domain name?

Your domain name is your address — what people type to find you, like yourbusiness.co.uk. Web hosting is the space your website actually lives in. You rent them separately, and you should own both yourself so you're never locked into one provider.

### Do I need expensive hosting for a small business website?

Almost never. A standard brochure or lead-generation site runs perfectly well on affordable shared or managed hosting. VPS, cloud and dedicated plans are built for busy online shops and large sites, so paying for them when you don't need them is money wasted.

### How much does a website and hosting cost with Nexus Lab Systems?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front: from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Ongoing hosting and maintenance sit on a care plan from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build and launch a site?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how much content and how many pages you need. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

### Can I move my hosting to a different provider later?

Yes. We don't lock anyone in — you own your site, your domain and your data. If you ever decide to move, you can take everything with you. That's how it should be.

bodyMarkdown:
Most small-business websites are polite, tidy and completely forgettable. They describe the business, list a few services, and then sit there hoping someone gets in touch. If yours feels a bit like that, you are not doing anything wrong on purpose. Nobody teaches a plumber, a dentist or a cafe owner how to write website content that converts a browser into a booking.

The good news is that this is a skill, not a talent. You do not need clever slogans or a marketing degree. You need to say the right things, in the right order, in language your customers actually use. Below is the exact approach we use when we write for clients, laid out so you can do it yourself, or at least know what good looks like.

## Start with the customer, not your business

Here is the mistake almost everyone makes. They open with a line about themselves: "Established in 2009, we are a family-run firm committed to quality and customer service." Nice, but your visitor does not care yet. They arrived with a problem, and in the first few seconds they are asking one silent question: "Can this business fix my thing?"

So answer it. If you are a boiler engineer in Leeds, your homepage should say something like "No hot water or heating in Leeds? We usually get you a same-week visit." That single line does more work than three paragraphs about your heritage. Write down the actual words your customers use when they describe their problem, then put those words near the top of the page.

- Lead with the problem you solve, not the year you were founded
- Use the customer's language, not industry terms ("blocked drain", not "drainage solutions")
- Make the first sentence answer "am I in the right place?"
- Save the company history for lower down, or the About page

## Write for one person reading on a phone

Most local searches happen on a mobile, often while someone is standing in a cold kitchen or sitting in a waiting room. That changes how you should write. Long, dense paragraphs that look fine on a laptop become an intimidating wall of grey on a small screen.

Picture one real customer and write as if you are speaking to them across a counter. Short sentences. Plenty of white space. One idea per paragraph. Read it back out loud, and if you run out of breath before the full stop, cut it in half. Warm, plain English beats clever every single time.

> If you run out of breath before the full stop, the sentence is too long.

## How to write website content that converts: the four-part structure

Every page that converts tends to follow the same simple shape. You do not need to be fancy, you just need all four parts present and in order. Missing one is usually why a page feels flat.

Work through these in sequence for your homepage and each main service page. It keeps you honest and stops you drifting into features nobody asked about. If you want to see how this looks applied across a full build, our approach to [website design](/services/website-design) is built around exactly this structure.

- **The promise**: what you do and who for, in one clear line at the top
- **The proof**: why you can be trusted (reviews, guarantees, years of experience, real photos)
- **The path**: what happens next, step by step, so there are no surprises
- **The prompt**: a clear, single next action, repeated where it makes sense

## Turn features into benefits your customer feels

A feature is a fact about what you do. A benefit is what that fact means for the customer's life. People buy the benefit, so your job is to translate. "Fixed-price quotes" is a feature. "You know the full cost before we start, so there is no nasty surprise on the invoice" is the benefit, and it is far more persuasive.

Go through your services and, next to each feature, write "which means..." and finish the sentence. A dentist offering "same-day emergency appointments" really means "you will not have to sit at home in pain waiting for next week." That is the version that gets the phone to ring. When you are ready to talk pricing, be just as plain: our own [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and published for the same reason, because vague costs make people hesitate.

## Tell them exactly what to do next

A page can be perfectly written and still fail if it forgets to ask for the sale. Do not assume the visitor will figure out the next step on their own. Tell them plainly what to do and what will happen when they do it, so there is no risk in taking action.

Keep it to one main call to action per page and repeat it where it naturally fits, rather than scattering five different links that pull people in different directions. "Get in touch" is weak because it asks the customer to do the thinking. "Send us a photo of the leak and we will tell you roughly what it will cost" is strong because it lowers the effort and removes the mystery. If writing all of this yourself feels like one job too many, that is honestly fine. Have a look at what we [do for small businesses](/services), or just [book a free teardown](/contact) and we will show you what to change first.

## Edit ruthlessly, then check it against reality

First drafts are always too long and too polite. Once you have written a page, go back and cut anything that does not help the reader decide. Delete the throat-clearing intros, the "we pride ourselves on" filler, and any sentence you could not say out loud to a customer without cringing.

Then do one final test. Give the page to someone who knows nothing about your business and ask them three questions: what does this business do, why would I choose them, and what do I do next? If they can answer all three in a few seconds, your content is doing its job. If they hesitate, you have found exactly what to fix.

## The quick wins

- Every feature should be followed by "which means..." and a real human benefit
- Specific beats vague: numbers, timescales and plain outcomes build trust
- One clear next step converts better than five competing options

## Frequently asked questions

### How long should a page of website content be?

Long enough to answer the customer's questions and no longer. For a local service page, that is often 300 to 600 words. Focus on covering the promise, the proof, the process and a clear next step. A short page that is clear will always beat a long page that rambles.

### Do I need to worry about SEO keywords when writing?

Write for the human first, then check your keywords second. Use the natural phrases your customers actually search for, like "emergency electrician Bristol", in your headings and opening lines. If you want help getting found on Google, our [SEO](/services/seo) work builds on good content rather than replacing it.

### How much does it cost to have you write and build the site?

Our websites are fixed-price so you know the cost up front, starting from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with copywriting included. Care plans start at £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you never get an hourly bill.

### How long does it take to get a new site live?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make edits yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### Can you just do the writing for me?

Yes. Good copywriting is included in our builds, and we can also rewrite the content on an existing site. The quickest way to see what needs changing is to book a free teardown, a short screen-recorded review of your current site with honest, specific suggestions.

title: How to Take Great Photos for Your Business Website
description: Learn how to take great photos for your business website using just your phone — simple lighting, framing and editing tips that win more local customers.
metaTitle: How to Take Great Photos for Your Business Website
excerpt: Great website photos don't need a pricey camera — just your phone, decent light and a few simple tricks. Here's how to get them right.
targetKeyword: how to take great photos for your business website

cta.sub: Book a free teardown and we'll show you exactly what's working, what isn't, and the quick wins hiding on your website.

bodyMarkdown:
Learning how to take great photos for your business website is one of the cheapest, fastest ways to win more customers — and one of the most overlooked. Most visitors decide whether you look trustworthy within a few seconds of landing on your page, and blurry snaps or obvious stock images quietly cost you enquiries every single day.

The good news is that you don't need an expensive camera or a design degree. With the phone already in your pocket and a handful of simple habits, you can take pictures that make your business look established, professional and genuinely yours. Here's exactly how to do it.

## Why real photos beat stock every time

Before anyone reads a word on your site, they have already judged it on the pictures. A sharp, honest photo of your actual shop, your team or your finished work tells a visitor you are real, local and worth trusting. A generic stock image of a smiling model in a headset does the opposite — people spot it instantly, and it slowly chips away at your credibility.

Think about how you choose a builder, a café or a dentist. You want to see the real place, the real faces and the real work before you commit. The same instinct is at play on your website, which means your photos are quietly doing sales for you around the clock, whether you have planned them or not.

> Your photos are quietly doing sales for you around the clock — for better or worse.

## How to take great photos for your business website with just a phone

You do not need to buy a camera. A modern smartphone takes pictures that are more than good enough for the web, as long as you handle the basics. Most local searches happen on mobile, so it makes sense that a phone captures images at exactly the sort of quality your customers will view them at.

Before you take a single shot, run through these quick habits:

- Wipe the lens. It sounds obvious, but a lens smeared from sitting in your pocket is the number one cause of soft, hazy photos.
- Tap the screen to focus on your subject, then hold steady with both hands or rest your elbows on something solid.
- Turn on the gridlines in your camera settings and keep horizons, shelves and door frames level — wonky photos look amateur.
- Shoot the same subject in both landscape and portrait, so you have the right shape for banners and for social later on.
- Avoid the digital zoom. Step closer instead — zooming just crops and softens the image.

## Get the light right

Light is the single biggest thing separating a great photo from a flat one, and it costs you nothing. Natural daylight is your best friend. Position your subject near a window or step outside, and shoot with the light falling onto the subject rather than coming from behind it, which leaves faces in shadow.

The famous British overcast sky is actually a gift here. A layer of cloud works like a giant softbox, spreading soft, even light with no harsh shadows — ideal for shopfronts, food and portraits. Avoid strong midday sun, which creates squinting faces and hard black shadows, and switch off any orange overhead lights indoors, as they give skin and walls an unflattering tint.

## Know what to photograph

Great individual shots still fall flat if they do not show the right things. Build a simple shot list before you start, so you come away with a full set that tells the whole story of your business rather than three photos of the same corner.

- Your premises — the shopfront, signage or the high street it sits on, so people can picture finding you.
- Your team — real faces, mid-conversation or at work, not a stiff line-up against a blank wall.
- Your work or products — close, well lit and from a few angles; before-and-after pairs are gold for trades and salons.
- Small details — a coffee being poured, tools laid out, a finished kitchen — the little touches that show you care.
- One strong horizontal hero shot for the very top of your homepage.

## Edit lightly, then think about the web

A little tidy-up goes a long way. Use your phone's built-in editor to straighten the horizon, crop out clutter and gently lift the brightness and contrast. Keep it natural — heavy filters date quickly and can misrepresent your colours, which matters if you are selling a product with a particular finish or shade.

One technical point people often miss: enormous photos slow your site down, and slow sites lose both visitors and rankings. Resize images to a sensible width before uploading, and give each file a plain, descriptive name such as oak-kitchen-worktop.jpg rather than IMG_4471. Clear file names and alt text also help your [local SEO](/services/seo) by telling search engines what each picture actually shows.

If all this feels like one job too many, that is completely fair — running a business is plenty on its own. We can arrange photography as part of a [website design](/services/website-design) project, or simply guide you on what to capture yourself. You can see the sort of results in our recent [work](/work), and our [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and clear, so you always know the cost before we start.

## The quick wins

- Real photos of your actual business build trust far better than any stock image.
- A clean lens and good natural light matter more than an expensive camera.
- Resize and rename your images before uploading to keep your site fast and easier to find.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do I really need professional photos, or will my phone do?

For most local businesses, a recent smartphone is genuinely good enough for the web, provided you get the lighting and framing right. A professional is worth it for a standout hero image or a hospitality menu, but you can build a strong, trustworthy site using your own phone photos.

### How many photos does my website actually need?

Fewer than you would think, as long as they are strong. A typical small-business site needs one standout hero image, a few of your premises and team, and a handful showing your work or products. Relevance and quality beat quantity every time.

### How much does a new business website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front — from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or a redesign is quoted per project.

### How long does it take to get a website live?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we receive your content and photos. Having your images ready — using the tips above — is one of the best ways to keep the whole project moving.

title: What to Prepare Before Hiring a Web Designer: A UK Small-Business Guide
description: Planning to hire a web designer? Here is exactly what to prepare — goals, content, budget and the questions to ask — so you get a better site, faster.
metaTitle: What to Prepare Before Hiring a Web Designer
excerpt: A simple, non-technical checklist of everything to sort out before you hire a web designer — so your project runs faster and costs less.
targetKeyword: what to prepare before hiring a web designer

cta.heading: See where your site stands
cta.sub: Book a free teardown and we will record a short, honest review of your current website — the quick wins, the fixes, no obligation.

faq.q: How much does it cost to hire a web designer in the UK?
faq.a: It varies widely, which is why fixed pricing helps you plan. Our websites are fixed-price from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the cost before you commit.

faq.q: How long does it take to build a website?
faq.a: Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks. The single biggest factor is how quickly your content — photos, wording, services and opening hours — is ready, so the preparation in this guide directly shortens the timeline.

faq.q: Do I have to write all the content myself?
faq.a: No. It helps enormously to gather the raw material — rough notes, a services list, photos and any old copy — but you do not need to be a writer. Copywriting can be part of the build, so you can hand over the basics and let us shape them.

faq.q: What do I actually need to give a web designer to get started?
faq.a: At a minimum: a clear goal for the site, your logo and brand colours, some real photos, a list of services or products, your contact details and opening hours, and any reviews you are happy to show. That is enough to begin a strong first draft.

faq.q: Do I own the website after it is built?
faq.a: With us, yes. You own your website, domain and data outright, with no lock-in. Always ask this of any designer before you sign, because some arrangements leave you unable to move your site elsewhere later.

bodyMarkdown:
Hiring a web designer can feel like a big leap, especially if it is your first time. The reassuring truth is that the result usually comes down to preparation. Knowing what to prepare before hiring a web designer means you get a better website, sooner, and often for less money — because the person building it is never left waiting on you.

You do not need to be technical. You just need to turn up with a clear idea of what you want, the raw materials to build it, and a few sensible questions. Here is exactly what to sort out first.

## Start with what you want the website to do

Before anyone touches a design, get clear on the job you want the site to do. A plumber chasing emergency call-outs needs a phone number that is impossible to miss and a page for every area they cover. A village cafe wants table bookings and a menu people can read on their phone over breakfast. A solicitor wants to look trustworthy and make it easy to request a call back.

Write down one or two main goals, a sentence each. Everything — the layout, the wording, the buttons — should push towards those goals. If you cannot say what the site is for, the design will drift, and you will feel it when the quotes come in.

## Gather your content before you start

Content is what slows most projects down. Designers can build fast, but they cannot invent your opening hours or photograph your work for you. Pull the following into one folder before you brief anyone.

If some of this is missing, do not stall the whole project. Good studios fold copywriting and photography guidance into the [web design and build](/services/website-design) itself, so you can hand over rough notes and let them shape it.

- Your logo, ideally the original file rather than a screenshot, plus any brand colours you already use.
- Real photos of your premises, team or finished work — a phone camera is fine if the light is good.
- A plain list of your services or products, with prices if you choose to show them.
- Contact details, opening hours and the areas you cover.
- Any reviews or testimonials you are happy to display, with the customer's first name.
- Whatever wording you already have — an old site, a leaflet, even notes in an email.

> Designers can build fast, but they cannot invent your opening hours or photograph your work for you.

## Know your budget and rough timeline

You will get a straighter answer from any designer if you can say what you are willing to spend. You do not need a precise figure, just a bracket. It also helps to decide when you need the site live — before a busy season, a new location opening, or a funding deadline.

Prices vary, but fixed-price studios make this easier because you know the cost up front. For context, our own websites start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month; you can see [our fixed prices](/pricing) in full. Most sites go live in three to six weeks, and how fast depends largely on how quickly your content is ready — which is exactly why the prep above matters.

## List the features you actually need

It is easy to ask for everything and pay for things you will never use. Separate what you genuinely need now from what would be nice one day.

Every build should include a simple admin panel so you can update text and images yourself without ringing anyone. Ask about that before you sign.

- A contact form and a click-to-call button that works on mobile.
- Online booking or appointments, if customers expect to reserve a slot.
- A simple shop, if you sell products directly.
- A gallery or portfolio to show off your work.
- A blog or news area — useful if being found on Google matters. If it does, plan for [local SEO](/services/seo) early rather than bolting it on later.

## Prepare a few questions for the designer

A short list of questions tells you a lot about who you are dealing with. Ask these of anyone you speak to, us included:

For the record, our answer is that **you own your site, domain and data outright**, the price is fixed, you deal with a small UK team directly, and every build includes 30 days of aftercare. Ongoing help is there if you want it through [care plans](/services/care-plans), but you are never tied in.

- Will I own the website, domain and content, or am I locked in?
- Is this a fixed price, or could it grow by the hour?
- Who will I actually speak to if something breaks?
- What happens after launch — is any support included?

## Prefer to skip the prep? We can do it for you

Being honest, not everyone has time to gather photos, write service lists and compare quotes. That is fine. Part of hiring a good studio is being able to hand over the messy bits and trust them to sort it. We can pull your content together, write the words and take the design decisions off your plate — you just approve the result.

If you would rather see where your current site stands first, we will record a short, honest review of it and walk you through what is holding it back. No sales script, no obligation.

## The quick wins

- Decide the one or two jobs your website must do before you brief anyone.
- Gather your logo, photos, services and reviews into a single folder to avoid delays.
- Know your budget bracket and go-live date so quotes come back fast and realistic.
- Ask every designer who owns the site, whether the price is fixed, and what support follows launch.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does it cost to hire a web designer in the UK?

It varies widely, which is why fixed pricing helps you plan. Our websites are fixed-price from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the cost before you commit.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks. The single biggest factor is how quickly your content — photos, wording, services and opening hours — is ready, so the preparation in this guide directly shortens the timeline.

### Do I have to write all the content myself?

No. It helps enormously to gather the raw material — rough notes, a services list, photos and any old copy — but you do not need to be a writer. Copywriting can be part of the build, so you can hand over the basics and let us shape them.

### What do I actually need to give a web designer to get started?

At a minimum: a clear goal for the site, your logo and brand colours, some real photos, a list of services or products, your contact details and opening hours, and any reviews you are happy to show. That is enough to begin a strong first draft.

### Do I own the website after it is built?

With us, yes. You own your website, domain and data outright, with no lock-in. Always ask this of any designer before you sign, because some arrangements leave you unable to move your site elsewhere later.

intro: Hiring a web designer can feel like a big leap, especially if it is your first time. The reassuring truth is that the result usually comes down to preparation. Knowing what to prepare before hiring a web designer means you get a better website, sooner, and often for less money — because the person building it is never left waiting on you.
You do not need to be technical. You just need to turn up with a clear idea of what you want, the raw materials to build it, and a few sensible questions. Here is exactly what to sort out first.

sections.h2: Start with what you want the website to do
paragraphs: Before anyone touches a design, get clear on the job you want the site to do. A plumber chasing emergency call-outs needs a phone number that is impossible to miss and a page for every area they cover. A village cafe wants table bookings and a menu people can read on their phone over breakfast. A solicitor wants to look trustworthy and make it easy to request a call back.

Write down one or two main goals, a sentence each. Everything — the layout, the wording, the buttons — should push towards those goals. If you cannot say what the site is for, the design will drift, and you will feel it when the quotes come in.

sections.h2: Gather your content before you start
paragraphs: Content is what slows most projects down. Designers can build fast, but they cannot invent your opening hours or photograph your work for you. Pull the following into one folder before you brief anyone.

If some of this is missing, do not stall the whole project. Good studios fold copywriting and photography guidance into the [web design and build](/services/website-design) itself, so you can hand over rough notes and let them shape it.
bullets: Your logo, ideally the original file rather than a screenshot, plus any brand colours you already use.
Real photos of your premises, team or finished work — a phone camera is fine if the light is good.
A plain list of your services or products, with prices if you choose to show them.
Contact details, opening hours and the areas you cover.
Any reviews or testimonials you are happy to display, with the customer's first name.
Whatever wording you already have — an old site, a leaflet, even notes in an email.
sections.pullquote: Designers can build fast, but they cannot invent your opening hours or photograph your work for you.

sections.h2: Know your budget and rough timeline
paragraphs: You will get a straighter answer from any designer if you can say what you are willing to spend. You do not need a precise figure, just a bracket. It also helps to decide when you need the site live — before a busy season, a new location opening, or a funding deadline.

Prices vary, but fixed-price studios make this easier because you know the cost up front. For context, our own websites start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month; you can see [our fixed prices](/pricing) in full. Most sites go live in three to six weeks, and how fast depends largely on how quickly your content is ready — which is exactly why the prep above matters.

sections.h2: List the features you actually need
paragraphs: It is easy to ask for everything and pay for things you will never use. Separate what you genuinely need now from what would be nice one day.
Every build should include a simple admin panel so you can update text and images yourself without ringing anyone. Ask about that before you sign.
bullets: A contact form and a click-to-call button that works on mobile.
Online booking or appointments, if customers expect to reserve a slot.
A simple shop, if you sell products directly.
A gallery or portfolio to show off your work.
A blog or news area — useful if being found on Google matters. If it does, plan for [local SEO](/services/seo) early rather than bolting it on later.

sections.h2: Prepare a few questions for the designer
paragraphs: A short list of questions tells you a lot about who you are dealing with. Ask these of anyone you speak to, us included:

For the record, our answer is that **you own your site, domain and data outright**, the price is fixed, you deal with a small UK team directly, and every build includes 30 days of aftercare. Ongoing help is there if you want it through [care plans](/services/care-plans), but you are never tied in.
bullets: Will I own the website, domain and content, or am I locked in?
Is this a fixed price, or could it grow by the hour?
Who will I actually speak to if something breaks?
What happens after launch — is any support included?

sections.h2: Prefer to skip the prep? We can do it for you
paragraphs: Being honest, not everyone has time to gather photos, write service lists and compare quotes. That is fine. Part of hiring a good studio is being able to hand over the messy bits and trust them to sort it. We can pull your content together, write the words and take the design decisions off your plate — you just approve the result.
If you would rather see where your current site stands first, we will record a short, honest review of it and walk you through what is holding it back. No sales script, no obligation.

keyTakeaways: Decide the one or two jobs your website must do before you brief anyone.
Gather your logo, photos, services and reviews into a single folder to avoid delays.
Know your budget bracket and go-live date so quotes come back fast and realistic.
Ask every designer who owns the site, whether the price is fixed, and what support follows launch.

bodyMarkdown:
Learning how to add online booking to your website is one of the highest-value jobs a small-business owner can tick off. When a customer can pick a time and confirm it in under a minute — without ringing you, waiting for a callback or playing phone tag — you win work you would otherwise lose. And a lot of those bookings land in the evening, long after you've locked up for the day.

The good news is you don't need to be technical to pull this off. Whether you run a barber's in Leeds, a physio clinic in Bristol or a boiler-servicing round across the Midlands, the process is broadly the same. Below is a plain-English walkthrough — plus an honest note on where it's worth handing the fiddly bits to someone else.

## First, decide what a booking means for you

Before you touch any software, get clear on what you're actually taking. A booking for a hair salon is a named service in a specific chair for 45 minutes. For a plumber it might be a two-hour arrival window rather than an exact slot. For a restaurant it's a table for a party size at a sitting. The tool you choose has to match how your business really runs, not the other way round.

Ten minutes with a pen here will save you hours later. Write down the answers to these before you sign up for anything:

- The services or appointment types people can book, and how long each one takes
- What they're booked against — a specific staff member, a room, a table or a piece of kit
- Your real availability, including breaks, travel time and days off
- Whether you take a deposit or full payment up front to cut no-shows
- How much notice you need, so nobody grabs a slot for twenty minutes' time

## Choose a booking tool that fits

There are two broad routes, and the right one depends on how much you already have in place.

For a solo consultant, a light scheduling app is often plenty. For a busy clinic with several staff, deposits and reminders, a proper booking system earns its keep. Pick the simplest tool that covers your list from step one — you can always upgrade later.

- **A dedicated booking app** — a general scheduler for simple one-to-one slots, or an industry tool built for salons, clinics or hospitality. You keep your calendar there and drop a booking button on your site.
- **A booking feature inside your website platform** — most modern sites can run a booking system directly, so the customer never has to leave your pages.

> Pick the simplest tool that covers your needs today. An over-featured system you never fully use is just another monthly bill.

## How to add online booking to your website, step by step

With your tool chosen, the actual set-up follows the same shape almost everywhere:

That last step matters more than any other. Most local searches happen on a phone, so if the form is awkward to tap or the confirmation never turns up, people quietly give up and ring a competitor instead.

- **Create your account and connect your calendar** so the tool blocks out times you're already busy and never double-books you.
- **Add your services, durations and prices**, along with any deposit rules and buffer time between jobs.
- **Set your real availability** — the hours and days you genuinely want to work, not the hours you feel you ought to offer.
- **Get your booking link or embed code.** Most tools give you both: a shareable link and a snippet of code that shows the calendar on your own page.
- **Put it on your website.** Add a clear booking button in your main menu, then repeat it on your homepage, service pages and contact page.
- **Test it as a customer** on your phone. Make a real booking, check the confirmation email arrives and confirm it lands in your calendar.

## Don't skip the bits that make bookings stick

A booking button is only half the job. The details below are what turn it from a novelty into a steady stream of confirmed work.

It's also worth keeping the whole thing tidy over time — updated prices, seasonal hours, new services. A little upkeep through a [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps the booking flow working while you get on with the actual work.

- **Automated reminders** by email or text — the single easiest way to cut no-shows.
- **A confirmation page and email** that reassure the customer it worked and tell them what happens next.
- **Mobile-friendly design**, because a booking form that needs pinching and zooming loses people.
- **A link from your Google Business Profile**, so someone can book straight from your Google listing.

## Do it yourself, or get it done for you

If your needs are simple and you're happy poking around settings, you can absolutely set this up yourself in an afternoon — plenty of small businesses do. But if your booking rules are complicated, your current site is fighting you, or you'd simply rather spend the afternoon on paying work, it's a job worth handing over.

We build online booking into the [websites](/services/website-design) we design, so it matches your brand and works properly on a phone from day one. Our fixed-price builds start at £477, and you can see the full range on our [pricing](/pricing) page — no hourly billing, no surprise invoices. If you already have a site and just want booking added and tested, [tell us what you're working with](/contact) and we'll take a look.

## The quick wins

- Online booking wins you work outside opening hours, when people actually search.
- Get clear on what you're booking before you choose any tool.
- Test the whole journey on your own phone before you go live.
- Reminders and a Google Business Profile link do a lot of the heavy lifting.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does it cost to add online booking to my website?

The booking software itself is often cheap — many tools have a free or low-cost monthly plan to start with. The bigger cost is the website it sits on. Our fixed-price builds start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, all with a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare included. Care plans start at £27 a month, and adding booking to an existing site is quoted per project.

### How long does it take to set up?

If you're doing it yourself with a simple tool, you can be live in an afternoon. As part of a full website build with us, most sites go live in three to six weeks, booking included. The part that takes longest is usually deciding your services, availability and deposit rules — get that clear and the technical set-up is quick.

### Do I need a new website to add booking?

Not necessarily. Most modern sites can take a booking button or an embedded calendar without a rebuild. It's only worth starting fresh if your current site is slow, hard to edit or doesn't work well on phones — in which case booking is a good reason to sort the whole thing at once.

### Will online booking work on mobile?

It should, and it must. Most local customers book from a phone, often in the evening, so test every step on your own mobile before you launch. If the form is fiddly to tap or the confirmation email doesn't arrive, people give up — a good set-up feels effortless on a small screen.

### How do I stop no-shows?

Two things do most of the work: take a deposit at the point of booking, and switch on automatic email or text reminders. Together they give people a nudge and a reason to turn up, and they cost you almost nothing once they're set up.

title: How to Set Up a Contact Form That Actually Gets Leads
description: A simple, step-by-step guide to building a contact form that turns website visitors into real enquiries — plus how to fix the leaks losing you leads.
metaTitle: How to Set Up a Contact Form That Actually Gets Leads
excerpt: Most contact forms quietly lose leads. Here's how to build one that turns visitors into real enquiries — without any technical know-how.
targetKeyword: how to set up a contact form that actually gets leads

cta.heading: See exactly what's leaking your leads
cta.sub: Book a free teardown and we'll record a short, honest review of your site — and the first fixes that will bring in more enquiries.

faq.q: What's the biggest mistake people make with contact forms?
faq.a: Asking for too much. Every extra field lowers the number of people who finish. Start with a name, one way to reach them and a short message, then gather the detail once they've replied.

faq.q: How many fields should a contact form have?
faq.a: For most local businesses, three or four is plenty. Aim to make it feel like sending a quick message rather than filling in paperwork — the shorter it looks, the more people complete it.

faq.q: How much does it cost to have you build or fix my site?
faq.a: Our websites are fixed-price and start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the cost before you begin.

faq.q: How long does it take to get a new site with a working form live?
faq.a: Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how much of your content is ready. Setting up and testing the form and its confirmations is a small part of that and something we always check before launch.

faq.q: Can I set up a good contact form myself?
faq.a: Yes. If you're comfortable with your website's editor, the steps in this guide will take you most of the way. If you'd rather not, a free teardown will show you exactly what to change first.

bodyMarkdown:
You've got a website, you've got a contact form, and yet the enquiries just aren't coming. It's one of the most common frustrations we hear from small-business owners across the UK. The good news is that learning how to set up a contact form that actually gets leads has very little to do with code and everything to do with a few practical decisions. Get those right and the same visitors you already have will start reaching out.

## Why most contact forms quietly lose leads

Here's the uncomfortable truth: a contact form can look perfectly fine and still be costing you business every single week. The problem is rarely the form itself — it's the small points of friction that pile up. A form buried at the bottom of the page. Too many nosy questions. No reassurance about what happens next. A broken submit button nobody has tested in months.

Most local searches happen on a phone, often while someone is standing in a queue or half-watching the telly. If your form is fiddly on mobile, asks for a person's life story, or leaves them wondering whether the message even sent, they simply close the tab. You never find out they were interested. That's the real cost — not the enquiries you lose, but the ones you never knew you had.

## Ask for less: the fields that earn you more replies

Every extra field you add is another reason to give up. As a rule, ask only for what you genuinely need to start a conversation — you can gather the rest once someone has replied. For most small businesses, that means keeping it to the essentials:

Skip anything you can look up or ask later. Job titles, full postal addresses and 'how did you hear about us?' can all wait. Fewer fields feels less like a form and more like firing off a quick message — and that's exactly the feeling you want.

- Name — a first name is usually enough to feel personal
- Email or phone — let people choose whichever they prefer
- A short message box — one open question beats five tick-boxes
- One optional detail that helps you quote, like 'what do you need?' or a rough budget

> Every extra field is another reason for a busy person to give up and close the tab.

## Put the form where people are ready to act

A form only works if people can find it the moment they decide to get in touch. Don't tuck it away on a single contact page and hope for the best. The strongest sites give people an obvious next step wherever interest is likely to peak — a short form in the footer of every page, a clear button after you've described a service, and a proper contact page for those who go looking. Good [website design](/services/website-design) treats the form as part of the journey, not an afterthought bolted on at the end. On mobile especially, put a tappable phone number right beside the form — some people will always prefer to call.

## Tell people what happens next

Sending a message into the void is uncomfortable. A few words of reassurance beside your form remove that hesitation. Tell people when they'll hear back — 'we usually reply the same working day' — and show a genuine thank-you message once they hit send, rather than leaving them staring at the same empty form wondering if anything happened.

Then the part that matters most: reply quickly. Someone who fills in a form is warm right now, not next Tuesday. The business that wins the work is very often just the one that answers first. If enquiries tend to land while you're on the tools or with a customer, set up an instant email alert so nothing slips through the cracks.

## Test it like a real customer

You'd be surprised how many forms quietly break — a plugin update, a changed email address, a spam filter swallowing every message. If you haven't tested yours this month, assume it might be broken and check:

Remember, too, that a form can only convert the visitors you already have. If your traffic is thin, pairing these fixes with stronger [local SEO](/services/seo) is what turns a tidy form into a steady stream of enquiries.

- Fill it in from your own phone and confirm the message actually arrives
- Look in spam and junk, then whitelist your form's sending address
- Add a simple spam guard so real enquiries aren't lost among junk
- Double-check the confirmation wording and reply-to details are correct

## Setting up a contact form that actually gets leads, without the tech headache

You can absolutely do all of this yourself, and plenty of owners do. But if you'd rather spend your time running the business, this is the sort of thing we sort out quickly. Our sites are fixed-price and include a simple contact form, an easy admin panel and thirty days of aftercare, so you always know the cost up front — have a look at our [pricing](/pricing) before you commit to anything.

If you're not sure what's letting your current form down, a free teardown is the easiest place to start. It's a short, screen-recorded review of your site where we point out exactly what's leaking leads and what to fix first — no jargon, no obligation. Get in [touch](/contact) and we'll take a proper look.

## The quick wins

- Ask for the fewest fields possible — a name, one contact detail and a short message is usually enough.
- Put a clear form or call button wherever interest peaks, not just on a hidden contact page.
- Reassure people what happens next, then reply fast — the business that answers first usually wins the work.
- Test your form every month, because broken forms fail silently.

## Frequently asked questions

### What's the biggest mistake people make with contact forms?

Asking for too much. Every extra field lowers the number of people who finish. Start with a name, one way to reach them and a short message, then gather the detail once they've replied.

### How many fields should a contact form have?

For most local businesses, three or four is plenty. Aim to make it feel like sending a quick message rather than filling in paperwork — the shorter it looks, the more people complete it.

### How much does it cost to have you build or fix my site?

Our websites are fixed-price and start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the cost before you begin.

### How long does it take to get a new site with a working form live?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how much of your content is ready. Setting up and testing the form and its confirmations is a small part of that and something we always check before launch.

### Can I set up a good contact form myself?

Yes. If you're comfortable with your website's editor, the steps in this guide will take you most of the way. If you'd rather not, a free teardown will show you exactly what to change first.

Good [website design](/services/website-design) treats the form as part of the journey, not an afterthought bolted on at the end. On mobile especially, put a tappable phone number right beside the form — some people will always prefer to call.

sections.h2: Tell people what happens next
paragraphs: Sending a message into the void is uncomfortable. A few words of reassurance beside your form remove that hesitation. Tell people when they'll hear back — 'we usually reply the same working day' — and show a genuine thank-you message once they hit send, rather than leaving them staring at the same empty form wondering if anything happened.

Then the part that matters most: reply quickly. Someone who fills in a form is warm right now, not next Tuesday. The business that wins the work is very often just the one that answers first. If enquiries tend to land while you're on the tools or with a customer, set up an instant email alert so nothing slips through the cracks.

sections.h2: Test it like a real customer
paragraphs: You'd be surprised how many forms quietly break — a plugin update, a changed email address, a spam filter swallowing every message. If you haven't tested yours this month, assume it might be broken and check:

Remember, too, that a form can only convert the visitors you already have. If your traffic is thin, pairing these fixes with stronger [local SEO](/services/seo) is what turns a tidy form into a steady stream of enquiries.
bullets: Fill it in from your own phone and confirm the message actually arrives
Look in spam and junk, then whitelist your form's sending address
Add a simple spam guard so real enquiries aren't lost among junk
Double-check the confirmation wording and reply-to details are correct

sections.h2: Setting up a contact form that actually gets leads, without the tech headache
paragraphs: You can absolutely do all of this yourself, and plenty of owners do. But if you'd rather spend your time running the business, this is the sort of thing we sort out quickly. Our sites are fixed-price and include a simple contact form, an easy admin panel and thirty days of aftercare, so you always know the cost up front — have a look at our [pricing](/pricing) before you commit to anything.

If you're not sure what's letting your current form down, a free teardown is the easiest place to start. It's a short, screen-recorded review of your site where we point out exactly what's leaking leads and what to fix first — no jargon, no obligation. Get in [touch](/contact) and we'll take a proper look.

keyTakeaways: Ask for the fewest fields possible — a name, one contact detail and a short message is usually enough.
Put a clear form or call button wherever interest peaks, not just on a hidden contact page.
Reassure people what happens next, then reply fast — the business that answers first usually wins the work.
Test your form every month, because broken forms fail silently.

bodyMarkdown:
We have all done it. You tap a link, stare at a blank white screen, and by the time the page finally appears you have already hit back and moved on to the next result. Your customers behave exactly the same way on your site. That is why learning how to make your website load faster is one of the highest-value jobs on your to-do list, and the good news is that most of it needs no coding at all.

This is a plain-English guide for busy small-business owners, not developers. We will walk through what actually slows a site down, the fixes you can make yourself this week, and the point where it makes sense to hand it over. No jargon, no guilt trips.

## Why a slow website quietly costs you

Most people find local businesses on their phone, often on patchy mobile data while they are standing on the high street or sitting on a bus. If your homepage takes five or six seconds to appear, a real chunk of those visitors give up before they ever see what you offer or how to book. Speed is not only about patience, though. It also feeds into your [SEO](/services/seo): Google treats page experience as one of many ranking signals, so a faster site can help you turn up in search as well as win the customer once you are there.

## How to make your website load faster: the quick wins

If you only have an afternoon, start here. These are the changes that give you the biggest improvement for the least effort:

Work down that list in order and you will feel the difference. Below, we dig into the ones that matter most.

- **Compress your images** before uploading them — on most small-business sites this single step fixes the majority of the problem.
- **Remove plugins, sliders and widgets** you are not really using; each one adds weight.
- **Turn on caching** so returning visitors are served a stored copy instead of rebuilding the page every time.
- **Cut down on fonts** — two styles is plenty; a dozen is a real drag.
- **Lazy-load images** below the fold so they load only as someone scrolls to them.
- **Test on a real phone** using mobile data, not just the fast wifi in your office.

## Fix your images first

Images are the number one reason small-business sites feel sluggish. A photo taken straight off a modern phone can be 4,000 pixels wide and several megabytes in size, yet it might only ever be shown in a small box on screen. The browser still has to download the whole thing. Resize each image to the width it actually appears at, run it through a free tool like Squoosh or TinyPNG to shrink the file, and save it in a modern format such as WebP where you can. You will often cut the file size dramatically with no visible drop in quality.

> Nine times out of ten, an oversized image is the thing making a small-business website crawl.

## Trim the extras slowing you down

Every plugin, chat bubble, booking widget and tracking script your site loads is another request the browser has to fetch, often from someone else's server. It all adds up. Be honest about what earns its place:

You do not need to strip your site back to nothing. You just need every extra to be pulling its weight.

- **Plugins and add-ons** you installed once and forgot — deactivate and delete them.
- **Social media feeds** embedded on the homepage, which are notoriously heavy.
- **Duplicate tracking tags** quietly doing the same job twice.
- **Autoplay video backgrounds**, which can load megabytes before anything else appears.

## Sort out hosting and caching

Cheap, overcrowded shared hosting is a common hidden cause of a slow site — your website is sharing a tired server with hundreds of others. Hosting based in or near the UK, close to your customers, shortens the distance your data has to travel. On top of that, caching stores a ready-made version of each page so it does not have to be rebuilt on every visit, and a content delivery network serves your images from a location near each visitor. Most decent hosts offer both, and it is often just a setting to switch on. Keeping all of this maintained over time is exactly what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for.

## When to do it yourself, and when to call someone

Plenty of the steps above are a comfortable weekend job, and we would rather you save your money and try them first. But if your site is built on a heavy, dated theme, or you have worked through the quick wins and it is still slow, the problem is usually baked into how the site was built — and no amount of tidying will fully fix that. That is when a rebuild on solid foundations pays for itself. Our [website design](/services/website-design) work is fixed-price so you know the cost up front, you can see exactly what that looks like on our [pricing](/pricing) page, and every site we build is fast by default. If you would rather someone simply take a look and tell you honestly what is going on, that is what a free teardown is for.

## The quick wins

- Oversized images are the most common cause of a slow website — compress and resize them before anything else.
- Turning on caching and choosing UK-based hosting gives a big speed boost for very little effort.
- Test your site on a real phone using mobile data, not just the fast wifi in your office.
- If the quick wins don't help, the problem is usually in how the site was built.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does it cost to make my website load faster?

If you do it yourself, the tools mentioned above are free, so the main cost is your time. If you would like us to handle it, a speed-focused tune-up is quoted per project once we have seen your site, and ongoing upkeep starts at £27 a month. If your current site is beyond a tune-up, a fresh build is fixed-price from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete — no hourly billing and no surprises.

### How long does it take to speed up a website?

The quick wins — compressing images, removing unused plugins, switching on caching — can often be done in a single afternoon. If the honest answer is a full rebuild on faster foundations, most of our sites go live within three to six weeks.

### Will a faster website improve my Google ranking?

It can help. Google uses page experience, including load speed, as one of many ranking signals, so a quicker site supports your wider SEO efforts. We would never promise a specific position, though — anyone who guarantees you the top spot is not being straight with you.

### How do I check how fast my website is?

Use a free tool like Google PageSpeed Insights, which grades your site and lists what is slowing it down. Just as important, open your own site on your phone using mobile data and time how long it takes to become usable.

### What usually makes a small-business website slow?

In our experience the usual suspects are huge uncompressed images, too many plugins and widgets, heavy page builders or themes, and cheap overcrowded hosting. Images are almost always the biggest single factor.
