bodyMarkdown:
If you run a small business, you probably don't think about website security until something breaks. But learning how to keep your website secure with SSL, backups and updates is far easier than it sounds — and it protects the one thing that brings you enquiries. You don't need to be technical. You need three habits and a bit of routine.

This guide walks through the three that matter most for a local business site: the padlock that tells visitors they're safe, the safety net that gets you back online after a mistake, and the updates that quietly close the doors attackers walk through. We'll keep it plain, and we'll be honest at the end about when it's worth handing the whole lot to someone else.

## Start with SSL: the padlock in the address bar

SSL is the technology behind the little padlock you see in your browser's address bar. When a site has it, the web address starts with **https** instead of http, and everything sent between your visitor and your site — a contact form, a booking, an enquiry — is encrypted. Without it, modern browsers show a blunt 'Not secure' warning right where people decide whether to trust you.

For a local business that mostly means one thing: lost enquiries. Someone searching for a plumber in Leeds or a dentist in Bristol isn't going to hand their phone number to a site the browser is warning them about. The good news is that SSL is usually free through your hosting, and a proper [website build](/services/website-design) should have it switched on and forcing https from day one.

It matters for Google too. HTTPS is a small but real ranking signal, so it quietly supports your [local SEO](/services/seo) alongside everything else you do. If your padlock is missing, or your certificate has quietly expired, that's the first thing to put right.

## Back up your site before you ever need to

A backup is a saved copy of your website you can restore if something goes wrong — and eventually something does. A plugin update breaks a page, a login gets compromised, or someone on your team deletes the wrong thing. With a recent backup, that's a ten-minute fix. Without one, it can mean rebuilding your site from scratch.

The most common problem we see in a teardown isn't the absence of backups — it's a business that assumes the host is handling it and has never actually checked. Five minutes confirming where your backups live, and how you'd restore one, is time very well spent.

- Back up the whole site — files and database — not just one or the other.
- Keep backups automatic and daily, or at least weekly, so nothing depends on you remembering.
- Store copies somewhere separate from your hosting, so one server problem can't take down both the site and its backup.
- Test a restore once, so you know it works before a real emergency, not during one.
- Keep a few weeks of history rather than only last night's copy, in case a problem goes unnoticed for a while.

## Keep everything updated — this is where most sites get caught

Most small-business sites run on software: a content system such as WordPress, a theme, and a handful of plugins. Each of those gets updates, and a lot of those updates exist to close security holes that are already public knowledge. Skip them for months and you're leaving known, published doors unlocked — which is exactly what automated attacks go looking for.

Updating is mostly straightforward, but it isn't risk-free: now and then an update clashes with something and breaks a page, which is precisely why the backup habit comes first. Work in a sensible order — back up, update, then check the site still works, paying special attention to your contact form and any booking or payment steps.

## How to keep your website secure without becoming an expert

You don't need to do any of this daily. A short monthly routine covers most small-business sites and takes less time than a coffee break. Check three things: that your padlock is still there and your SSL certificate hasn't lapsed, that a recent backup exists and you know how to restore it, and that your core software, theme and plugins are up to date. Click through your key pages afterwards and you've handled the vast majority of what actually goes wrong.

Two extra habits make a real difference for very little effort. Use strong, unique passwords with two-factor login on your admin account, and remove any plugins or user accounts you no longer use. Every unused thing sitting on your site is one more thing that can be exploited.

> Security for a small business isn't a project — it's a fifteen-minute habit you repeat once a month.

## When it's worth handing it to someone else

Plenty of owners run this routine themselves, and if that's you, brilliant. But if logging into a hosting panel makes your stomach drop, or you'd simply rather spend that time on actual customers, this is exactly the sort of job worth handing over. It's low-glamour, easy to forget, and expensive to ignore.

That's what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for — someone keeping your SSL valid, your backups running and your software updated in the background, so you never have to think about it. Care plans start from £27 a month, and you can see how everything fits together on our [pricing page](/pricing). Either way, you own your site, domain and data — there's no lock-in and no hourly surprises.

Not sure where your own site stands on any of this? That's exactly what a free teardown is for: a short screen-recorded walkthrough where we point out what's solid and what needs attention.

## The quick wins

- SSL (the https padlock) encrypts visitor data and reassures customers — and it's usually free through your host.
- Automatic, off-site, tested backups turn a disaster into a ten-minute fix.
- Updating your core software, theme and plugins closes known security holes that automated attacks rely on.
- A fifteen-minute monthly check covers most small-business sites.
- A care plan or a free teardown is there for when you'd rather not do it yourself.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do I really need SSL if I only have a simple brochure website?

Yes. Even without a shop or logins, browsers now label any http site as 'Not secure', and that warning sits right next to your business name at the exact moment a potential customer is deciding whether to trust you. SSL is usually free through your hosting, so there's no good reason to go without it.

### How often should I back up my website?

For most small-business sites, a daily or at least weekly automatic backup, stored separately from your hosting, is plenty. The key is that it's automatic, off-site and tested — a backup you've never tried to restore is really just a hopeful guess.

### How much does it cost to keep a website secure and maintained?

SSL is typically free through your host, so the main ongoing cost is the time to keep things updated and backed up. If you'd rather not do that yourself, our care plans start from £27 a month and cover SSL, backups, updates and small fixes. New websites are fixed-price from £477, so you always know the cost up front — no hourly billing and no surprises.

### How long does it take to sort out security on an existing site?

Fixing the basics — installing SSL, setting up automatic backups and running any outstanding updates — is usually a same-day job on an existing site, not a rebuild. If you're having a new site built with us, security is included from the start, and most builds go live in three to six weeks.

### What happens if my website gets hacked or breaks after an update?

This is exactly why backups matter. With a recent, tested backup you can usually restore the site to how it was in minutes, rather than rebuilding it. If you're on a care plan, keeping those backups current and getting you back online quickly is part of what you're paying for.

Automatic, off-site, tested backups turn a disaster into a ten-minute fix.
Updating your core software, theme and plugins closes known security holes that automated attacks rely on.
A fifteen-minute monthly check covers most small-business sites.
A care plan or a free teardown is there for when you'd rather not do it yourself.

bodyMarkdown:
Getting people onto your website is only half the job. The harder and far more valuable question is how to turn website visitors into paying customers once they arrive. Plenty of UK small businesses pay for ads or rank well locally, then watch most of that traffic click away without ever picking up the phone or filling in a form. The good news is that the fixes are rarely technical, and you can make most of them yourself this week.

We build and optimise sites for a living, so we will be honest about what moves the needle and what is a waste of your time. This guide walks through the practical steps, in plain English, whether you run a plumbing round in Leeds, a salon in Cardiff or a small accountancy practice in Reading.

## Decide what a paying customer actually does

A conversion is simply the moment a visitor does the one thing you most want them to do. For a plumber that might be a phone call. For a dentist, a booked consultation. For a bookkeeper, a tidy enquiry through a form. Write that single action down before you touch anything else, because you cannot improve a result you have not defined. Most sites underperform not because they look bad, but because they quietly ask the visitor to do nothing in particular.

Pick one primary action per page and make everything else secondary. A visitor handed five equal options usually takes none of them.

## Make your offer clear in the first five seconds

When someone lands on your site, they are silently asking three questions: what do you do, do you do it near me, and can I trust you. If your homepage does not answer all three before they scroll, most people leave. Say plainly what you offer, where you work and who you help. A heading like "Emergency electricians covering South Manchester, same-day callouts" beats a vague "Welcome to our website" every time.

This is where clear [website design and build](/services/website-design) earns its keep. Good design is not decoration. It is making the right thing obvious and the next step impossible to miss.

## What actually turns website visitors into paying customers

Once the basics are in place, a handful of proven changes do most of the heavy lifting. None of them need a developer, and you can work through the lot in an afternoon.

- **A clear, repeated call to action.** Put the same simple next step — call, book or enquire — near the top, in the middle and at the foot of every page.
- **Contact details people can see.** A phone number in the header and a short form beat a lone "Contact" tab buried behind a menu.
- **Real photos and honest prices.** A from-price and pictures of your team, van or premises filter out time-wasters and build instant trust.
- **Fast loading on mobile.** Most local searches happen on a phone, and a site that takes an age to load loses the sale before the page even appears.
- **Genuine reviews near the action.** A few honest words from a local customer, placed right beside your enquiry button, reassure the nervous.

## Remove the friction between interested and in touch

Every extra step between wanting to contact you and actually doing it costs you customers. Long forms are the worst offender. Ask for a name, a way to reach them and a line about the job, and nothing more. You can always ask the rest once they reply.

Make it effortless on a phone: a tap-to-call number, a form that fits the screen, and no pinching or zooming to read your prices. If you serve several areas or trades, a dedicated [industry page](/industries) helps the right people feel you are talking directly to them.

## Build trust so saying yes feels safe

People do not buy from a business they are unsure about. Small signals of trust do a surprising amount of work: a real address, a photo of a real person, clear pricing, and reviews that sound like actual customers rather than marketing. Being upfront about cost, in particular, removes the biggest unspoken worry a visitor has.

Good [copywriting and local SEO](/services/seo) work together here. The words on the page do the reassuring, and being found for the right local searches brings you people who are already halfway to buying.

> Most visitors do not need convincing that they have a problem. They need reassuring that you are the safe, obvious choice to fix it.

## Test small changes, and know when to hand it over

You do not need fancy tools to improve. Change one thing at a time and watch what happens: move the phone number up, shorten the form, add a review beside the button. Then ask a friend or a customer to find your prices and book, and watch where they hesitate. Those pauses are your to-do list.

If the tinkering is eating time you would rather spend serving customers, this is the point where getting help pays for itself. We build fixed-price websites designed to turn visitors into enquiries from £477, most going live in three to six weeks, and a [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month keeps everything fast, safe and up to date. You can [see exactly what is included and what it costs](/pricing), or book a free teardown for honest feedback on where your current site is leaking business.

## The quick wins

- Define the one action you want each page to drive before changing anything else.
- Answer what, where and can-I-trust-you above the fold, or most visitors leave.
- Shorter forms, visible contact details and honest prices convert better than clever design.
- Fix the page before you pay to send it more traffic.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does it cost to get a website that converts?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing: from £477 for Starter, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or a redesign of your existing site is quoted per project once we have seen what you are working with.

### How long does it take?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we get your content, photos and feedback. Small conversion fixes to an existing site — clearer buttons, a shorter form, sharper copy — can often be done far quicker than a full build.

### How do I know if my website is actually converting visitors into customers?

Pick your one main action, then keep a simple tally of how many people take it — calls, form enquiries or bookings — against how many visitors you get. If lots of people arrive but few get in touch, your traffic is fine and the problem is on the page. That gap is exactly what a free teardown is built to find.

### Do I need paid ads to get more paying customers?

Not necessarily. Plenty of local businesses win steady work from a well-filled Google Business Profile, genuine reviews and a site built to convert the visitors they already get. Ads can help once the fundamentals are right, but paying to send more people to a page that does not convert just loses money faster.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out quick wins and honest problems in plain English. There is no cost and no obligation, and you keep the recording either way.

bodyMarkdown:
If you have looked into advertising your business online, someone has probably told you to build a landing page. It gets mentioned a lot and explained rarely. So this guide answers it plainly: what is a landing page, and when do you need one? No jargon, no marketing waffle — just what it is and whether it is worth your money.

The short version is that a landing page is not your homepage, and it is not your whole website. It is a single page with one job. Once that idea clicks, deciding whether you need one gets a lot easier — and below we walk through the exact moments when a landing page pays for itself.

## What a landing page actually is

A landing page is a single web page built around one goal. That goal is usually a specific action you want the visitor to take: book an appointment, request a quote, call you, or buy one thing. The name is literal — it is the page people land on after clicking a link, whether that link sits in a Google advert, an email, an Instagram post, or a search result.

Say you run a boiler-repair business in Leeds and you are paying for Google Ads on the term emergency boiler repair. The person clicking is not in the mood to browse. They want a phone number and the reassurance that you can help today. A good landing page gives them exactly that and nothing else — no menu, no blog, no list of your fifteen other services. One message, one action.

## How a landing page differs from your homepage

Your homepage is a reception desk. Its job is to greet everyone and send them in the right direction — services here, about us there, contact over there. That is exactly what it should do, because homepage visitors arrive with all sorts of intentions. A landing page is the opposite. It is a corridor with one door at the end.

- **One goal, not many.** The whole page pushes towards a single action instead of offering ten routes.
- **Fewer distractions.** Landing pages often drop the main menu, so there is nowhere to wander off to.
- **A matched message.** The headline echoes the advert or link that brought the visitor in, so the page feels like the right place to be.
- **Easy to measure.** Because there is one action, you can see precisely how many people took it and what each enquiry cost you.

## When do you actually need a landing page?

Here is the honest answer: you need a landing page when you are sending a specific group of people to do a specific thing. Most often that means you are spending money to get them there. If any of the following sound like you, a landing page will almost certainly earn its keep.

- **You are running paid ads.** Sending paid clicks to your homepage is like paying for a taxi and getting out three streets early. A focused page converts far more of that traffic.
- **You are promoting one offer.** A seasonal deal, a new treatment, an open day — anything with a single, clear ask.
- **You are targeting one service or one area.** A page just for kitchen fitting in Bristol will beat a general services page for that search, whatever your [industry](/industries).
- **You are collecting leads.** A downloadable guide, a quote form, or a waiting list all work best on a page with no competing links.
- **You are testing a new idea.** Before you build out a whole new service, a landing page tells you whether anyone actually wants it.

> A landing page turns a click you paid for into a customer you can keep.

## What makes a landing page work

A landing page is simple, but simple is not the same as easy. The ones that perform tend to share the same handful of ingredients.

If you also want the page to pull in visitors from Google for free, not just from ads, it needs to be written and structured for search. That is where a landing page and [local SEO](/services/seo) start working together — the same focused page can rank for a specific service in a specific town while it converts your paid traffic.

- **A headline that matches the click.** If your advert said same-day boiler repair, the page should say it back within the first second.
- **One clear call to action, repeated.** Ask for the same thing near the top and again at the bottom. Do not make people hunt.
- **Proof that you are real.** Genuine reviews, photos of your actual work, and any accreditations that matter in your trade.
- **Fast loading on a phone.** Most local searches happen on a mobile, and a slow page loses people before they read a word.
- **Short and focused.** Cut anything that does not move the visitor towards that one action.

## Do you need a landing page — or a better website?

Here is the part a lot of agencies skip. Sometimes the honest answer is that you do not need a landing page at all. If your whole site looks dated and buries your phone number, a single slick landing page is a plaster over a broken leg. You would get more from fixing the [website design and build](/services/website-design) underneath everything.

For most small businesses the smart move is a strong homepage that does its job, plus one or two landing pages for the campaigns that matter. Every build we do is fixed-price, so you know the cost before we start — you can see the full [pricing](/pricing) up front — and you own the site, the domain and the data at the end of it. No lock-in, no surprises.

Not sure which camp you are in? That is exactly what a free teardown is for. Send us your current site and we will [record a short review](/contact) of what is working, what is quietly costing you leads, and whether a landing page would genuinely help.

## The quick wins

- A landing page is a single page with one job: to get the visitor to take one specific action.
- Your homepage points people everywhere; a landing page points them at one thing.
- You need one most when you are paying for traffic — ads, a single promotion, or lead collection.
- Sometimes a stronger homepage beats a new landing page. Be honest about which you actually need.

## Frequently asked questions

### What is the difference between a landing page and a website?

A website is a collection of pages that together tell your whole story — home, services, about, contact and so on. A landing page is a single page with one focused goal, usually reached from an advert or a link. You can have a landing page as part of a website, or as a standalone page built for one specific campaign.

### Do I need a landing page if I already have a website?

Not always. If your homepage already turns visitors into enquiries, you may not need one yet. But the moment you start paying for ads or pushing a single offer, a dedicated landing page will usually convert far better than sending that traffic to a busy homepage.

### How much does a landing page cost?

It depends on the scope, so we quote landing pages per project rather than off a menu, and everything we do is fixed-price so you know the figure before we start. For reference, our full website builds start at £477 for a Starter site, and care plans to keep things updated start at £27 a month. You own the site, domain and data either way.

### How long does it take to build a landing page?

A single landing page is usually quicker than a full website. For context, most of our full sites go live within three to six weeks, and a focused one-page build typically lands well inside that. The exact timeline depends mostly on how quickly we get your content and sign-off.

### Can I build a landing page myself?

You can, with tools like Wix or Squarespace, and for a quick test that is a fair place to start. The catch is that the details that make a page convert — a matched headline, a fast mobile load, one obvious action — are easy to get wrong. If the page is spending your ad budget, it is usually worth getting it built properly.

The second is telling everyone the site is live before testing the contact form, so enquiries quietly vanish. Both are easy to avoid if you follow the steps above.

faq.q: What is a free teardown?
faq.a: It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out what is working, what is costing you enquiries and what we would fix first. There is no charge and no obligation. It is the easiest way to see how your site measures up against this checklist without lifting a finger yourself. bodyMarkdown:
Launching a website feels a bit like opening a new shop. You want the door to open smoothly, the lights on, the prices clear and someone ready to answer the phone. Miss a detail and the first visitor notices before you do. This is the ultimate website launch checklist for small businesses, written for the owner who is not technical and does not want to be.

Work through it in order and you will go live with confidence. Skip a step and you might be chasing broken links or a missing contact form on day one, usually while you are meant to be running your actual business. If any of it makes your eyes glaze over, that is fine, this is exactly the sort of thing we do every week.

## Get the foundations right before you touch design

Before you fuss over colours or fonts, sort the plumbing. These are the things that are painful to change later and easy to get right now. Most owners rush past them because they feel boring, then pay for it when an email bounces or the domain renewal lapses.

Make sure every one of these is in your own name and under your own login. Ownership is the single biggest thing people get wrong, and it is why we build every site so that you own your site, domain and data with no lock-in.

- Register your domain in your business name, not your web person's, and note the renewal date
- Set up business email on your domain (you@yourbusiness.co.uk always beats a Gmail address on your business cards)
- Confirm you have logins for your hosting, domain and any tools, and store them somewhere safe
- Decide on one clear goal for the site: phone calls, bookings, quote requests or online sales
- Write your core pages before design starts so the layout fits real words, not placeholder text

## Check every page reads well and works

Now for the content itself. Read every page out loud, ideally on your phone, because most local searches happen on a mobile and that is how your customers will see it. If a sentence trips you up when you say it, it will trip up a reader too.

Check the practical details a customer actually needs: your phone number, opening hours, the areas you cover and a genuine reason to choose you. A plumber in Leeds and a wedding photographer in Bath need very different pages, and good [copywriting and website design](/services/website-design) makes that difference obvious in the first few seconds.

- Business name, phone number and address match everywhere, letter for letter
- Every button and link goes where it should, with no dead ends
- The contact form actually sends, and you have tested it from a real inbox
- Images are your own or properly licensed, and none are stretched or blurry
- Spelling and grammar checked by a second pair of eyes, not just you

> If a sentence trips you up when you say it out loud, it will trip up your customer too.

## Set up the technical bits that Google cares about

This is the part owners dread, but the essentials are simpler than they sound. You do not need to understand how a car engine works to check the oil, and you do not need to be a developer to tick these off. Get them in place and you give yourself a fair chance of being found.

A fast, secure, findable site is the groundwork for every bit of [SEO and local SEO](/services/seo) that follows. Launch without it and you are effectively opening with the lights off.

- The padlock (SSL certificate) shows in the browser, so the site loads as https not http
- Pages load quickly, especially the homepage, on a normal phone signal rather than office wifi
- Page titles and descriptions are written for each page, not left blank or duplicated
- The site is submitted to Google Search Console so Google knows it exists
- A basic analytics tool is installed so you can see what visitors actually do

## Connect your Google Business Profile

For a local business, your Google Business Profile often does more heavy lifting than the website itself. It is the box that appears on the right when someone searches your name, and the map pin they tap when they want directions or your number.

Claim it, fill it in completely and make sure the details match your website exactly. Add real photos, your opening hours and the services you offer. If you want a hand tying your profile and site together, that is a core part of what we do for [local businesses across the UK](/industries).

## Do a final walk-through, then go live

Before you announce anything, do one calm walk-through as if you were a first-time customer. Open the site on a phone and a laptop. Tap through every page. Fill in the form. Call the number to check it rings the right phone. It is the digital version of walking the shop floor before you unlock the door.

Once it is live, tell people. Update your social profiles, your email signature and any directory listings so they point to the new address. And keep an eye on things for the first few weeks, which is exactly why every build we do includes 30 days aftercare and the option of a [simple care plan](/services/care-plans) to keep it healthy after that.

If reading this checklist has quietly convinced you that you would rather not do it all yourself, that is a perfectly sensible conclusion. We build fixed-price websites for UK small businesses so you know the cost up front, most go live in three to six weeks, and you can see the [full pricing here](/pricing). Either way, use this list, launch well, and give your new site the opening day it deserves.

## The quick wins

- Sort ownership and email before anything else, they are painful to fix later
- Test the contact form from a real inbox before you tell anyone the site is live
- Secure, fast and findable beats pretty every single time

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a small business website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price so you know the cost before we start. They begin at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, with no hourly billing and no surprise invoices. Care plans to keep everything running start at **£27 a month**, and SEO or a full redesign is quoted per project. You can see the [full breakdown on our pricing page](/pricing).

### How long does it take to launch a website?

Most of our sites go live within **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly we get your content and photos. The checklist steps above run alongside the build, so nothing is left to the last minute. Every build also includes 30 days of aftercare so you are not on your own the moment it goes live.

### Can I launch a website myself, or do I need a professional?

You can absolutely do it yourself with a checklist like this one, and plenty of owners do. The honest answer is that it takes time and attention you might rather spend running your business. If you would rather hand it over, we handle the whole thing, and you still own your site, domain and data at the end with no lock-in.

### What is the most common mistake when launching a website?

Two things, really. The first is not owning your own domain and email, which becomes a headache the day you part ways with whoever set it up. The second is telling everyone the site is live before testing the contact form, so enquiries quietly vanish. Both are easy to avoid if you follow the steps above.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out what is working, what is costing you enquiries and what we would fix first. There is no charge and no obligation. It is the easiest way to see how your site measures up against this checklist without lifting a finger yourself. Just [get in touch](/contact) and we will record one for you.

title: Why Every Dental Practice Needs a Modern Website
description: See why every dental practice needs a modern website to win new patients — what a great one does, what it costs, and how to fix yours. Book a free teardown.

excerpt: Your website is often a patient's first impression of your practice — here's why a modern one turns online searchers into booked appointments.
targetKeyword: why every dental practice needs a modern website

cta.heading: See what's costing you patients
cta.sub: Book a free teardown and we'll record a short, honest review of your current dental website — and exactly what to fix first.

bodyMarkdown:
Ask yourself one honest question: when someone in your town searches for a new dentist at nine in the evening on their phone, what do they find? For a growing number of practices, the answer is a slow, dated site that hasn't been touched in years — or worse, just a Facebook page. That is exactly why every dental practice needs a modern website: not as a vanity project, but as the tool that turns a curious searcher into a booked patient.

Most people choose a dentist much the way they choose a restaurant — online, on mobile, in a few minutes. Your website is doing the selling whether you've invested in it or not. Here is why it matters, and what a modern one should actually do for you.

## Your website is your busiest team member

Think about how a new patient really finds you. They rarely walk past and pop in. They pull out their phone, search, skim two or three practices, read a handful of reviews and pick one — often late in the evening when your reception has long since gone home. Your website is the only member of your team working at that moment, and it either wins the booking or waves it goodbye. Most of that happens on mobile, so a site that's fiddly to tap, slow to load or hard to read on a small screen is quietly costing you patients you never knew searched for you.

## Why every dental practice needs a modern website

Dentistry is personal, and often a little nerve-wracking. People are trusting you with their health and their appearance, so they look for reassurance long before they pick up the phone. A clean, current, well-written website does that reassuring for you: it signals a professional, well-run practice that takes care over the details. A tired one does the opposite. Fair or not, **patients judge the quality of your clinical care by the quality of your website** — if it looks like it was built in 2012 and never touched again, they assume the same of your surgery. With NHS lists stretched and more people paying privately, the practices that present themselves well online are the ones capturing that demand.

## What a modern dental website actually does

A modern dental website isn't about flashy design for its own sake. It's about removing every reason a patient might have to click away. At a minimum, a good one should:

Get those right and your site stops being a digital brochure and starts being a **booking engine**. That is the difference a considered [website design and build](/services/website-design) makes.

- Load quickly and look right on any phone, tablet or laptop
- Let patients book or request an appointment without having to ring during opening hours
- Answer the questions you get asked every day — treatments, costs, nervous patients, parking and access
- Show real photos of your practice and team rather than generic stock images
- Make your patient reviews and GDC registration easy to find
- Help you appear on Google when someone nearby searches for a dentist

> A patient who can't book on their phone at nine in the evening will simply book with the practice down the road who let them.

## Getting found by patients nearby

A beautiful website nobody can find won't fill your book. Most people looking for a dentist search with local intent — 'dentist near me', 'emergency dentist' plus their town, 'Invisalign' plus their area — and Google decides who to show. Getting into those results comes down to a well-optimised site paired with a complete, active Google Business Profile: accurate opening hours, real photos and a steady trickle of genuine reviews. Strong [local SEO](/services/seo) is what puts you in front of people at the exact moment they've decided to act, rather than leaving it to whichever competitor happens to rank above you. For a local practice, showing up in your own town is usually worth far more than ranking nationally for anything.

## What it costs and how to start

The good news is that a professional dental website no longer needs a five-figure budget or a six-month project. We work to fixed prices, so you know the cost before you commit — our websites start at £477, with most practices choosing a middle package for booking, treatment pages and proper local SEO. You can see the full [fixed-price packages](/pricing) and pick what fits. Once you're live, a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month keeps everything updated, secure and backed up, so your site never drifts back into looking dated. You own your site, domain and data throughout — no lock-in, no hourly billing — and most builds go live in three to six weeks. If you'd like an honest, no-obligation look at where your current site is letting patients slip away, book a free teardown and we'll record a short review of exactly what to fix first.

## The quick wins

- Patients choose dentists online, on mobile and often out of hours — your website makes the first impression before you ever do.
- A modern site should load fast, let people book without phoning and answer the questions patients actually ask.
- Being found matters as much as looking good: local SEO and an active Google Business Profile put you in front of nearby searchers.
- You don't need a huge budget — fixed-price builds start at £477 and most go live in three to six weeks.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do busy dental practices still need a modern website?

Yes. Waiting lists and word of mouth can dry up faster than you'd expect, and new patients — especially private ones — almost always check you online first. A modern website protects you against quieter spells and makes sure the patients searching right now choose you rather than a competitor.

### How much does a dental website cost?

We work to fixed prices with no hourly billing. Websites start at £477 (Starter), £1,170 (Growth) and £2,370 (Complete), and care plans start at £27 a month. SEO projects and redesigns are quoted per project, so [tell us what you need](/contact) and we'll give you a firm figure up front.

### How long does it take to build a dental website?

Most practices go live within three to six weeks, depending on how much content and photography is ready. Every build includes a simple admin panel so your team can make changes, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

### Can patients book appointments through the site?

Yes. We can add online booking or a simple appointment-request form so patients can get in touch at any hour, not just when reception is open. It takes pressure off your phone lines and captures enquiries you'd otherwise miss.

### Will a new website help me rank on Google?

A well-built, fast site paired with an active Google Business Profile gives you the best possible chance of showing up for local searches. We can't promise a specific position — nobody honestly can — but strong local SEO consistently puts practices in front of nearby patients.

bodyMarkdown:
Your practice could have the gentlest dentist in town and still lose patients before anyone ever calls. Most people choose a dentist the way they choose everything now: they land on your website, decide in seconds whether they trust you, and either book or bounce. Getting that decision to go your way comes down to a handful of dental website features that turn visitors into patients — not clever design tricks, just the right things in the right place.

The good news is that none of them are expensive or complicated. Below are the features that do the heavy lifting, why they matter, and how to check whether your current site already has them.

## The dental website features that turn visitors into patients

A dental website has one job: convince someone with toothache, or a parent booking a check-up, that your practice is the safe, easy choice. It does that through a small set of features working together. Here is the shortlist a good [dental website](/services/website-design) needs before anything else.

Miss two or three of these and you will feel it in your appointment book, even if the site looks smart. Let's take the ones that matter most in turn.

- Online booking, or a request form that works on a phone at any hour
- A click-to-call button that is visible the moment the page loads
- Clear, upfront pricing — or at least a sense of what things cost
- Real reviews and your GDC registration on show
- Photos of the actual practice and team, not stock models
- Fast loading and a layout that reads well on a small screen

## Make booking the easiest thing on the page

Most people find a dentist on their phone, often in the evening when your reception is closed. If the only way to book is to call during working hours, you are asking a nervous person to wait — and waiting gives them time to click a competitor instead. An online booking tool, or at least a short request form, lets them act the moment they have decided.

Pair it with a click-to-call button that sits at the top of every page on mobile. One tap, no scrolling, no hunting for a number. For urgent problems — a lost filling, a broken tooth over the weekend — that single button is often the difference between a booking and a bounce.

## Build trust before they pick up the phone

Choosing a dentist is a trust decision, especially for anxious patients. Your website has to do the reassuring your team would do in person, and a few honest touches carry a lot of weight.

Show real reviews pulled from your Google Business Profile. Put your GDC registration and the names of your dentists where people can see them. Use photos of your actual reception, surgery and team rather than stock models — patients want to picture walking through your door. And be upfront about fees. You do not need a full price list, but a nervous patient who can see roughly what a check-up or a filling costs is far more likely to book than one left guessing.

> A nervous patient doesn't book the best dentist. They book the one they trust enough to walk into.

## Help the right people find you first

The best features in the world do nothing if no one reaches the page. When someone searches for an emergency dentist or a check-up near your town, you want to be in that short list at the top. That is [local SEO](/services/seo): a tidy Google Business Profile, a consistent name, address and phone number everywhere online, pages that mention the areas you serve, and reviews that keep coming in. It is the quiet engine behind everything else on this list.

## Keep it fast, simple and something you own

Speed matters more than most practices realise. A page that takes too long to load on mobile data loses people before they read a word. Keep images optimised, keep the design clean, and do not bury the important things under sliders and pop-ups.

Just as important, the site should be yours. You should be able to update your opening hours, add a new dentist or change a price without waiting on anyone. Every site we build for [practices like yours](/industries) comes with a simple admin panel so you can, plus 30 days of aftercare. After that, [a simple care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps it updated, backed up and secure from £27 a month. And because everything is fixed-price, you know the cost up front — [our prices](/pricing) start at £477 with no hourly billing and no lock-in.

## The quick wins

- Make booking possible in one tap, at any hour — online booking plus a sticky click-to-call button.
- Trust signals win anxious patients over: real reviews, GDC registration, genuine photos and clear fees.
- Local SEO is what gets the right people to the page in the first place.
- A fast site you can edit yourself keeps patients happy and keeps you in control.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a dental website cost?

Our dental websites are fixed-price, so there are no hourly bills or surprises. Builds start at £477 for Starter, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans that keep the site updated and secure start at £27 a month, and SEO or redesigns are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build a dental website?

Most sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we get your content, photos and sign-off. We agree the timeline before we start, so you are never left wondering where things stand.

### Do I really need online booking?

You do not strictly need a live booking system, but you do need an easy way for people to act. A short request form plus a click-to-call button covers most practices well. If your patient numbers justify full online booking, it usually pays for itself quickly.

### Will I be able to update the website myself?

Yes. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can change opening hours, prices or staff details without calling us, plus 30 days of aftercare while you settle in. You own the site, domain and data outright, with no lock-in.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website. We walk through what is working, what is quietly costing you bookings, and the quick wins you could make — no charge and no obligation.

intro: Your practice could have the gentlest dentist in town and still lose patients before anyone ever calls. Most people choose a dentist the way they choose everything now: they land on your website, decide in seconds whether they trust you, and either book or bounce. Getting that decision to go your way comes down to a handful of dental website features that turn visitors into patients — not clever design tricks, just the right things in the right place.
The good news is that none of them are expensive or complicated. Below are the features that do the heavy lifting, why they matter, and how to check whether your current site already has them.

sections.h2: The dental website features that turn visitors into patients
paragraphs: A dental website has one job: convince someone with toothache, or a parent booking a check-up, that your practice is the safe, easy choice. It does that through a small set of features working together. Here is the shortlist a good [dental website](/services/website-design) needs before anything else.
Miss two or three of these and you will feel it in your appointment book, even if the site looks smart. Let's take the ones that matter most in turn.
bullets: Online booking, or a request form that works on a phone at any hour
A click-to-call button that is visible the moment the page loads
Clear, upfront pricing — or at least a sense of what things cost
Real reviews and your GDC registration on show
Photos of the actual practice and team, not stock models
Fast loading and a layout that reads well on a small screen

sections.h2: Make booking the easiest thing on the page
paragraphs: Most people find a dentist on their phone, often in the evening when your reception is closed. If the only way to book is to call during working hours, you are asking a nervous person to wait — and waiting gives them time to click a competitor instead. An online booking tool, or at least a short request form, lets them act the moment they have decided.

Pair it with a click-to-call button that sits at the top of every page on mobile. One tap, no scrolling, no hunting for a number. For urgent problems — a lost filling, a broken tooth over the weekend — that single button is often the difference between a booking and a bounce.

sections.h2: Build trust before they pick up the phone
paragraphs: Choosing a dentist is a trust decision, especially for anxious patients. Your website has to do the reassuring your team would do in person, and a few honest touches carry a lot of weight.

Show real reviews pulled from your Google Business Profile. Put your GDC registration and the names of your dentists where people can see them. Use photos of your actual reception, surgery and team rather than stock models — patients want to picture walking through your door. And be upfront about fees. You do not need a full price list, but a nervous patient who can see roughly what a check-up or a filling costs is far more likely to book than one left guessing.
sections.pullquote: A nervous patient doesn't book the best dentist. They book the one they trust enough to walk into.

sections.h2: Help the right people find you first
paragraphs: The best features in the world do nothing if no one reaches the page. When someone searches for an emergency dentist or a check-up near your town, you want to be in that short list at the top. That is [local SEO](/services/seo): a tidy Google Business Profile, a consistent name, address and phone number everywhere online, pages that mention the areas you serve, and reviews that keep coming in. It is the quiet engine behind everything else on this list.

sections.h2: Keep it fast, simple and something you own
paragraphs: Speed matters more than most practices realise. A page that takes too long to load on mobile data loses people before they read a word. Keep images optimised, keep the design clean, and do not bury the important things under sliders and pop-ups.

Just as important, the site should be yours. You should be able to update your opening hours, add a new dentist or change a price without waiting on anyone. Every site we build for [practices like yours](/industries) comes with a simple admin panel so you can, plus 30 days of aftercare. After that, [a simple care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps it updated, backed up and secure from £27 a month. And because everything is fixed-price, you know the cost up front — [our prices](/pricing) start at £477 with no hourly billing and no lock-in.

Trust signals win anxious patients over: real reviews, GDC registration, genuine photos and clear fees.
Local SEO is what gets the right people to the page in the first place.
A fast site you can edit yourself keeps patients happy and keeps you in control.

bodyMarkdown:
When a tap bursts at seven on a Sunday evening, nobody reaches for a brochure. They grab their phone, search 'emergency plumber near me', and tap the first result that looks like it can actually help. Winning that job often has less to do with being the best plumber on the street and more to do with your website. The website essentials every plumber needs all come down to one thing: making it effortless for a stressed stranger to trust you and get you on the phone.

The good news is you don't need a big, expensive site full of clever animations. You need a fast, clear one that does a handful of jobs really well. Here is what actually matters, what you can safely skip, and how to tell whether your current site is quietly losing you work.

## Be clear about the one job your site has to do

Your website has a single main job: turn a worried search into a booked call. Everything else is decoration. Before you spend time on logos or colour schemes, make sure a first-time visitor can see who you are, the areas you cover, what you fix, and how to reach you within a few seconds. Most local searches happen on a mobile, often outdoors and often in a hurry, so write for a reader who has one thumb free and very little patience.

## The website essentials every plumber needs

Strip a plumber's website back to what genuinely earns work and you are left with a short list. Get these right and you are already ahead of most of your competition.

- A click-to-call button that shows the moment the page loads — no scrolling, no hunting for a number.
- The areas you cover in plain words — the actual towns and postcodes, not just 'and surrounding areas'.
- A short, honest list of what you do: emergency call-outs, boiler repairs, leaks, bathrooms and Gas Safe work.
- Your Gas Safe registration number and any trade accreditations, shown clearly rather than buried.
- Real reviews or your Google rating near the top, where a nervous customer sees them first.
- Photos of your own team, van and finished jobs — not stock images of someone else's pipes.
- A simple contact form for non-urgent jobs, alongside your number for the ones that can't wait.
- Fast loading and a layout that works with one thumb on a phone screen.

> Most people who need a plumber are stressed and on their phone. If they can't tap 'call' in a few seconds, they're already dialling the next result.

## Speed and mobile come first, not last

A slow website loses jobs before a single word gets read. If your homepage takes five seconds to appear on a phone, plenty of visitors have already gone back and tapped the next plumber down the list. Keep pages light, compress your images, and avoid heavy sliders and pop-ups that fight the reader. Test it yourself on 4G rather than office wifi, and try booking your own call-out as if you were a customer in a panic. For a trade that lives on urgent enquiries, a [fast, well-built website](/services/website-design) is not a nice-to-have — it is the whole game.

## Trust is what you're really selling

You are asking someone to let a stranger into their home, often when they are already stressed and out of pocket. That is a big ask, and your website has to earn it. Show your Gas Safe number, your genuine reviews, and photos of your own work rather than stock images. A proper About page with a name and a face does more than any slogan, because people book people, not logos. If you cover several towns or specialise in certain jobs, a clear line about the [trades and areas you serve](/industries) helps a visitor feel you are genuinely local to them.

## Help Google send you the right calls

A brilliant website that nobody finds will never ring the phone. For a plumber, most of that visibility comes from local search: a complete, accurate Google Business Profile, the same contact details everywhere online, honest reviews, and clear pages for the services and areas you cover. Sensible [local SEO](/services/seo) puts you in front of people at the exact moment their boiler packs in. No honest agency can promise you the top spot overnight, but steady, proper work moves you in the right direction and helps you stay there.

## What good looks like after launch

You can have every one of these essentials without a five-figure budget. A focused plumber's site does not need dozens of pages — it needs the right handful, built well and then kept in good order. Software needs updating, and a broken enquiry form can quietly cost you a fortnight of leads before you spot it, so a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) that keeps the site fast, secure and backed up usually pays for itself. Just as important, make sure you own the site, domain and data at the end, rather than renting it and being locked in. We work to fixed prices so you know the cost up front, most builds go live in three to six weeks, and you can see straightforward options on [our pricing page](/pricing). If you would rather see what is holding your current site back first, a free teardown is a short screen recording that walks through it and points out the quick wins.

## The quick wins

- Your website has one job: turn a worried local search into a booked call.
- Click-to-call, coverage areas, your Gas Safe number and real reviews matter more than fancy design.
- Speed and mobile come first — a slow site loses jobs before anyone reads a word.
- Pair a fast site with local SEO so people find you the moment their boiler fails.

## Frequently asked questions

### What are the most important things a plumber's website needs?

The essentials are a click-to-call button that shows instantly, the areas and services you cover in plain words, your Gas Safe number, genuine reviews, photos of your own work, and pages that load fast on a phone. Get those right before you worry about anything fancier.

### How much does a plumber's website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price. They start at **£477** for a Starter build, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. Care plans to keep the site running start at **£27 a month**, and SEO or a full redesign is quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build a plumber's website?

Most of our builds go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly we get your content, photos and Gas Safe details. Every site comes with a simple admin panel so you can make changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once it is live.

### Do I need SEO as well as a website?

A website gets you ready for the call; local SEO helps the call actually happen by getting you found when someone searches. For most plumbers the two work best together — a fast, clear site plus a well-kept Google Business Profile and consistent details across the web. No one can honestly guarantee the top spot, but steady work makes a real difference over time.

### Will I own my plumber's website?

Yes. With us you own your site, domain and data outright — there is no lock-in and nothing held hostage if you ever move on. Always check this with any web company before you sign, because some rent you a site you can never take with you.

bodyMarkdown:
You've probably already got a website. The real question is whether it's bringing you work or just sitting there like a business card nobody reads. For most trades — from [plumbers and electricians to roofers and joiners](/industries) — the site looks perfectly fine, yet the phone stays quiet. Learning how tradespeople can get more leads from their website comes down to a handful of practical fixes, not an expensive rebuild or a clever marketing trick.

Someone searching for an emergency plumber in Leeds or a reliable sparky in Bristol makes their decision in seconds. They're on their phone, they're in a hurry, and they'll ring whoever makes it easiest. The changes below are the ones that reliably turn that visitor into a booked job.

## Make it effortless to get in touch

The biggest leak in most trade websites is a phone number that's hard to find. If a customer has to hunt for it, they've already tapped back to Google and rung the next firm on the list. Your number should sit top-right on every page and be tappable on a mobile, because most local searches happen on a phone with one thumb.

Give people more than one way to reach you, and never force them through a twenty-field form. A short enquiry box — name, number, postcode, what's wrong — alongside a call or WhatsApp button covers almost everyone. A [website built around getting enquiries](/services/website-design) treats that contact step as the main event, not something buried on a separate page.

## Get found when people search for your trade

You could have the tidiest site in the county, but if it doesn't appear when someone types 'electrician near me', it can't win you the job. This is where local SEO earns its keep. Claim and fully complete your Google Business Profile, keep your business name, address and phone number identical everywhere they appear online, and gather reviews steadily rather than in one big rush.

Getting the basics right here often does more for a small trade business than any paid advertising. If you'd rather not wrestle with it yourself, our [local SEO service](/services/seo) handles the profile, the pages and the technical bits that help Google trust your site.

## Build a page for every job and every town

A single 'services' page trying to cover boiler repairs, bathroom fits and emergency call-outs across three counties will struggle to rank for any of them. Google likes specifics, and so does a worried customer at eight in the evening. Give each core service its own page and, where it makes sense, a page for each main town or area you cover — written properly, not the same paragraph with the place name swapped in. That's how you show up for 'boiler repair in Stockport' rather than hoping a generic homepage does the work.

## On a phone, speed beats looks

Most of your customers are searching one-handed, sometimes standing over a leak. If your homepage takes five seconds to load, a good number of them are gone before they've even seen your name. Big background videos and heavy image sliders look impressive on a designer's monitor and quietly cost you work in the real world.

Keep pages light, compress your photos, and check that every button works with a thumb. A site that loads quickly and reads cleanly on a small screen will out-earn a prettier one that stutters, every time.

## Earn trust before the phone rings

People are naturally wary of letting a stranger into their home. Your website's real job is to answer one quiet question — 'can I trust this person?' — before they ever pick up the phone. A few honest signals do that better than any amount of polished design:

- Real photos of your own jobs — a genuine before-and-after beats a stock image every time.
- Actual reviews from Google or Checkatrade shown on the page, not just a logo linking away.
- Trade accreditations such as Gas Safe, NICEIC or NAPIT, displayed where people can see them.
- A short, human line about who you are, how long you've been going and the areas you cover.
- Some pricing guidance, even a simple 'from' figure, so enquiries arrive already half-sold.

> A visitor who trusts you before they ring is a warmer lead than any paid advert can buy.

## How tradespeople can get more leads from their website

Put it together and the pattern is simple: make it fast, make it obvious, and make it trustworthy. Here's a quick checklist to hold your own site against:

None of this needs a huge budget. Our [fixed-price website packages](/pricing) start at £477, so you know the full cost before anything begins, and you own the site, domain and content outright with no lock-in. If you'd like to know what's quietly losing you jobs right now, book a free teardown and we'll send you a short screen-recorded walkthrough of your current site.

- Loads in under three seconds on a mobile — impatient customers won't wait around.
- Phone number and a call button visible without scrolling.
- A clear headline saying exactly what you do and where you do it.
- Separate service and location pages that match how people actually search.
- Proof — photos, reviews and accreditations — near the top and throughout.
- One obvious next step on every page: call, message or fill in the form.

## The quick wins

- Most trade websites lose leads because the phone number is hard to find and the site is slow on a mobile.
- Separate service and location pages help you show up when people search for your trade nearby.
- Real photos, genuine reviews and trade accreditations win more jobs than slick design ever will.
- You don't need a big budget — small fixes to contact, speed and trust move the needle most.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for tradespeople cost?

Our websites are fixed-price and start at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. There's no hourly billing, so you know the full cost before we begin.

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly you can send us photos and details. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

### Will a new website guarantee me more leads?

No honest agency can promise you specific rankings or a set number of enquiries. What we can do is build a fast, trustworthy site that shows up in local searches and makes it easy to get in touch — the things that reliably bring more work over time.

### Do I have to pay someone every month to keep it running?

Not necessarily. You own your site, domain and data outright, so there's no lock-in. If you'd rather not worry about updates, backups and hosting, our [care plans](/services/care-plans) start at £27 a month, but they're entirely optional.

sections.h2: Earn trust before the phone rings
paragraphs: People are naturally wary of letting a stranger into their home. Your website's real job is to answer one quiet question — 'can I trust this person?' — before they ever pick up the phone. A few honest signals do that better than any amount of polished design:
bullets: Real photos of your own jobs — a genuine before-and-after beats a stock image every time.
Actual reviews from Google or Checkatrade shown on the page, not just a logo linking away.
Trade accreditations such as Gas Safe, NICEIC or NAPIT, displayed where people can see them.
A short, human line about who you are, how long you've been going and the areas you cover.
Some pricing guidance, even a simple 'from' figure, so enquiries arrive already half-sold.
sections.pullquote: A visitor who trusts you before they ring is a warmer lead than any paid advert can buy.

sections.h2: How tradespeople can get more leads from their website
paragraphs: Put it together and the pattern is simple: make it fast, make it obvious, and make it trustworthy. Here's a quick checklist to hold your own site against:

None of this needs a huge budget. Our [fixed-price website packages](/pricing) start at £477, so you know the full cost before anything begins, and you own the site, domain and content outright with no lock-in. If you'd like to know what's quietly losing you jobs right now, book a free teardown and we'll send you a short screen-recorded walkthrough of your current site.
bullets: Loads in under three seconds on a mobile — impatient customers won't wait around.
Phone number and a call button visible without scrolling.
A clear headline saying exactly what you do and where you do it.
Separate service and location pages that match how people actually search.
Proof — photos, reviews and accreditations — near the top and throughout.
One obvious next step on every page: call, message or fill in the form.

keyTakeaways: Most trade websites lose leads because the phone number is hard to find and the site is slow on a mobile.
Separate service and location pages help you show up when people search for your trade nearby.
Real photos, genuine reviews and trade accreditations win more jobs than slick design ever will.
You don't need a big budget — small fixes to contact, speed and trust move the needle most.

bodyMarkdown:
When someone finds your restaurant online, they are usually hungry, in a hurry and deciding fast. Get the restaurant website essentials right — menus, bookings and more — and you turn that quick look into a booked table. Get them wrong and the diner taps back to Google and picks the place down the road.

You do not need a flashy site with clever animations to win that decision. You need the handful of things every diner is actually looking for, laid out so clearly that booking feels like the obvious next step. Here is what matters, and why.

## Start with the menu — it's what they came for

The menu is the number one reason people visit a restaurant website. Not your founding story, not your interior shots — the food and the prices. So make it impossible to miss and easy to read.

Put the menu one tap from your homepage and show it as real text on the page, not a PDF or a photo of a printed sheet. PDFs are slow to load, awkward to read on a phone and invisible to Google. If a visitor has to pinch and zoom to read your starters, you have already spent a little of their goodwill. Real text also means a search engine can read your dishes, which helps you show up when someone nearby searches for something like wood-fired pizza or a Sunday roast.

Keep your prices current, too. Nothing sours a first visit like arriving to find the £12 main is now £16. If your menu changes often, this is exactly the kind of thing a well-built [restaurant website](/services/website-design) with a simple admin panel should let you edit yourself in minutes.

## Make booking a table effortless

A diner who is ready to book is a diner you do not want to make work. Aim for the fewest possible steps between this looks good and table booked.

If you take online bookings, put a clear booking option on every page — in the header, next to the menu, and again at the foot. If you take bookings by phone, make the number tappable so a mobile visitor can call in one touch rather than copying digits across apps. Whatever you use, avoid dumping people onto a clunky third-party page that looks nothing like your brand and demands ten fields before it will hold a table.

For quieter weeknights, pairing that booking button with a line like walk-ins always welcome reassures the spontaneous crowd as well as the planners.

> Every extra click or form field between hungry and booked is a chance for the diner to change their mind.

## Win on mobile, and win on speed

Most people looking for somewhere to eat are doing it on a phone — often stood on the pavement or sat in the car, deciding then and there. If your site is slow or fiddly on mobile, they will not wait for it.

That means large, tappable buttons, text you can read without zooming, and pages that load in a couple of seconds. Heavy background videos and enormous, uncompressed photos are the usual culprits behind a sluggish restaurant site. Trim the image sizes, keep the design clean, and test the whole thing on a real phone rather than only your laptop.

## Help hungry locals find you

Being found is half the battle, and for restaurants that battle is almost entirely local. When someone types Sunday roast near me or best curry in the town centre, Google leans heavily on your Google Business Profile and the signals coming from your website.

Claim and fully fill out your Google Business Profile, keep your name, address and phone number identical everywhere they appear, and make sure your opening hours are right — especially over bank holidays and Christmas. Strong [local SEO](/services/seo) ties your website and your profile together, so you turn up in the map results where hungry locals are actually looking.

## The trust details that tip a browser into a booking

Once a visitor likes the look of your food, a few practical details decide whether they commit. Leave them out and even a keen diner hesitates.

Make sure your website clearly covers:

None of this needs to be clever. It just needs to be there, accurate and quick to find.

- Opening hours, including kitchen last-orders and any different weekend times
- Your full address with a map link, plus a note on parking or the nearest station
- A few honest, well-lit photos of the food, the room and the people who run it
- Dietary information — vegan, vegetarian, gluten-free and clear allergen notes
- Whether you are dog-friendly, child-friendly or set up for larger groups
- A link to your live reviews so newcomers can see you are trusted

## Bringing your restaurant website essentials together

A good restaurant website is not a one-off job you finish and forget. Menus change, prices move, seasonal specials come and go, and Christmas bookings want a banner up by October. A site that is easy to update yourself — or a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) that handles the tweaks for you — keeps everything current without eating into your evenings.

The good news is that none of this has to be expensive or drawn out. We build restaurant sites at fixed prices, so you know the cost up front, you own everything, and most go live in three to six weeks. You can see exactly where you would stand on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

If you already have a site and you are not sure it is pulling its weight, the quickest way to find out is a free teardown — a short, screen-recorded review where we walk through your menu, booking flow and mobile experience and point out what is quietly costing you covers.

## The quick wins

- Lead with the menu as readable text, not a PDF, and keep prices up to date.
- Make booking a one- or two-tap action, with a tappable phone number on mobile.
- Speed and mobile usability decide most restaurant visits — test on a real phone.
- Tie your website to a complete Google Business Profile so locals find you.
- Cover the practical details: hours, address, dietary info, photos and reviews.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a restaurant website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the total up front with no hourly billing. Starter builds begin at **£477**, Growth at **£1,170** and Complete at **£2,370**, with optional care plans from **£27 a month** for ongoing updates. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

### How long does it take to build a restaurant website?

Most of our sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how much content you have ready and how quickly we get your menu, photos and details. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### Should my menu be a PDF on my website?

No. PDFs are slow to open, hard to read on a phone and largely invisible to Google. Show your menu as real text on the page instead. It loads instantly, reads cleanly on mobile, and lets search engines pick up your dishes when people search locally.

### Do I need online booking, or is a phone number enough?

It depends on how you take reservations. Busy restaurants usually benefit from online booking on every page, while smaller or walk-in venues do fine with a tappable phone number and clear opening hours. The key is making whichever option you choose obvious and effortless on mobile.

### Can I update the menu and prices myself?

Yes. Every site we build includes a simple admin panel, so you can change dishes, prices and specials in minutes. If you would rather not touch it, a [care plan](/services/care-plans) means we handle the updates for you.

title: How Cafes and Salons Can Win More Bookings Online
description: Plain-English ways to help your cafe or salon fill quiet slots and win more bookings online, from your website to your Google listing. See how.
metaTitle: How Cafes and Salons Can Win More Bookings Online
excerpt: Quiet Tuesdays and empty 2pm slots usually aren't about the service — they're about how you show up online. Here's how to fix it.
targetKeyword: how cafes and salons can win more bookings online

cta.heading: Losing bookings you can't see?
cta.sub: Get a free teardown of your cafe or salon website — a short, honest video showing exactly where bookings are leaking and how to plug the gaps.

faq.q: How much does a website for a cafe or salon cost?
faq.a: Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. Starter builds begin at **£477**, Growth at **£1,170** and Complete at **£2,370**, and care plans start at **£27 a month**. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. You can see exactly what's included on our [pricing page](/pricing).

faq.q: How long does it take to build?
faq.a: Most sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly we get your content, photos and booking details. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make small changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

faq.q: Do I need a separate booking system?
faq.a: Usually, yes. A dedicated booking or reservation tool handles availability, deposits and reminders far better than a plain contact form. We'll connect the one you already use, or help you choose one, and link it so booking is never more than a tap or two away.
