faq.a: No honest agency can promise exact numbers or rankings. What a well-built, fast, easy-to-book site does is remove the friction that quietly costs you customers and give Google a clearer reason to show you. The rest is your great coffee or your brilliant stylists doing their job.

faq.q: What is a free teardown?
faq.a: It's a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out what's helping and what's costing you bookings — the quick wins and the bigger opportunities. There's no charge, and no obligation to work with us afterwards.

bodyMarkdown:
Picture someone on the high street at five o'clock, phone in hand, deciding where to grab a flat white or squeeze in a quick trim before heading home. They search, they tap the first place that looks open and easy to book, and that's that. If that place isn't you, the gap in your day isn't really about your coffee or your colourists — it's about how you show up online. This is a plain, practical guide to how cafes and salons can win more bookings online, with no big budget or marketing degree required.

Most of the wins here are small and fixable. You don't need to be on every platform or post ten times a day. You need a website that loads quickly, a booking button people can't miss, and a Google presence that puts you in front of local people at the exact moment they're deciding. Whether you run a single-site coffee shop or a busy salon like plenty of the other [hospitality and beauty businesses](/industries) we work with, the same handful of things move the needle.

## Make booking the easiest thing on your website

Open your own website on your phone and behave like a customer. From the moment the homepage loads, count the taps it takes to actually book a table or an appointment. If it's more than two, you're quietly losing people who would happily have come in. Booking should be the loudest, clearest action on the page — not buried under a menu or hidden behind a contact form nobody checks.

None of this needs a fancy rebuild. A clean, fast [website design](/services/website-design) that puts booking front and centre will usually do more for your diary than any amount of clever marketing. Get these basics right before you spend a penny on anything else.

- Put a booking button in the header and repeat it at the foot of every page.
- Link straight to one booking or reservation system, so nobody has to hunt for it.
- Show opening hours and prices up front, so people don't have to ring to find out.
- Add your address and a map, so walk-ins and first-timers know exactly where you are.
- Make your phone number tappable, so one press starts the call.

## How cafes and salons can win more bookings online through local search

Most people looking for a nearby cafe or salon never scroll past the little map at the top of Google. Those three results — the local pack — are prime real estate, and you don't have to pay for them. You earn your spot with a complete, accurate Google Business Profile and a steady trickle of genuine reviews, which together tell Google you're a real, active business worth showing.

Fill in everything: opening hours, photos of the space and the work, your booking link, the services you offer. Reply to reviews, good and bad, like a real person. Ask happy customers to leave a quick word while they're still buzzing from a great cut or a lovely lunch. Over time this local visibility, backed by sensible [local SEO](/services/seo), is what turns a Google search into someone walking through your door.

## Win on mobile, because that's where people decide

Nearly everyone finding you is doing it on a phone, often one-handed, often in a hurry. If your site is slow, pinchy or awkward on a small screen, they'll bounce back to Google and pick someone else before your images have even loaded. Speed and simplicity aren't nice-to-haves here — they're the whole game.

Keep pages light, use large tap-friendly buttons, and make sure the booking step works flawlessly on mobile before you worry about anything else. Test it on a real phone, on mobile data, not just on your office wifi. The experience your customer actually gets is the only one that counts.

> Most local searches happen on a phone, often while someone is standing outside deciding where to go — so your site has to work in that exact moment.

## Turn first-timers into regulars

Winning the booking is only half the job. The real money in a cafe or salon is in people who come back, so give them a reason to. Capture emails at the point of booking, send the occasional offer for a quiet weekday, and keep your website looking current — fresh photos, up-to-date prices, seasonal menus or treatment lists.

A site that never changes slowly starts to look closed. A simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps everything updated, backed up and secure, so you can stay focused on your customers rather than wrestling with your website. Small, regular touches keep you in mind long after that first visit, and they cost far less than winning a brand-new customer from scratch.

## The quick wins

- Make booking a one- or two-tap action on every page of your site.
- Your Google Business Profile and reviews often matter more than your homepage.
- Fast, mobile-first pages win the customers who decide on the spot.
- Keeping your site fresh turns one-off visitors into loyal regulars.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a cafe or salon cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. Starter builds begin at **£477**, Growth at **£1,170** and Complete at **£2,370**, and care plans start at **£27 a month**. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. You can see exactly what's included on our [pricing page](/pricing).

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how quickly we get your content, photos and booking details. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make small changes yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

### Do I need a separate booking system?

Usually, yes. A dedicated booking or reservation tool handles availability, deposits and reminders far better than a plain contact form. We'll connect the one you already use, or help you choose one, and link it so booking is never more than a tap or two away.

### Will a new website guarantee more bookings?

No honest agency can promise exact numbers or rankings. What a well-built, fast, easy-to-book site does is remove the friction that quietly costs you customers and give Google a clearer reason to show you. The rest is your great coffee or your brilliant stylists doing their job.

### What is a free teardown?

It's a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out what's helping and what's costing you bookings — the quick wins and the bigger opportunities. There's no charge, and no obligation to work with us afterwards.

description: A practical guide to what to include on an accountancy website: the pages, trust signals and local SEO that turn visitors into booked clients.

faq.q: What pages should an accountant's website have?
faq.a: At a minimum: a homepage, a page for each core service (self-assessment, limited company accounts, VAT, payroll, bookkeeping), an about page with real qualifications, a pricing or how-we-charge page, genuine testimonials, and an easy-to-find contact page. Pages aimed at specific clients, like contractors or landlords, help you stand out and rank.

faq.q: Do I need to show prices on my accountancy website?
faq.a: You do not have to publish a full price list, but you should explain how you charge. Being open about fixed fees or typical starting points builds trust, saves you time, and filters out enquiries that were never a good fit in the first place.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run an accountancy practice, your website is often the first thing a prospective client sees long before they ever pick up the phone. So the question of what to include on websites for accountants matters more than most owners think. A neat homepage and a phone number is not enough on its own.

The good news is that a site which actually wins clients does not need to be complicated. It needs to answer the questions people really have, prove you are qualified and trustworthy, and make the next step obvious. Here is what to put on the page, and why each part earns its place.

## Start with what your clients are worried about

Most people looking for an accountant are not hunting for the cheapest option. They are trying to work out who they can hand their finances to without lying awake at night. That means your site has to speak to real worries: am I paying too much tax, will I miss a deadline, is my bookkeeping a mess, and can I actually get hold of this person when something goes wrong.

Write in plain English, not jargon. Swap "statutory compliance solutions" for "we file your accounts and tax returns on time." When a sole trader or a small limited company owner lands on your site, they should feel understood within the first few seconds. Everything else you add works better once that feeling is in place.

> Most people choosing an accountant are not comparing spreadsheets. They are deciding who they can trust with their money.

## What to include on websites for accountants

At a minimum, a practice website should cover the core pages below. Each one does a specific job, from ranking in search to answering the question a nervous first-time client is too shy to ask out loud.

Thoughtful [website design](/services/website-design) ties these pages together so the site flows, rather than feeling like a stack of documents bolted on over the years.

- A clear page for each main service, such as self-assessment, limited company accounts, VAT, payroll and bookkeeping, so both people and search engines understand what you do.
- A pricing page, or at least an honest explanation of how you charge, whether that is a fixed monthly fee, a price per return, or a tailored quote.
- An about page with real names, faces and qualifications, not stock photos of strangers in suits.
- Pages for the clients you want more of, like contractors, landlords, hospitality or e-commerce, written in their language.
- Genuine reviews or testimonials, ideally tied to a named business or a recognisable sector.
- A contact page with more than one way to reach you.

## Show your credentials and build trust

Accountancy is a trust business, so the proof matters. Display your professional body clearly, whether that is ICAEW, ACCA, AAT or CIMA, along with any licences and your anti-money-laundering supervision. If you work with specific software such as Xero, QuickBooks or FreeAgent, say so, because plenty of clients search by the tools they already use.

Be upfront about fees, too. You do not have to publish a full price list, but explaining how you charge removes a major barrier and quietly filters out the wrong enquiries. Our own [fixed prices](/pricing) exist for the same reason: people relax when they can see the cost before they commit to anything.

## Make it easy to get in touch

You would be surprised how many accountancy sites bury the phone number and hide behind a single contact form. Give people options: a visible phone number, an email address, a short enquiry form, and ideally a way to book a call directly. Ask only for what you need on the form, because a name, an email and a one-line reason for getting in touch is plenty to start a conversation.

If you serve a mix of clients, a simple question like "are you a sole trader, limited company or landlord?" helps you prepare before the first call. And when someone is finally ready to talk, do not make them hunt for you. Put a clear route to [get in touch](/contact) on every page.

## Help local clients find you

Many small businesses still want an accountant they can meet, or at least one who clearly understands their area. That makes local search valuable. Claim and complete your Google Business Profile, keep your name, address and phone number consistent everywhere online, and build content around the places and sectors you actually serve.

Strong [local SEO](/services/seo) means that when someone nearby searches for "accountant near me" or "small business accountant," you have a fair chance of appearing. Most of those searches happen on a phone, so your site has to load quickly and read well on a small screen or the click is wasted.

## Keep it accurate all year round

An accountancy website is never quite finished. Tax thresholds change, deadlines shift, and Making Tax Digital keeps moving the goalposts. A page that still quotes last year's allowances quietly chips away at your credibility with exactly the people you are trying to win over.

This is where a simple maintenance routine pays off. A [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps the software updated, the content current and the site backed up, so you are not scrambling to patch things together in the middle of self-assessment season.

## The quick wins

- Write for worried clients, not other accountants. Plain English beats jargon every time.
- Cover the essentials: clear service pages, real credentials, honest pricing and easy contact.
- Local SEO and a fast, mobile-friendly site help nearby businesses actually find you.
- Keep tax figures and deadlines current so your site stays credible all year.

## Frequently asked questions

### What pages should an accountant's website have?

At a minimum: a homepage, a page for each core service (self-assessment, limited company accounts, VAT, payroll, bookkeeping), an about page with real qualifications, a pricing or how-we-charge page, genuine testimonials, and an easy-to-find contact page. Pages aimed at specific clients, like contractors or landlords, help you stand out and rank.

### Do I need to show prices on my accountancy website?

You do not have to publish a full price list, but you should explain how you charge. Being open about fixed fees or typical starting points builds trust, saves you time, and filters out enquiries that were never a good fit in the first place.

### How much does an accountant's website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price so you know the cost up front, starting at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Ongoing care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or redesigns are quoted per project. No hourly billing and no surprises.

### How long does it take to build an accountancy website?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how much content is ready and how quickly we get feedback. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, and you own your site, domain and data with no lock-in.

### Will a new website help me rank on Google?

A well-structured, fast site with clear service pages and strong local SEO gives you a much better chance of ranking than an outdated one. We cannot promise specific positions, but the fundamentals we build in are exactly what search engines and local clients are looking for.

bodyMarkdown:
When someone looks for a solicitor, they are rarely in a relaxed mood. They might be buying a home, going through a divorce, or sorting out a loved one's estate. Before they ever pick up the phone, they land on your website and make a snap decision about whether your firm feels safe and capable. That is why building trust online sits at the very centre of good websites for solicitors: the design has to reassure an anxious visitor in seconds.

The good news is that trust is not a mystery. It comes from a handful of practical signals you can build into every page. Here is how to earn it.

## First impressions happen in seconds

On a small screen, in the middle of a stressful week, a visitor decides very quickly whether to stay or hit the back button. Most local searches happen on mobile, so your site has to load fast and read cleanly on a phone. A dated look, tiny text, or a layout that shifts around as it loads quietly tells people your firm may be equally behind the times, even when your legal work is excellent.

Calm, professional [website design](/services/website-design) does the opposite. Generous spacing, clear headings and a considered colour scheme signal that you are organised and pay attention to detail. Clients cannot judge your case law, so they judge what they can see.

## What building trust online means for solicitors

Trust online is built from concrete signals, not vague promises. For a law firm, the ones that carry the most weight are the simplest to get right:

- Your SRA regulation shown clearly, including your firm's SRA number and the clickable digital badge in the footer.
- Real photographs of your team and premises rather than stock images of strangers in suits.
- Named solicitors with their qualifications, areas of practice and a short, human bio.
- Genuine reviews from real clients, never invented or bought.
- Honest information about fees, or at least how your pricing works, so nobody feels ambushed.
- A visible complaints procedure and privacy policy, which the regulator expects and clients quietly appreciate.

> People do not instruct a website they do not trust, and they decide long before they read a word about the law.

## The pages a solicitor's website really needs

A trustworthy site is also a well-structured one. Each area of your practice deserves its own page so it can rank for the right search and speak to one specific worry. You can see how we lay this out across different firms in our [recent work](/work).

As a starting point, most solicitors need:

- A clear home page that says who you help and where you are based.
- A separate page for each practice area, such as conveyancing, family law, wills and probate, or employment.
- A proper meet-the-team page with faces and credentials.
- A fees or pricing page, since the SRA already requires price transparency for certain services and clients reward openness.
- An easy contact page with phone, email, a short form and your address.

## Speed, security and accessibility

Trust is technical as well as visual. A padlock on every page, a site that loads in a couple of seconds, and text that works for someone using a screen reader or reading on a crowded train all reassure visitors without them noticing. A slow or insecure site does the reverse, and a browser warning about a missing certificate can end a client relationship before it begins.

Accessibility is not just good manners either. It widens the audience who can actually use your site and aligns with the standards public-facing organisations are increasingly expected to meet. This is where a solid build and a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) to keep everything patched and backed up quietly earn their keep.

## Getting found: local SEO for law firms

A trustworthy website only helps if people can find it. Most instructions begin with a local search such as conveyancing solicitor in Leeds or family solicitor near me. Strong [local SEO](/services/seo), a complete Google Business Profile and dedicated pages for each town and practice area help you appear at the moment someone is ready to make contact.

Reviews feed into this too, so trust and visibility reinforce one another. The firm that looks credible and shows up first has already won half the decision.

## Keeping it fixed-price and simple

Many firms put off a new website because they fear an open-ended bill. Our approach is the opposite: [fixed-price packages](/pricing) so you know the cost up front, with no hourly billing and no surprises. You own the site, domain and data, so there is no lock-in, and you deal with a small UK team directly.

Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, and most sites go live in three to six weeks. If you would like to see how your current site measures up on trust, that is exactly what a free teardown is for.

## The quick wins

- Clients judge a solicitor's website in seconds, mostly on mobile, so speed and a calm, professional design matter before a word is read.
- Trust comes from concrete signals: SRA regulation, real photos, named solicitors, genuine reviews and honest fee information.
- A clear page for each practice area, backed by local SEO and a Google Business Profile, helps you get found and reassure at the same time.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a solicitor cost?

Our websites are fixed-price so you know the cost up front, with no hourly billing. Prices start at **£477** for the Starter package, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete. Care plans start at **£27/month**, and SEO or a full redesign is quoted per project. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing page](/pricing).

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how many practice-area pages you need and how quickly you can get us content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### Do I need to publish my fees on the website?

The SRA's transparency rules require many firms to publish price and service information for certain areas of work. Beyond the rules, being open about how your fees work is one of the strongest trust signals you can send. We can help you present this clearly without giving away more than you want to.

### Can I update the website myself afterwards?

Yes. Every build includes a straightforward admin panel so you can edit text, add team members and publish articles without touching code. If you would rather we handle updates, security and backups for you, that is what our care plans are for.

### What is a free teardown?

It is a short, screen-recorded review of your current website. We walk through it as a prospective client would, point out where trust is leaking away, and highlight the quick wins. There is no obligation and nothing to prepare.

faq.a: Yes. Portals put your listings next to every competitor and charge you for the privilege. faq.q: What actually makes an estate agent website generate enquiries?
faq.a: Three things above all: an obvious, low-friction valuation request; genuine local content that proves you know the area; and a fast, mobile-friendly experience with real trust signals. Looks help, but those are what turn visitors into calls.

bodyMarkdown:
Estate agent websites that generate enquiries don't win because they look expensive. They win because a homeowner thinking about selling lands on the page, quickly sees that you know their area, and finds it easy to ask for a valuation. Most agency sites get the first part right and the last part badly wrong.

Whether you're an independent sales and lettings office or a two-branch high street agent, your website has one real job: turn a curious visitor into a booked valuation or a viewing. This guide covers what actually moves that needle, in plain English, with no jargon and no guesswork.

## How estate agent websites generate enquiries

Enquiries come from a short chain of moments. Someone searches for an agent or a specific property, clicks through, decides within seconds whether you feel local and credible, then either acts or leaves. Estate agent websites that generate enquiries strip the friction out of every link in that chain.

The agents who struggle usually have a site that behaves like a brochure: lots of photos, a property feed pulled in from a portal, and a contact form buried three clicks deep. It looks fine. It just never asks for the business. A [website that's built to convert](/services/website-design) does the opposite — every page has an obvious next step, and the most valuable one, a valuation request, is never more than a tap away.

## Make the valuation request impossible to miss

For a sales agent, the free valuation is the offer that turns a browser into a lead. Treat it like your shop window. Put a clear valuation button in the header, repeat it at the foot of every area page, and keep the form short — name, postcode, phone. You can qualify the rest on the call.

Long forms scare people off. Ask for the number of bedrooms, the reason for moving and a preferred appointment slot before you've earned any trust, and most homeowners quietly give up. Ask for the minimum, reply fast, and let the conversation do the heavy lifting.

> A homeowner deciding who to invite round for a valuation is choosing a person, not a portal — your site has to make that choice feel easy.

## Sell your local knowledge, not just the properties

The portals already list every property in the country. What they can't do is prove you understand a particular street, a school catchment or a commuter line. That local knowledge is your edge, and your website is where you put it on show.

Area guides are the simplest way to do it. A short, honest page for each patch you cover — what it's like to live there, typical prices, who tends to buy — pulls double duty: it reassures sellers that you know their market, and it gives Google genuinely local pages worth ranking.

- A page for each town, suburb or postcode you actively sell in, written in your own words
- Honest notes on schools, transport links and the kind of buyer each area draws
- Recent sold prices or market trends, so the page reads as current rather than generic
- A short thinking-of-selling prompt, with a valuation link, at the end of every guide

## The quiet dealbreakers: speed, mobile and trust

Most people browse property on a phone, often in the evening on the sofa. If your site is slow to load, awkward to scroll or fiddly to tap, they don't complain — they just leave. These basics rarely get discussed, yet they quietly decide whether an enquiry ever happens.

Keeping all of this working as browsers and standards change is exactly what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for — small, steady upkeep so the site winning enquiries this year still does next year.

- Speed: pages that load in a couple of seconds, even on mobile data
- Mobile layout: buttons and forms sized for thumbs, not desktop mice
- Trust signals: real photos of your team and office, clear opening hours, and a phone number people can see at a glance
- Genuine reviews from local sellers and landlords, shown on the pages that matter

## Write like you'd speak, then help Google

Estate agency copy has a habit of drifting into corporate mush — 'bespoke property solutions', 'unrivalled market expertise'. Homeowners skim straight past it. The pages that convert sound like a knowledgeable local person talking plainly: what you do, where you do it, and why a seller should call you first.

That same plain writing is what search engines reward now. Answer the real questions people ask — what's my home worth, how much do you charge, how quickly can you sell — and you help buyers, sellers and Google at the same time. Clear beats clever every time.

## Get found by sellers in your patch

A brilliant website nobody finds is an expensive business card. To bring in enquiries you need to show up when someone in your town searches for an agent, or types a street name into Google. That is where [local SEO](/services/seo) earns its keep.

A well-optimised Google Business Profile, consistent contact details across the web, and those area guides working together will do more for a high street agent than any amount of paid advertising. It is the same groundwork we lay across the [industries we build for](/industries), tuned to how people actually choose an estate agent.

None of this demands a five-figure budget. You can see [our fixed prices](/pricing) up front, pick the level that fits, and know exactly what you're paying before anyone starts work.

## The quick wins

- Your website's real job isn't to look expensive — it's to turn visitors into valuation and viewing enquiries.
- Make the free valuation the clearest action on the site, and keep the form to name, postcode and phone.
- Area guides prove local knowledge the portals can't, and give Google genuinely local pages to rank.
- Speed, a clean mobile layout and real trust signals quietly decide whether an enquiry ever happens.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does an estate agent website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the full cost before any work begins — no hourly billing. Builds start at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete. SEO work and redesigns are quoted per project, and care plans start at **£27 a month**.

### How long does it take to build?

Most of our sites go live within **three to six weeks**, depending on how much content and photography is ready. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can edit staff, area guides and listings yourself, plus **30 days of aftercare** once you're live.

### Do I still need a website if I already advertise on the big portals?

Yes. Portals put your listings next to every competitor and charge you for the privilege. Your own site is where you win instructions rather than sell someone else's property — it's where sellers judge whether to trust you with their biggest asset, and where a valuation enquiry costs you nothing per lead.

### What actually makes an estate agent website generate enquiries?

Three things above all: an obvious, low-friction valuation request; genuine local content that proves you know the area; and a fast, mobile-friendly experience with real trust signals. Looks help, but those are what turn visitors into calls.

### Can you improve my existing site instead of rebuilding it?

Often, yes. If the foundations are sound, we can improve speed, local SEO and the valuation journey without starting from scratch. A free teardown is the quickest way to find out which route makes sense for you.

bodyMarkdown:
If you consult for a living, most of your work probably arrives through word of mouth. A former colleague recommends you, someone remembers a talk you gave, an old client comes back. It works, right up until the month it doesn't. That is exactly why consultants need a professional website: not to replace your reputation, but to catch every person who hears your name and quietly types it into Google to check you out.

Because they do check. A prospect who is about to trust you with a strategy, a rebrand or a six-figure decision will look you up first. What they find in those ten seconds decides whether you feel like a safe pair of hands or a gamble. A tidy, credible site tips that moment in your favour before you have said a word.

## Your website is the reference you don't have to ask for

Think about how a new client actually finds you. Someone mentions your name over coffee. The prospect nods, then goes home and searches for you that evening. If the first result is a half-finished LinkedIn profile or a landing page you built in an afternoon three years ago, the warm introduction cools fast.

A professional website does the vouching for you. It shows the kind of problems you solve, who you solve them for and what it is like to work with you. It answers the quiet questions people are too polite to ask: are you still trading, are you any good, and can you handle something my size. Done well, it turns a lukewarm referral into a booked call.

This matters more for consultants than for almost any other trade, because you are selling judgement rather than a product someone can hold. The website is where you make that invisible thing feel real and specific.

> Prospects check you out before they ever call. Your website decides what they find.

## What a consulting website actually needs to do

A consultant's site does not need to be big. It needs to be clear and to earn trust quickly. Most of the ones that work well cover the same handful of jobs without any padding.

- **Say who you help and how** in plain language, above the fold, so nobody has to guess what you do.
- **Prove it** with real outcomes, case studies or the logos of sectors you have worked in, rather than vague talk about synergy and value.
- **Show the person**, because clients hire you, not a faceless firm. A proper photo and a straight-talking about page do a lot of heavy lifting.
- **Make the next step obvious** with one clear way to get in touch, not a maze of forms and phone numbers.
- **Load fast and read well on a phone**, since a good share of that late-night name-checking happens on mobile.

## Looking credible is a competitive advantage

In consulting, your competition is often another individual or a small firm who does broadly what you do. When two consultants look similar on paper, the one whose website feels considered and current usually wins the meeting. Fair or not, people read a sharp site as a sign of a sharp mind.

It also lets you charge what you are worth. A prospect who arrives via a professional, confident website has already decided you operate at a certain level, so the conversation about fees starts from a stronger place. A weak site does the opposite: it invites people to haggle, because nothing about it signals that you are in demand.

You do not need to spend a fortune to get there. A focused, well-built site does more than a sprawling one. If you want a sense of what that costs, our [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and published up front, so there are no surprises and no hourly meter running.

## A site that keeps working after launch

The other reason consultants need a professional website is momentum. A good site is not a brochure you print once and forget. It is a base you build on, publishing the odd article, adding a new case study, sharpening the way you describe a service as your focus shifts.

That is also where being found comes in. When someone searches for the exact problem you solve, you want to be on the page. Sensible [SEO](/services/seo) and a proper Google Business Profile mean you catch demand that has nothing to do with your existing network, which is how you stop relying entirely on referrals.

The trick is keeping it current without it becoming a second job. A simple admin panel and a light [care plan](/services/care-plans) mean small changes take minutes and the site stays fast, secure and up to date while you get on with the actual consulting.

## Where to start if your site is holding you back

If any of this stung a little, you are not alone. Plenty of excellent consultants are quietly losing work to a website that does not match the quality of their thinking. The good news is that it is a straightforward fix, and often a smaller job than people expect.

A clear, honest [website](/services/website-design) that says who you help, proves you can do it and makes contacting you easy will pay for itself in the clients it stops you from losing. Most of the ones we build for consultants and other [professional services](/industries) go live in three to six weeks, and you own the site, domain and data outright.

If you already have a site and are not sure whether it is helping or quietly costing you, the simplest next step is to have someone look at it properly and tell you the truth.

## The quick wins

- Referrals still check you out online first, so your website decides whether a warm introduction turns into a booked call.
- A consulting site only needs a few pages, but each must say who you help, prove it and make contact obvious.
- A credible, current site lets you compete on quality and protect your fees instead of being haggled down.
- Fixed-price builds from £477 go live in three to six weeks, and you own the site, domain and data.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do consultants really need a website, or is LinkedIn enough?

LinkedIn is useful, but you don't control it, you can't shape it the way you'd shape your own site, and it looks the same as every other profile. A professional website is the one place online that is entirely yours, where you can prove your expertise on your own terms and send prospects to a page built to win the work. Think of LinkedIn as the introduction and your website as the interview.

### What should a consultant's website include?

At a minimum: a clear statement of who you help and how, proof in the form of case studies or outcomes, a genuine about page with a photo, and one obvious way to get in touch. It should load quickly, read well on a phone and feel current. You do not need endless pages, you need a small number of pages that each do a job.

### How much does a professional website for a consultant cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. They start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, depending on how much you need. Care plans to keep everything running start at £27 a month. If you want ongoing SEO or a redesign of an existing site, we quote that per project. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

### How long does it take to build?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how quickly we can agree the content and get your feedback. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make changes yourself afterwards, plus 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### I already have a website. How do I know if it is any good?

The quickest way is a free teardown. We record a short screen review of your current site and walk through what is working, what is quietly costing you clients and what to fix first. There is no obligation and no jargon, just a straight assessment you can act on whether you work with us or not.

bodyMarkdown:
Most people looking for a fresh cut or a set of nails start the same way: phone in hand, thumb scrolling. When it comes to websites for beauty salons and barbers, that single habit changes everything. Your site isn't a brochure anymore — it's the moment someone decides whether to book with you or tap back to the next result. Get it right and your chair fills itself. Get it wrong and you're leaning on walk-ins and word of mouth alone.

The good news is you don't need a sprawling site or a big budget. You need a fast, mobile-friendly page that answers a few key questions and lets people book in seconds. Here's what actually works.

## What websites for beauty salons and barbers need to do

Strip away the fancy stuff and a salon or barber site has one job: turn a curious scroll into a booked appointment. Everything else supports that. Before you think about colours or fonts, make sure the basics are nailed. Within a few seconds, a visitor should be able to work out what you offer, roughly what it costs, and how to grab a slot.

Nail these first, and the rest of the site has room to shine:

- **A booking button that follows them** — visible the moment the page loads and again at the bottom, so nobody has to hunt for it.
- **Clear prices** — even a simple 'from' list stops the endless 'how much is a balayage?' messages and filters out time-wasters.
- **Opening hours and location** — with a tap-to-call number and a map link for anyone deciding right now.
- **Real photos of your work** — not stock images of someone else's salon.
- **A few honest reviews** — social proof does the convincing you can't do yourself.

## Make booking the easiest thing on the page

Most local searches happen on a phone, often in a spare five minutes between other things. If booking means emailing you and waiting for a reply, you've lost people who would happily have paid. Connect your site to a booking tool — Fresha, Treatwell, Booksy, or a simple built-in form — so someone can pick a stylist, choose a time and confirm without leaving your page.

Whatever you use, the flow should be obvious. One clear button, as few taps as possible, and a confirmation they can trust. A good [website design](/services/website-design) puts that booking step front and centre on every screen, not buried on a 'contact' page two clicks deep.

## Let your photos do the selling

Beauty and barbering are visual trades. Nobody books a fade or a set of extensions on words alone — they want to see the finish. Your gallery is your strongest sales tool, so treat it like one. Clear, well-lit photos of real cuts, colours and treatments beat any amount of clever copy.

You don't need a professional shoot every week. A tidy, consistent set of phone photos, taken in good light against a clean background, does the job. Keep it current, and show the range of what you do — a barber who only posts skin fades won't win the beard-trim crowd. To see how other trades present their work, our [recent projects](/work) show the kind of clean, photo-led layouts that convert.

> In this trade your gallery does more selling than any headline ever will.

## Show up when locals search

Someone typing 'barber near me' or 'gel nails in Leeds' is ready to book today. Local SEO is how you land in front of them. Start with your Google Business Profile: complete every field, add photos, and keep your name, address and phone number identical everywhere they appear online. Google rewards that consistency.

Your website backs the profile up. Mention the areas you serve, the treatments you specialise in, and answer the questions people actually ask. Done well, [local SEO](/services/seo) brings a steady stream of nearby customers without paying for every click. It's slower than ads, but it compounds over time.

## Fast, secure and actually yours

A salon site that takes five seconds to load on mobile loses bookings before the photos even appear. Speed matters, and so does trust — a secure, up-to-date site reassures people handing over their details. Just as important, the site should belong to you: your domain, your images, your customer data, with no lock-in if you ever decide to move on.

That's why we build on fixed prices with no hourly billing, and offer simple [care plans](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month to keep everything fast, backed up and current. If you're weighing up the numbers, our [pricing](/pricing) sits on one page — websites start at £477, and most go live in three to six weeks. No surprises, no drawn-out projects.

## The quick wins

- Put an obvious booking button on every screen — most salon visitors are on their phone and ready to act.
- Let real photos of your work carry the sale, and keep the gallery current and varied.
- A complete Google Business Profile plus a fast, mobile-friendly site is how locals find you.
- Choose fixed-price and full ownership so your site, domain and customer data stay yours.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a salon or barbershop cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost before we start. They begin at **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete, with the right tier depending on how many services, pages and booking features you need. Care plans to keep everything running start at **£27 a month**, and SEO or a redesign is quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build?

Most salon and barber sites go live in **three to six weeks**. The timing depends mostly on how quickly we get your photos, service list and prices — the build itself moves fast once we have your content. Every site includes a simple admin panel so you can update prices and hours yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare.

### Do I need online booking, or is a contact form enough?

A contact form works, but online booking almost always wins. People browsing at 9pm want to confirm a slot then and there, not wait for a reply. We can connect popular tools like Fresha, Treatwell or Booksy, or build a simple booking form into your site — whichever suits how you run your diary.

### Can I update the photos and prices myself?

Yes. Every build includes a straightforward admin panel, so you can swap in new photos, tweak your price list or change opening hours without calling anyone. If you'd rather not touch it, a care plan means we handle those updates for you.

### Will a new website help me rank on Google?

A fast, well-structured site gives you a strong foundation, but ranking locally also depends on your Google Business Profile, reviews and consistent business details. We set the site up properly and can help with local SEO — though nobody can honestly promise a specific position, and anyone guaranteeing the top spot is worth avoiding.

intro: Most people looking for a fresh cut or a set of nails start the same way: phone in hand, thumb scrolling. When it comes to websites for beauty salons and barbers, that single habit changes everything. Your site isn't a brochure anymore — it's the moment someone decides whether to book with you or tap back to the next result. Get it right and your chair fills itself. Get it wrong and you're leaning on walk-ins and word of mouth alone.
The good news is you don't need a sprawling site or a big budget. You need a fast, mobile-friendly page that answers a few key questions and lets people book in seconds. Here's what actually works.

sections.h2: What websites for beauty salons and barbers need to do
paragraphs: Strip away the fancy stuff and a salon or barber site has one job: turn a curious scroll into a booked appointment. Everything else supports that. Before you think about colours or fonts, make sure the basics are nailed. Within a few seconds, a visitor should be able to work out what you offer, roughly what it costs, and how to grab a slot.
Nail these first, and the rest of the site has room to shine:
bullets: **A booking button that follows them** — visible the moment the page loads and again at the bottom, so nobody has to hunt for it.
**Clear prices** — even a simple 'from' list stops the endless 'how much is a balayage?' messages and filters out time-wasters.
**Opening hours and location** — with a tap-to-call number and a map link for anyone deciding right now.
**Real photos of your work** — not stock images of someone else's salon.
**A few honest reviews** — social proof does the convincing you can't do yourself.

sections.h2: Make booking the easiest thing on the page
paragraphs: Most local searches happen on a phone, often in a spare five minutes between other things. If booking means emailing you and waiting for a reply, you've lost people who would happily have paid. Connect your site to a booking tool — Fresha, Treatwell, Booksy, or a simple built-in form — so someone can pick a stylist, choose a time and confirm without leaving your page.

Whatever you use, the flow should be obvious. One clear button, as few taps as possible, and a confirmation they can trust. A good [website design](/services/website-design) puts that booking step front and centre on every screen, not buried on a 'contact' page two clicks deep.

sections.h2: Let your photos do the selling
paragraphs: Beauty and barbering are visual trades. Nobody books a fade or a set of extensions on words alone — they want to see the finish. Your gallery is your strongest sales tool, so treat it like one. Clear, well-lit photos of real cuts, colours and treatments beat any amount of clever copy.

You don't need a professional shoot every week. A tidy, consistent set of phone photos, taken in good light against a clean background, does the job. Keep it current, and show the range of what you do — a barber who only posts skin fades won't win the beard-trim crowd. To see how other trades present their work, our [recent projects](/work) show the kind of clean, photo-led layouts that convert.
sections.pullquote: In this trade your gallery does more selling than any headline ever will.

sections.h2: Show up when locals search
paragraphs: Someone typing 'barber near me' or 'gel nails in Leeds' is ready to book today. Local SEO is how you land in front of them. Start with your Google Business Profile: complete every field, add photos, and keep your name, address and phone number identical everywhere they appear online. Google rewards that consistency.

Your website backs the profile up. Mention the areas you serve, the treatments you specialise in, and answer the questions people actually ask. Done well, [local SEO](/services/seo) brings a steady stream of nearby customers without paying for every click. It's slower than ads, but it compounds over time.

sections.h2: Fast, secure and actually yours
paragraphs: A salon site that takes five seconds to load on mobile loses bookings before the photos even appear. Speed matters, and so does trust — a secure, up-to-date site reassures people handing over their details. Just as important, the site should belong to you: your domain, your images, your customer data, with no lock-in if you ever decide to move on.

That's why we build on fixed prices with no hourly billing, and offer simple [care plans](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month to keep everything fast, backed up and current. If you're weighing up the numbers, our [pricing](/pricing) sits on one page — websites start at £477, and most go live in three to six weeks. No surprises, no drawn-out projects.

keyTakeaways: Put an obvious booking button on every screen — most salon visitors are on their phone and ready to act.
Let real photos of your work carry the sale, and keep the gallery current and varied.
A complete Google Business Profile plus a fast, mobile-friendly site is how locals find you.
Choose fixed-price and full ownership so your site, domain and customer data stay yours.

bodyMarkdown:
A homeowner weighing up a loft conversion or a side-return extension will look you up long before they pick up the phone. They want to see the last kitchen you fitted, the garden wall you rebuilt, the tired terrace you brought back to life. That is why learning how builders can showcase their projects online matters almost as much as the quality of the work itself. Your finished jobs are the strongest proof you can offer, and your website is where a stranger decides whether to trust you with their home.

The trouble is that most builders let that proof go to waste. The work is first class, but the photos sit on a phone camera roll or scroll past on a Facebook page nobody looks back through. This guide walks you through getting your best projects in front of the right people, in plain English, so a quiet browse turns into a booked quote.

## Why your finished jobs are your best salesperson

Building work is a big, nervy purchase. People are handing over thousands of pounds and letting a team into their home for weeks, so they buy on trust and proof far more than on price. A wall of real, finished projects does more to earn that trust than any amount of copy about being reliable and professional.

Word of mouth still brings in plenty of work, but even a warm referral checks you online before they call. If a neighbour recommends you and your site shows ten sharp before-and-after jobs, the decision is half made before you speak. If it shows nothing, or a single blurry photo from 2019, that momentum quietly stalls. It is worth a look at how a clear gallery is laid out on our **[recent work](/work)** pages before you plan your own.

## How builders can showcase their projects online

The heart of it is simple: treat every good job as a short story. Not just a photo, but the problem the customer had, what you did, and the result they were left with. That structure turns a flat gallery into something a prospective client can picture themselves in. Here is what a project worth showing usually includes:

- **A clear before and after.** The transformation is the whole point, so shoot the same angle before you start and again once you finish.
- **A short, honest write-up.** Three or four sentences on the brief, the work and any hurdle you solved. Skip the jargon.
- **The location, roughly.** Saying a job was in Harrogate or Sale helps nearby customers picture you working near them.
- **The type of work.** Label it plainly, whether it is a loft conversion, a full renovation, an extension or a repair, so people can find work like theirs.
- **One standout detail.** A tricky steel, a reclaimed-brick match, a deadline you hit. Specifics are what make a job memorable.
- **A strong, well-lit finished photo.** One good image beats ten dark, cluttered ones every time.

## Photograph the work so it sells

You do not need a professional camera. A modern phone is more than enough, but a few habits make a real difference. Shoot in daylight, tidy the space first, and take the after photo from the same spot as the before so the change lands. Get the whole room or elevation in frame rather than a close-up of a single skirting board.

Take more photos than you think you need, on every single job, even the ones you are not sure you will use. A finished project you forgot to photograph is a sale you can never make twice. Build the habit into the work and your gallery grows on its own, without a marketing budget.

> Your camera roll is a marketing budget you have already paid for. Most builders just never spend it.

## Turn your gallery into enquiries

A handsome gallery that nobody acts on is just a scrapbook. The job of the page is to move an impressed visitor to get in touch, so make that effortless. Put a clear way to enquire beside your best work, add click-to-call on mobile, and pull your Google reviews onto the page so proof and praise sit side by side.

It also helps to be upfront about how you work and roughly what things cost. A simple extensions-from figure, or a short note on your process, filters out the time-wasters and reassures the serious ones. When you brief a studio to build this properly, a good **[website design and build](/services/website-design)** puts the enquiry button exactly where the interest peaks.

## Help the right people find your projects on Google

Great projects only win work if people can find them. When you write up each job with the type of work and the town it was in, you are quietly feeding the exact phrases people search, like loft conversion Chorlton or garage conversion near me. That is the everyday engine of **[local SEO](/services/seo)**, and it rewards builders who add jobs regularly far more than those who built a site once and left it.

Back it up with a complete Google Business Profile: keep your details right, add photos of recent jobs, and reply to every review. Over time that is what earns you a spot in the local map results, where a big share of these searches land. We cannot promise a top ranking, and you should be wary of anyone who does, but consistent, well-labelled project pages are how you give yourself the best shot.

## What a project-led website costs

You can start today for nothing, simply by photographing every job properly and keeping a running folder. When you are ready for a site that shows the work off well and brings in enquiries, we build to fixed prices so you know the full cost up front, with no hourly billing. Sites start at £477 for a Starter build, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for a Complete site, and the full breakdown is on our **[pricing](/pricing)** page.

Most builds go live in three to six weeks, you own the site, domain and data outright, and every build comes with a simple admin panel so you can add new projects yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare. If you would rather keep your hands on the tools, a **[care plan](/services/care-plans)** from £27 a month keeps everything updated and lets us post your latest jobs for you.

## The quick wins

- Treat every finished job as a short story: the before, the after, and the result you delivered.
- Photograph every project in daylight from consistent angles, even the jobs you might not use.
- Put a clear way to enquire and your genuine reviews right next to your best work.
- Label each job by type and location so nearby customers can find work like theirs on Google.
- A fixed-price, project-led site starts at £477 and lets you add new jobs yourself.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a building firm cost?

We work to fixed prices, so you know the full cost up front with no hourly billing. Websites start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for a Complete build, with SEO and redesigns quoted per project. Care plans to keep everything running and to add new projects start at £27 a month.

### How long does it take to build my website?

Most sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many projects and photos you want to show and how quickly you can send them over. We agree the timeline before we start, so there are no surprises.

### How many projects should I put on my website?

Quality beats quantity. Six to ten of your strongest, well-photographed jobs make a far better impression than fifty average ones. Start with your best work, then keep adding a new project each time you finish one so the gallery stays fresh.

### What if I do not have good photos of my past jobs?

Start from your very next job. A modern phone is all you need, and a habit of shooting before, during and after quickly builds a gallery. In the meantime, book a free teardown and we will show you how to make the most of the photos you already have.

### Do I have to add new projects myself, or can you do it?

Either works. Every build comes with a simple admin panel so you can upload new jobs in minutes, with no code involved. If you would rather stay on the tools, a care plan means we post your latest projects for you.

bodyMarkdown:
Selling online used to mean a big budget and a developer on speed dial. Not any more. Whether you run a shop on a busy high street or pack orders from a spare room, the e-commerce essentials for small UK retailers are more within reach than they have ever been. But there is still a handful of things you have to get right, or you quietly lose sales you never even see.

This guide walks through what actually matters: a shop that works on a phone, payments and delivery people trust, product pages that turn up in search, and a setup you can run without a full-time team. No jargon, and no upselling you into features you will never use.

## Start with a shop that works on a phone

Picture how your customers actually shop. They are on the bus, in a queue, half-watching the telly, and they are almost always on a phone. If your online shop is fiddly on a small screen, slow to load, or asks people to pinch and zoom to read a price, you lose them before they have added a single thing to the basket.

A good [online shop](/services/website-design) is built mobile-first, which means it is designed for the smallest screen and then scaled up, not the other way round. Buttons are large enough to tap. Images load quickly. The checkout is short and forgiving. None of this is glamorous, but it is where most sales are quietly won or lost.

## The e-commerce essentials for small UK retailers, in plain English

Strip away the buzzwords and the list of things you genuinely need is short. Get these right and you are already ahead of most independent shops online:

You do not need every feature under the sun. A tight shop that does the basics well will out-sell a bloated one every time.

- **A fast, mobile-first shop** — quick to load and easy to use with one thumb.
- **Trusted payment options** — cards plus Apple Pay and Google Pay at a minimum, so people can pay the way they already do.
- **Clear delivery and returns** — costs and timescales shown up front, before checkout, not buried on a policy page.
- **Strong product pages** — honest descriptions, real photos, and the details people ask about like size, materials and dimensions.
- **Findable in search** — pages structured so Google understands what you sell.
- **A simple back office** — one place to manage stock, orders and prices without calling a developer.

## Payments, delivery and returns that build trust

Most lost sales are not about price. Someone wanted your product, reached the checkout, and then hesitated: the postage was a surprise, the returns policy was vague, or the only payment option was a card they did not have to hand.

Show delivery costs early. If you offer click-and-collect or local delivery, say so plainly. Spell out your returns policy in a sentence a busy person can understand. And offer the payment methods people already trust, because a shopper who can tap Apple Pay in two seconds is far more likely to finish than one hunting for their card.

> People rarely abandon a basket because the price is too high. They abandon it because something made them hesitate.

## Getting found: product pages and local SEO

A beautiful shop with no visitors is an expensive hobby. Getting found is part product pages, part search.

Write product descriptions for humans first, but include the words people actually type. 'Handmade oak dining table' beats 'the Avon, our signature piece'. Give every product a clear title, a proper description and good photography. Then make sure the technical side is sound so search engines can read it, which is where [SEO](/services/seo) earns its keep.

If you also have a physical shop, do not ignore local search. A well-kept Google Business Profile, consistent contact details and a few pages aimed at your town help nearby customers find you when they search for what you sell. Plenty of small retailers get their first online orders from people who are only a few miles away.

## Keep it running without a full-time team

The best shop is one you can actually keep on top of. That means a simple admin panel where you can add a product, change a price or mark something out of stock in a couple of minutes, with no code and no waiting.

It also means having someone in your corner when a plugin needs updating or a payment setting changes. That is what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for: the quiet maintenance that keeps a shop fast, secure and open for business. Just as important, insist on owning your site, your domain and your data, so you are never held hostage by an agency or a platform you have outgrown.

Everything we build is fixed-price, so you know the cost before you commit. You can see where an online shop sits on our [pricing](/pricing) page, and if you would rather talk it through, [get in touch](/contact) and we will point you in the right direction, whether or not you end up working with us.

## The quick wins

- Most of your customers are shopping on a phone, so build mobile-first or lose them at the checkout.
- Surprise postage and vague returns cost more sales than high prices do.
- Own your site, domain and data, and keep it maintained so it stays fast and secure.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does an e-commerce website cost for a small UK retailer?

Our websites are fixed-price so you know the cost up front, with no hourly billing. Prices start from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, and care plans start from £27 a month. A full online shop is usually quoted per project because it depends on how many products and features you need, and we will give you a clear figure before any work begins. You can see the ranges on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

### How long does it take to build an online shop?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how ready your products, photos and copy are. A larger catalogue can take a little longer, but we agree the timeline with you at the start so there are no surprises. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### Do I need a physical shop to sell online?

No. Plenty of small UK retailers sell purely online from home, and an e-commerce site works just as well for them. If you do have a shop, adding local SEO and click-and-collect can bring in nearby customers too, so you get the best of both.

### Which payment methods should my shop offer?

At a minimum, offer card payments alongside Apple Pay and Google Pay, since most people now expect to pay in a couple of taps. Options like PayPal or buy-now-pay-later can help for higher-priced items, but keep it simple. Every extra choice at checkout is one more thing for a shopper to weigh up.

### Will I be able to update products myself?

Yes. Every site we build includes a straightforward admin panel, so you can add a product, change a price or mark something out of stock in minutes without touching any code. If you would rather hand off the upkeep entirely, a care plan covers it.

**Trusted payment options** — cards plus Apple Pay and Google Pay at a minimum, so people can pay the way they already do.
**Clear delivery and returns** — costs and timescales shown up front, before checkout, not buried on a policy page.
**Strong product pages** — honest descriptions, real photos, and the details people ask about like size, materials and dimensions.
**Findable in search** — pages structured so Google understands what you sell.
**A simple back office** — one place to manage stock, orders and prices without calling a developer.

sections.h2: Payments, delivery and returns that build trust
paragraphs: Most lost sales are not about price. Someone wanted your product, reached the checkout, and then hesitated: the postage was a surprise, the returns policy was vague, or the only payment option was a card they did not have to hand.

Show delivery costs early. If you offer click-and-collect or local delivery, say so plainly. Spell out your returns policy in a sentence a busy person can understand. And offer the payment methods people already trust, because a shopper who can tap Apple Pay in two seconds is far more likely to finish than one hunting for their card.
sections.pullquote: People rarely abandon a basket because the price is too high. They abandon it because something made them hesitate.

sections.h2: Getting found: product pages and local SEO
paragraphs: A beautiful shop with no visitors is an expensive hobby. Getting found is part product pages, part search.

Write product descriptions for humans first, but include the words people actually type. 'Handmade oak dining table' beats 'the Avon, our signature piece'. Give every product a clear title, a proper description and good photography. Then make sure the technical side is sound so search engines can read it, which is where [SEO](/services/seo) earns its keep.

If you also have a physical shop, do not ignore local search. A well-kept Google Business Profile, consistent contact details and a few pages aimed at your town help nearby customers find you when they search for what you sell. Plenty of small retailers get their first online orders from people who are only a few miles away.

sections.h2: Keep it running without a full-time team
paragraphs: The best shop is one you can actually keep on top of. That means a simple admin panel where you can add a product, change a price or mark something out of stock in a couple of minutes, with no code and no waiting.

It also means having someone in your corner when a plugin needs updating or a payment setting changes. That is what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for: the quiet maintenance that keeps a shop fast, secure and open for business. Just as important, insist on owning your site, your domain and your data, so you are never held hostage by an agency or a platform you have outgrown.

Everything we build is fixed-price, so you know the cost before you commit. You can see where an online shop sits on our [pricing](/pricing) page, and if you would rather talk it through, [get in touch](/contact) and we will point you in the right direction, whether or not you end up working with us.

keyTakeaways: Most of your customers are shopping on a phone, so build mobile-first or lose them at the checkout.
Surprise postage and vague returns cost more sales than high prices do.
Own your site, domain and data, and keep it maintained so it stays fast and secure.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a gym or train clients one to one, your website really has one job: turn a curious visitor into a booked session. Most of the website tips for personal trainers and gyms you'll find online forget that. They obsess over slick animations and stock photos of dumbbells, when what actually fills your timetable is a clear offer, an easy booking step and honest proof that you get people results.

Here are the changes that make the biggest difference to a fitness website, in plain English, plus how to spot the ones quietly costing you enquiries right now.

## Put booking front and centre

When someone lands on your site, they've usually already decided they want to train. They're just checking you're the right fit and easy to reach, so don't make them hunt. Your main action, whether that's a free consultation, a taster class or a quick call, should sit right at the top of the page and follow them as they scroll down.

Good [website design](/services/website-design) makes that next step obvious on every page, not buried three clicks deep. Put your phone number where a thumb can reach it, keep forms short (name, email and what they're after), and if you use a booking tool, link straight to it rather than asking people to email and wait for a reply.

## The pages every fitness website needs

You don't need twenty pages. You need a handful that answer what a new member is actually thinking. Before you add anything clever, make sure you've nailed the essentials:

Get these right and most visitors will have everything they need to say yes.

- A clear services or **membership** page that lists what you offer and roughly what it costs
- A simple booking or enquiry step that works in a couple of taps
- An about page with real photos of you and your space, not stock imagery
- A timetable or class schedule people can actually read on a phone
- Genuine reviews and results from members who have trained with you

## Don't hide your prices

Fitness is one of the few trades where hiding prices is still normal, and it quietly costs you good enquiries. People assume the worst, or they simply move on to a competitor who is upfront. You don't have to publish every package or session rate, but give a starting point, a range or a clear 'from' figure so visitors can picture whether you're the right fit before they get in touch.

The trainers who do this well tend to hear from warmer, better-qualified people who are already close to booking, rather than tyre-kickers fishing for a number. Being open about money builds trust before you've even spoken.

## Website tips for personal trainers and gyms that help you get found

A beautiful site nobody sees won't fill your classes. When someone nearby searches for a personal trainer or a gym in your area, you want to be one of the names they see, and most of those searches happen on a phone, sometimes while they're walking past your door.

That comes down to local SEO: a fully filled-out Google Business Profile, the same name, address and phone number everywhere online, real reviews, and pages written around what people actually type in. It's worth investing in proper [local SEO](/services/seo) once your site is solid, because it keeps working long after the launch buzz fades.

> If your gym doesn't show up when someone searches for a trainer nearby, it doesn't matter how polished your website looks.

## Show proof, without the hype

People are trusting you with their body and their time, so proof matters more than promises. Skip the guaranteed-results language and let real members do the talking. A few honest testimonials, genuine before-and-after photos you have permission to use, and clear detail about your qualifications will beat a page of superlatives every time.

If you're just starting out and don't have testimonials yet, be upfront about it. Show your training style, your space and what a first session actually looks like. People warm to honesty far more quickly than they warm to hype.

## Keep it fast, mobile and up to date

Most people will open your site on a phone between other things, so it has to load quickly and read well on a small screen. A slow, cluttered site loses enquiries you'll never even hear about. Compress your images, keep the layout simple, and test every page on your own phone before you call it finished.

Your timetable, prices and offers change, so you need to update them without paying someone each time. A simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps your site fast, secure and current, and it's worth checking our [pricing](/pricing) so you know the cost up front instead of guessing. Owning your site, domain and data also means you're never held hostage by whoever built it.

## The quick wins

- Make booking or enquiring the single most obvious thing on every page.
- Be upfront about prices to attract warmer, better-qualified enquiries.
- Show up locally with a strong Google Business Profile and honest reviews.
- Keep it fast and mobile-friendly, and make sure you can update it yourself.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a personal trainer or gym cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost before we start and there's no hourly billing. They start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for a Complete build, with care plans from £27 a month if you'd like us to keep everything running. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build?

Most fitness websites we build go live within 3 to 6 weeks, depending on how much content and photography is ready. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can update your timetable and prices yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

### Do I need a booking system built into my website?

Not necessarily. Plenty of trainers and gyms do well with a short enquiry form or a link to a booking tool they already use. The important thing is that booking or getting in touch takes a couple of taps, not a hunt through several pages.

### Will a new website get me to the top of Google?

No honest agency can promise you the top spot or guaranteed rankings. What a good site does is give you the foundation to compete: fast, mobile-friendly pages, clear local information and a Google Business Profile that's properly set up. Local SEO then builds your visibility over time.

### Can I update my own timetable and prices?

Yes. Every site we build includes a simple admin panel, and you own your site, domain and data outright, so you're never locked in. If you'd rather not touch it, a care plan means we handle updates, security and backups for you.
