bodyMarkdown:
If a patient can't book you in under a minute, they book someone else. That's the reality behind good websites for physiotherapists and private clinics: your site isn't a brochure, it's your busiest receptionist. It answers questions at ten at night, takes bookings while you're treating patients, and quietly decides whether a first-time visitor trusts you before they've ever met you. Get it right and it fills your diary. Get it wrong and it hands warm enquiries to the clinic down the road.

The encouraging part is that a clinic website doesn't need to be complicated to work hard. It needs a clear path to booking, honest proof that you know what you're doing, and enough local signals for Google to show you to nearby patients. Nail those three and everything else is detail. Here's how to do it without wasting money.

## What websites for physiotherapists and private clinics really need

A physio clinic isn't an online shop or a national brand, and it shouldn't be built like one. You serve people within a few miles who are often in pain, mid-decision and browsing on a phone between appointments. That changes the job your site has to do. Every element should shorten the distance between 'my back is killing me' and 'I've booked in for Tuesday'. Strip out the stock photos of strangers, the clinical jargon about modalities and manual techniques, and the ten-item menu that buries what matters. Lead with what you treat, who you help and how to book — in that order.

## The pages every clinic website needs

You don't need forty pages. You need a handful that each earn their place. For most physiotherapy and private clinics, that means:

- **A homepage** that states your main treatments, your area and a booking button within the first screen — no scrolling required.
- **A page for each core treatment** — back pain, sports injuries, post-operative rehab, women's health — so patients and Google can find the specific thing they searched for.
- **An honest pricing page**. Clinics that show their prices win trust and filter out enquiries they can't help; hiding cost just adds friction.
- **An about page with real faces**, qualifications and registrations such as HCPC and CSP membership — the proof you're a safe pair of hands.
- **A contact page** with your address, an embedded map, parking and transport notes, and how to reach you outside opening hours.

## Make booking effortless

Booking is where most clinic websites quietly leak patients. If your only option is 'call us', you lose everyone browsing after hours, everyone who dislikes the phone, and everyone comparing three clinics at once — and that's most of them. Put a clear booking button in your header, at the foot of every treatment page and in the footer. If you use practice-management software with online scheduling, link straight to it. If you still take bookings by hand, a short form that drops into your inbox beats a phone number on its own. The rule never changes: never make a ready-to-book patient go hunting.

> Most local searches happen on a phone, often outside working hours. If booking needs a phone call, you're shut at the exact moment patients are ready.

## Getting found when patients search locally

Showing up for 'physiotherapist near me' or 'sports massage in your town' comes down to a few fundamentals done properly. Strong [local SEO](/services/seo) means a claimed and complete Google Business Profile, the same name, address and phone number everywhere they appear online, a dedicated page for each treatment and each area you cover, and a steady trickle of genuine reviews. Technical basics matter just as much: a fast site that behaves well on a phone ranks better and converts better at the same time. None of this is magic, and no honest agency can promise you the number-one spot — but the fundamentals move you up reliably and keep you there.

## Trust does most of the selling

Health is personal. Before someone trusts you with their injury, they want to know you're qualified, established and easy to deal with. So show it plainly: your team's names and photographs, professional registrations, the conditions you treat, clear pricing and reviews from real patients. Lose the generic stock imagery and the vague promises, because patients see straight through them. A calm, clear, well-built [physiotherapy website](/services/website-design) says 'these are professionals' before a single word is read. Keeping it accurate matters too — a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps your hours, prices and team details right without you ever touching the code.

## Fixed-price, and you own everything

Clinic owners are stretched thin, so the last thing you need is a web project that drifts for months or a bill you can't predict. We work to a fixed price, so you know the full cost before we start, and most sites go live in three to six weeks. You own your site, your domain and your data — no lock-in and no one holding your business hostage. Our [fixed-price packages](/pricing) start at £477 for a Starter site, with Growth and Complete options for busier practices, and every build includes a simple admin panel and thirty days of aftercare. Physiotherapy clinics are just one of the many [local businesses we help](/industries) get online properly.

## The quick wins

- Your website's main job is turning a searching patient into a booked appointment — every page should serve that.
- Show real credentials, faces and prices; trust is what converts nervous, in-pain visitors into bookings.
- Get the local SEO fundamentals right, keep the site fast on mobile, and make booking a single tap.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website for a physiotherapy clinic cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost before we start — no hourly billing. Starter sites begin at £477, Growth at £1,170 and Complete at £2,370, depending on how many treatments and pages you need. Care plans to keep everything updated and secure start at £27 a month, and SEO or full redesigns are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build a clinic website?

Most clinic sites go live in three to six weeks. The timeline depends mainly on how quickly we can gather your treatment details, photos and reviews, and how many pages you need. We agree the scope up front, so there are no surprises, and every build includes thirty days of aftercare once you're live.

### Do I need a separate page for each treatment?

Usually, yes. A dedicated page for back pain, sports injuries, post-op rehab and so on helps patients find exactly what they searched for and gives Google a clear reason to rank you for that term. It also lets you explain each treatment properly, which builds trust and answers questions before the phone rings.

### Can I update the website myself?

Yes. Every site we build includes a simple admin panel, so you can change opening hours, prices, staff and treatments without touching code. If you'd rather not think about it at all, a care plan means we handle updates, backups and security for a fixed monthly fee.

### Will a new website guarantee more patients?

No one can honestly guarantee rankings or a specific number of bookings, and you should be wary of anyone who does. What a well-built, fast, trustworthy site does is remove the obstacles between a searching patient and a booked appointment — and give the local SEO fundamentals room to work.

bodyMarkdown:
The big chains have deep pockets, warehouses full of stock and marketing budgets you could never hope to match. So it is easy to assume the fight for online customers is already lost before you have even started. It isn't. The real question of how local shops can compete with big retailers online has a surprisingly practical answer, and almost none of it depends on outspending Amazon.

You win by being the one thing the giants can never be: close, personal and genuinely part of the community you serve. Your website's job is to make that obvious in the handful of seconds someone spends deciding where to buy. Here is how to do exactly that, without a huge budget or a full-time marketing team.

## Stop trying to beat them at their own game

The mistake most independent retailers make online is trying to look like a shrunken version of a national chain. You copy their layout, chase the same broad keywords and try to compete on headline price. That is a race you simply cannot win, because the big retailers have already bought every advantage that money can buy. Meet them on their terms and you will always finish second.

Your edge is everything they quietly gave up in order to get big. You know your regulars by name. You can recommend the right product in thirty seconds flat. You can hold something behind the counter until Saturday. None of that fits neatly in a warehouse spreadsheet, and all of it is worth putting front and centre online. Our notes by [industry](/industries) show how different kinds of shop lean on these same strengths.

## Win the 'near me' searches the giants can't own

Most local searches happen on a phone, often from someone standing a few streets away who wants to buy today, not next week. When they type 'hardware shop near me' or 'florist open now', Google leans heavily on local signals rather than raw advertising budget. This is the one arena where a small shop can genuinely outrank a national brand, because relevance and proximity beat spend.

Claim and fully complete your Google Business Profile, keep your name, address and phone number identical everywhere they appear, and gather honest reviews from happy customers. Then back all of it up with a website that tells Google exactly where you are and what you sell. Getting your [local SEO](/services/seo) in order is usually the fastest and cheapest way to appear in front of nearby buyers before the chains do.

## Turn your website into your best salesperson

Your shop closes at five; your website never does. For a growing number of customers it is the very first thing they meet, and it is quietly answering the questions that decide whether they visit, call, or scroll straight on to a competitor. A tired, slow or confusing site sends them right back to the retailer whose name they already trust.

A shop website that earns its keep does a handful of simple things really well:

You do not need a sprawling online store to pull this off. A clear, well-built [shop website](/services/website-design) that loads quickly and tells the honest truth about your business will out-convert a bloated, cluttered one every single time.

- Loads fast on a phone and looks tidy on a small screen, because that is where most people will find you.
- Shows your opening hours, address and a map without anyone having to dig around for them.
- Makes your phone number tappable, so calling you takes one thumb rather than a copy-and-paste.
- Explains what makes you different in plain words: local delivery, expert advice, click-and-collect, or stock the chains never carry.
- Uses real photos of your shop, your team and your products instead of stock images anyone else could use too.

> Your shop closes at five. A good website keeps selling all night.

## How local shops can compete with big retailers online on a budget

Here is the genuinely reassuring part: none of this needs a chain-sized budget. You are not trying to build the next Amazon or ship parcels across the country overnight. You are trying to be the obvious choice for the people who live and work nearby, and that is a far cheaper thing to win.

Put your money where it actually moves the needle: a fast, mobile-friendly site, a well-tended Google Business Profile and a steady trickle of reviews and fresh updates. Our [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and published up front, so you know the cost before you commit and never get a surprise invoice halfway through. Keep it simple, keep it consistent, and let the giants pay for the expensive stuff.

## Show up consistently, not just once

Big retailers win partly through sheer repetition — they are always there, always visible. You can borrow the very same habit at a tiny fraction of the effort. Post to your Google Business Profile and social channels whenever something changes, reply to every review, and refresh your site when your range or opening hours do. A shop that looks alive and current earns trust that a polished-but-abandoned page never will.

If keeping on top of all that sounds like one job too many on an already long list, a simple [care plan](/services/care-plans) takes the updates, backups and small fixes off your plate for a set monthly fee. Your site stays quick, current and secure without eating into your evenings.

## The quick wins

- You cannot outspend big retailers, but you can out-local them on the searches that matter most.
- A fast, mobile-friendly website with clear location details often beats a chain's bloated one for nearby buyers.
- Consistency — reviews, updates and a well-kept Google Business Profile — builds the trust that wins repeat custom.

## Frequently asked questions

### Can a local shop really outrank big retailers on Google?

For broad, national searches, no — the chains have too much authority. But for local, intent-heavy searches like 'near me' or 'open now', proximity and relevance matter far more than budget, and that is exactly where a well-optimised local shop can appear above them.

### Do I need a full online shop to compete?

Not always. Plenty of independent retailers win with a simple, fast website that shows their range, location and reasons to visit, plus click-and-collect or local delivery. Full e-commerce is worth it when you genuinely want to sell and ship online, not just because a chain does.

### How much does a shop website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price and start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or a redesign is quoted per project. You always know the price before we begin — no hourly billing and no surprises.

### How long does it take to build?

Most sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your photos and content. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you're live, and you own your site, domain and data outright.

### What is a free teardown?

It's a short, screen-recorded review of your current website where we point out what's quietly costing you customers and what to fix first. It's free, there's no obligation, and you keep the recording whether or not you decide to work with us.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a shop, a salon or a trades business, most of your customers are searching for you on a phone, often within a few miles of your front door. Local SEO for small businesses explained in plain terms is simply this: the work that helps your business appear when someone nearby searches for what you sell. Get it right and a search for 'emergency plumber' turns into a booked job. Get it wrong and the competitor two streets away takes the call instead.

The good news is that local search is one of the few arenas where a small, independent business can genuinely outrank a national chain. You do not need a big budget or a marketing department. You need a handful of the right things done properly and kept up to date. Here is how it all works, without the jargon.

## Local SEO for small businesses, explained simply

Local SEO is search engine optimisation aimed at a place rather than the whole country. When someone types 'barber in Leeds' or taps 'dentist near me', Google tries to show the most useful nearby options. In practice that means the little map with three businesses pinned to it, plus the ordinary blue links underneath. Local SEO is everything you do to earn a spot in those results.

It matters because the intent behind the search is so strong. Someone looking up 'locksmith near me' at nine on a Friday night is not browsing — they are ready to pay, right now. Being visible at that exact moment is worth far more than any amount of vague brand awareness. And because you are only competing with other businesses in your area, the hill you are climbing is much smaller than it first appears.

## The three things Google weighs up

Google will never hand out an exact formula, but it has been open about the three factors that drive local rankings. Almost everything worth doing falls under one of them:

- **Relevance** — how well your business matches what the person typed. A profile that clearly says you are a wedding photographer beats a vague 'creative studio' every time.
- **Distance** — how close you are to the searcher, or to the place they named. You cannot move your premises, but you can make sure Google knows exactly where you are.
- **Prominence** — how well known and well regarded you appear to be, judged on reviews, mentions across the web and the quality of your own website.

> Most local businesses that struggle are simply leaving easy points on the table in one of these three areas.

## Your Google Business Profile does the heavy lifting

If you do one thing after reading this, claim and complete your Google Business Profile — the free listing that feeds the map and the 'near me' results. It is the single biggest lever most small businesses have, and it is astonishing how many sit half-finished or are left to go stale for years.

Fill in every field honestly and in full: your categories, opening hours, service area, products and a genuine description of what you actually do. Add real photos of your premises, your team and your work — not stock images. Then keep it alive. Answer questions, post the occasional update and, above all, reply to your reviews. A steady trickle of recent, honest reviews with thoughtful replies is one of the strongest signals you can send Google that you are a real, active, trusted business.

## Simple local SEO wins you can start this month

You do not need to do everything at once. A few consistent habits will move the needle further than one big burst of effort you never repeat. Start here:

- Make sure your business name, address and phone number are written identically everywhere online — your site, your profile, directories and social pages.
- Ask happy customers for a review while the job is still fresh in their mind, and make it effortless with a direct link.
- Give each main service its own page on your website, so 'boiler repair' and 'bathroom fitting' each have somewhere relevant to land.
- Mention the towns and neighbourhoods you genuinely serve in your copy, naturally, rather than stuffing in a long list of place names.
- Get listed in a few trusted local and industry directories that fit your trade.

## Where your website comes in

Your Google Business Profile gets you found, but your website is where a visitor decides whether to trust you. A slow, cluttered or fiddly-on-mobile site quietly undoes all your hard work. Since most local searches happen on a phone, a fast site that loads cleanly and makes it obvious how to call or book is not a nice-to-have — it is the difference between an enquiry and a bounce.

This is where good [website design](/services/website-design) and focused [local SEO](/services/seo) work hand in hand. If you serve a particular trade it also pays to speak your customers' language — the way a dentist's patients search is nothing like the way a builder's clients do, which is why we tailor sites by [industry](/industries). And because everything we do is [fixed-price](/pricing), you always know the cost before any work begins. If you would like a straight, honest second opinion on where your current site stands, a free teardown is a short screen-recorded review with no strings attached.

## The quick wins

- Local SEO helps you show up when nearby customers search for what you sell — where buying intent is at its highest.
- Google ranks local results on relevance, distance and prominence, and you can influence all three.
- A complete, active Google Business Profile is the single biggest lever for most small businesses.
- A fast, mobile-friendly website turns the visibility you earn into actual enquiries.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does local SEO cost?

We do not bill by the hour. Websites are fixed-price from £477 for a Starter build, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, and ongoing SEO is quoted per project once we have seen your site and your local market — so you always know the number before we start. Care plans, which keep everything updated and running, begin at £27 a month.

### How long does local SEO take to work?

Local SEO is a slow build rather than a switch you flip. Expect anywhere from a few weeks to a few months for meaningful movement, depending on how competitive your area and trade are. If a new website is part of the plan, most of ours go live in three to six weeks.

### Can I do local SEO myself?

Yes, and you should start today. Claiming and fully completing your Google Business Profile, keeping your name, address and phone number consistent, and steadily gathering honest reviews will get most small businesses a long way at no cost. The point where it usually pays to bring in help is the website and the ongoing upkeep.

### Do I need a brand-new website for local SEO?

Not always. If your current site is reasonably fast and works well on mobile, it may just need better structure and service pages. If it is slow, dated or hard to use on a phone, that will be holding you back — a free teardown will tell you honestly which camp you are in.

faq.q: Can anyone guarantee the number one spot?
faq.a: No — and be wary of anyone who promises it. Rankings depend on your competitors, the searcher's location and Google's own judgement, none of which any agency controls. What a good partner can do is get the fundamentals right and steadily improve your position over time.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a local business, showing up on Google Maps is often worth more than ranking anywhere else on the web. When someone searches 'plumber near me' or 'dentist in Leeds', the map pack — those three listings with the little red pins — is what most people tap first. So working out how to rank on Google Maps is one of the highest-value things you can do. The good news is that it comes down to a handful of fundamentals you control, not luck or a huge budget.

This guide walks through what Google actually looks at when it ranks local results, and the practical steps that nudge your business up the map. No jargon, no tricks — just the things that genuinely work.

## How to rank on Google Maps: what Google looks at

Google ranks local results on three things: **relevance**, **distance** and **prominence**. Relevance is how well your business matches what someone typed. Distance is how close you are to the person searching. Prominence is how well known and trusted you appear to be, based on reviews, mentions and your wider web presence.

You can't do much about distance — your address is your address. But relevance and prominence are largely in your hands, and that's where the work pays off. Get those two right and you can outrank a nearer competitor who has never bothered.

- Relevance: your categories, services and description matching real searches
- Distance: your registered address versus where the searcher is standing
- Prominence: reviews, links, local mentions and a solid, fast website

## Start with your Google Business Profile

Your Google Business Profile (the old Google My Business) is the listing that feeds the map. It's free, yet most owners fill in half of it and then wonder why they're invisible. A complete, verified profile is the single biggest step towards showing up, and it's the foundation of any good local SEO effort.

If you'd rather someone set this up properly and keep it optimised, that's part of what our [local SEO service](/services/seo) covers.

- Claim and verify the listing so you actually control it
- Choose the most accurate primary category, then add relevant secondary ones
- List every service you offer and write a clear, honest description
- Upload real photos of your premises, team and finished work
- Add correct opening hours, phone number and the areas you cover
- Keep it alive with the occasional post, offer or update

## Win more reviews — and reply to every one

Reviews are one of the strongest prominence signals Google has, and they're the first thing a potential customer reads. A steady stream of recent, genuine reviews tells Google your business is active and trusted. Ten thoughtful reviews from this year beat fifty from 2019.

Make it easy: ask every happy customer, send them a direct review link, and reply to everything that comes in — the good and the awkward. Replying shows you're paying attention and gives Google fresh signals to read.

One warning: never buy fake reviews. Google is good at spotting them, and getting caught can sink your listing for good.

> A steady trickle of honest reviews will do more for your map ranking than almost anything else you can do this month.

## Make your website pull its weight

Google cross-checks your profile against your website, so the two need to tell the same story. A slow, thin or clunky site on a mobile drags your ranking down — and most local searches happen on a phone, often when someone is out and about and ready to buy.

Make sure your business name, address and phone number match your profile exactly, add proper pages for each service and location you cover, and keep the whole thing quick to load. If your current site is holding you back, a fresh [website design and build](/services/website-design) can fix the foundations at the same time.

## Keep your details consistent everywhere

Google builds confidence in your business by seeing the same information in lots of places. If your address says 'Street' on one directory and 'St.' on another, or an old phone number is still floating about, that inconsistency quietly chips away at your prominence.

List your business on the directories that matter — Google, Bing, industry bodies and trusted local sites — and use exactly the same name, address and phone number every time. It's tedious, but it works, and it's especially worth it in competitive fields. We work across a range of [industries](/industries), and the same principles apply whether you're a café, a clinic or a contractor.

## Treat local SEO as a habit, not a one-off

Here's the part nobody likes: ranking on Google Maps isn't a switch you flip. Your competitors are working on the same thing, and Google rewards businesses that stay active. Profiles that get fresh reviews, new photos and the odd post tend to hold their spot; ones that go quiet slowly slip down.

The trick is to build small habits — ask for a review after every job, add a photo when you finish something you're proud of, and check your details still match across the web. A little each month beats a frantic push once a year.

## The quick wins

- Google ranks local results on relevance, distance and prominence — you control two of the three
- A fully completed, verified Google Business Profile is non-negotiable
- Recent, genuine reviews are your single biggest lever
- Your website and business details must match everywhere they appear
- Ranking is an ongoing habit, not a one-off task

## Frequently asked questions

### How long does it take to rank on Google Maps?

There's no fixed timeline, but most businesses see movement over a few weeks to a few months of steady effort. A brand-new profile in a competitive area takes longer than an established one. It's ongoing work rather than a one-off fix, which is why many owners keep it ticking over with a monthly plan.

### How much does local SEO cost?

We quote SEO per project, so you get a fixed price up front with no hourly billing — every business starts from a different place. If you also need a new website, our builds start from £477 (Starter), and ongoing care plans start at £27 a month. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing page](/pricing).

### Do I need a website to rank on Google Maps?

You can appear on the map with just a Google Business Profile, but a good website makes a real difference. It adds prominence, backs up the details on your profile and gives searchers somewhere to learn more and get in touch — which often turns a map view into an actual enquiry.

### Can anyone guarantee the number one spot?

No — and be wary of anyone who promises it. Rankings depend on your competitors, the searcher's location and Google's own judgement, none of which any agency controls. What a good partner can do is get the fundamentals right and steadily improve your position over time.

### Does replying to reviews really help?

Yes. Replying gives Google fresh activity to read, shows customers you're engaged, and lets you handle the occasional negative review gracefully. Aim to respond to every review within a few days — thank the good ones, and stay calm and helpful on the rest.

bodyMarkdown:
If you have ever searched for a plumber, an emergency dentist or a decent Sunday roast near you, the results that appear at the top — the little map, the star ratings, the opening hours — are powered by Google Business Profiles. So what is a Google Business Profile, and why does it matter so much for a small business? In plain terms, it is your free listing on Google Search and Google Maps, and for a lot of local firms it brings in more enquiries than the website ever does.

It used to be called Google My Business, which is why you will still see both names floating about. Whatever you call it, it is one of the most valuable pieces of marketing you can own — and one of the most neglected. Here is what it is, what it does, and how to make yours earn its keep.

## What is a Google Business Profile, and why does it matter?

A Google Business Profile is the free listing Google gives every business so it can appear on Search and Maps. It holds your name, address, phone number, opening hours, photos, reviews and a handful of other details. When someone nearby searches for what you do, Google decides which few businesses to show them — and your profile is a big part of how it chooses.

It matters because most local searches happen on a mobile, often with real intent behind them. Someone typing 'barber near me' or 'boiler repair Leeds' is usually ready to call or visit, not idly browsing. A strong profile puts you in front of that person at the exact moment they want you, without paying for an ad. Get it right and you show up in the map results, look trustworthy at a glance, and make it easy to ring you. That is the whole game.

> For many local businesses the profile brings in more phone calls than the website — yet it is the thing owners spend the least time on.

## Where your profile actually shows up

Your profile is not one thing in one place. It quietly feeds several parts of Google that customers use every day:

Because it powers all of these at once, a well-kept profile works in the background while you get on with the job.

- The local pack — the boxed group of three businesses with a map that sits near the top of local search results.
- Google Maps — where people search directly for nearby services and tap through for directions.
- The knowledge panel — the information box that appears when someone searches for your business by name.
- Voice and 'near me' searches, where Google leans heavily on profile data to give an answer.

## What a profile that works actually includes

Setting one up takes minutes; filling it out properly is where the results come from. The profiles that pull their weight tend to have:

None of it is complicated. It just needs doing, and then keeping on top of — which is exactly the sort of thing our [care plans](/services/care-plans) are built to handle for busy owners.

- Accurate name, address and phone number that match your website exactly — Google rewards consistency.
- The right categories, chosen carefully, so you appear for the services you actually offer.
- Real photos of your work, premises and team, not stock images.
- Opening hours kept up to date, bank holidays included.
- A steady trickle of genuine reviews, with a reply to each one.
- Regular posts and updates, so the profile looks active rather than abandoned.

## The mistakes that quietly cost you customers

Most profiles are not broken, just half-finished. The usual culprits:

Fixing these is usually a quick win. It is also where good [local SEO](/services/seo) starts, long before you spend a penny on adverts.

- Claiming the profile, adding a logo, and never touching it again.
- Wrong or inconsistent details — an old phone number here, a different address there — which confuses Google and customers alike.
- Ignoring reviews, good and bad. A calm, polite reply to a critical review often reassures the next reader more than the review itself worries them.
- No photos, so you look less real than the competitor two doors down who added twelve.

## Your profile and your website are a team

A Google Business Profile is brilliant at getting you found and making the first impression. But the moment someone wants to know more — see your prices, read about a service, book in — they click through to your website. If that site is slow, confusing or awkward on a phone, the profile has done its job and the website has undone it.

That is why we treat the two together. A [fast, clear website](/services/website-design) gives the profile somewhere worth sending people, and the two reinforce each other in search. You do not need anything fancy or expensive to get this right — you can see our [straightforward pricing](/pricing) up front, with no hourly billing and no surprises.

## How to get started

If you have never claimed your profile, search your business name on Google, look for the 'Own this business?' or 'Claim this business' link, and follow the verification steps. If you already have one, spend twenty minutes checking every detail is correct and adding a few recent photos — you will often notice the difference within weeks.

And if you would rather someone simply looked at the whole picture for you, that is what we are here for. [Get in touch](/contact) and we will tell you honestly what is working and what is not.

## The quick wins

- A Google Business Profile is your free listing on Google Search and Maps — the same tool once called Google My Business.
- For many local businesses it drives more enquiries than the website, because it reaches people the moment they are ready to buy.
- Accurate details, real photos, genuine reviews and regular updates separate a profile that works from one that just exists.
- Your profile and website work best as a pair — one gets you found, the other closes the deal.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is a Google Business Profile free?

Yes. Creating and managing a Google Business Profile is completely free — Google does not charge you to appear on Search or Maps. The only costs come if you choose to pay someone to set it up and maintain it, or to build the website it links through to.

### What is the difference between Google Business Profile and Google My Business?

They are the same thing. Google renamed Google My Business to Google Business Profile a while back, so older guides and plenty of business owners still use the old name. Nothing about how it fundamentally works has changed.

### How long does it take to set up and get verified?

The listing itself takes a few minutes to create. Google then verifies that you own the business, which can take anywhere from a few minutes to a few days depending on the method. If you also want a website that matches, most of our builds go live within three to six weeks.

### How much does it cost to get help with my profile and website?

The profile itself is free. If you want us involved, our websites are fixed-price from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, and care plans that keep everything updated start at £27 a month. SEO work is quoted per project.

### Do I still need a website if I have a Google Business Profile?

Yes. The profile gets you found and makes a strong first impression, but people click through to your website to check prices, read about your services and get in touch. The two work as a pair — you really want both.

bodyMarkdown:
Every small-business owner eventually hits the same fork in the road: should you invest in SEO or paid ads to get found on Google? Both put you in front of people searching for what you sell. They just work in very different ways, cost different amounts and pay off on different timelines. This guide walks through **SEO vs paid ads** in plain English, so you can decide which is right for your business rather than guessing.

There is no single correct answer here. A plumber in Leeds who needs the phone ringing this week has different needs from a wedding photographer building a name over the next year. By the end, you'll know which lever to pull first, and when it makes sense to pull both.

## What SEO and paid ads actually mean

When you search on Google, the results split into two camps. The entries marked "Sponsored" at the very top are paid ads, mostly Google Ads. Businesses bid to appear there and pay a fee each time someone clicks. Below those sit the organic results, and earning a spot there is what search engine optimisation (SEO) is all about.

Think of it as renting versus owning. With paid ads you rent your position: the moment you stop paying, your listing vanishes. With SEO you build an asset. It takes longer to earn a strong organic ranking, but once you're there, the traffic keeps arriving without a per-click cost. Neither is better in the abstract. They suit different budgets, timelines and goals.

- **Paid ads:** instant visibility, you pay per click, results stop when spending stops.
- **SEO:** slower to build, no per-click fee, compounds and lasts over time.
- **Local SEO:** getting your Google Business Profile and map listing to show for nearby searches, which matters hugely for tradespeople, clinics and shops.

## The honest trade-offs

Paid ads are the fast lane. Set up a campaign properly and you can appear at the top of Google within a day, which is ideal for a launch, a seasonal push or a quiet spell you need to fill quickly. The catch is that costs are ongoing and, in competitive trades, a single click can run to several pounds. Stop the budget and the leads stop with it.

SEO is the long game. It rewards patience: most small businesses see meaningful movement over three to six months, not overnight. But that traffic is effectively free once you've earned it, and people tend to trust organic results more than ads. Our [SEO and local SEO service](/services/seo) is built around that steady, compounding growth rather than quick wins that fade.

> Paid ads rent you a spot today. SEO builds an asset that keeps working long after the invoice is paid.

## Which one fits your business right now

The right choice depends less on the tactics and more on where your business sits today. Cash flow, how fast you need leads and how competitive your trade is all point you in a direction.

As a rough guide, paid ads make sense when you need results now or you're testing a new offer. SEO makes sense when you're playing a longer game and want to stop paying for every single visitor. Many established businesses run both, but if the budget only stretches to one, be honest about your timeline.

- **Lean towards paid ads if:** you've just launched, you have a time-sensitive offer, or you need enquiries within days.
- **Lean towards SEO if:** you can wait a few months, you want to lower your cost per lead over time, or you rely on local searches like "emergency electrician near me".
- **Consider both if:** you have steady cash flow and want ads covering you today while SEO builds your long-term foundation.

## Why your website decides the outcome either way

Here's the part most guides skip: neither SEO nor paid ads works well if your website lets you down. Send paid traffic to a slow, confusing page and you're paying for clicks that bounce. Rank organically for a great keyword but bury your phone number, and those hard-won visitors leave without getting in touch.

Both channels funnel people to the same place, so a fast, clear, mobile-friendly site is the foundation under everything. Most local searches happen on a phone, often while someone is out and about, so the site has to load quickly and make the next step obvious. That's exactly what we focus on with [website design and build](/services/website-design): pages that turn visitors into enquiries, whichever channel sent them.

If you're weighing up spend, it's worth seeing our [transparent, fixed-price options](/pricing) first. Websites start at £477, with care plans from £27 a month, so you know the cost up front with no hourly surprises.

## The quick wins

- Paid ads buy speed and stop the moment you stop paying; SEO is slower but builds a lasting asset.
- Pick based on your timeline, budget and how competitive your trade is, not on which sounds trendier.
- Whichever you choose, a fast and clear website is what turns the traffic into actual enquiries.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is SEO or paid ads better for a small local business?

It depends on your timeline. If you need enquiries this week, paid ads get you to the top of Google quickly. If you can wait a few months and want to stop paying per click, SEO (especially local SEO and your Google Business Profile) usually gives a better long-term return. Plenty of local businesses do best running both once cash flow allows.

### How much do SEO and paid ads cost?

With paid ads you set your own budget and pay Google per click, so costs are ongoing. SEO is an investment in your own site rather than a per-click fee. At Nexus Lab Systems our websites are fixed-price from £477 (Starter), £1,170 (Growth) and £2,370 (Complete), with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the cost up front with no hourly billing. See our [pricing](/pricing) for the full breakdown.

### How long does SEO take to work?

SEO is a longer game than ads. Most small businesses start seeing meaningful movement in organic rankings over three to six months, depending on how competitive their trade and area are. Paid ads, by contrast, can put you at the top of Google within a day. That difference in speed is usually the deciding factor.

### Can I do both SEO and paid ads at the same time?

Yes, and many established businesses do. Ads cover you today while SEO builds your foundation for tomorrow. The key is that both send people to the same website, so if the site is slow or unclear you'll waste money on both. Get the site right first, then layer the channels on top.

### Do I need a new website before investing in either?

Not always, but it's worth checking. If your current site is slow, hard to use on a phone or doesn't make the next step obvious, you'll leak leads no matter how much traffic you send it. A free teardown is a short screen-recorded review of your existing site that shows exactly where visitors drop off, so you can fix the foundation before spending on marketing.

intro: Every small-business owner eventually hits the same fork in the road: should you invest in SEO or paid ads to get found on Google? Both put you in front of people searching for what you sell. They just work in very different ways, cost different amounts and pay off on different timelines. This guide walks through **SEO vs paid ads** in plain English, so you can decide which is right for your business rather than guessing.

There is no single correct answer here. A plumber in Leeds who needs the phone ringing this week has different needs from a wedding photographer building a name over the next year. By the end, you'll know which lever to pull first, and when it makes sense to pull both.

sections.h2: What SEO and paid ads actually mean
paragraphs: When you search on Google, the results split into two camps. The entries marked "Sponsored" at the very top are paid ads, mostly Google Ads. Businesses bid to appear there and pay a fee each time someone clicks. Below those sit the organic results, and earning a spot there is what search engine optimisation (SEO) is all about.

Think of it as renting versus owning. With paid ads you rent your position: the moment you stop paying, your listing vanishes. With SEO you build an asset. It takes longer to earn a strong organic ranking, but once you're there, the traffic keeps arriving without a per-click cost. Neither is better in the abstract. They suit different budgets, timelines and goals.
bullets: **Paid ads:** instant visibility, you pay per click, results stop when spending stops.
**SEO:** slower to build, no per-click fee, compounds and lasts over time.
**Local SEO:** getting your Google Business Profile and map listing to show for nearby searches, which matters hugely for tradespeople, clinics and shops.

sections.h2: The honest trade-offs
paragraphs: Paid ads are the fast lane. Set up a campaign properly and you can appear at the top of Google within a day, which is ideal for a launch, a seasonal push or a quiet spell you need to fill quickly. The catch is that costs are ongoing and, in competitive trades, a single click can run to several pounds. Stop the budget and the leads stop with it.

SEO is the long game. It rewards patience: most small businesses see meaningful movement over three to six months, not overnight. But that traffic is effectively free once you've earned it, and people tend to trust organic results more than ads. Our [SEO and local SEO service](/services/seo) is built around that steady, compounding growth rather than quick wins that fade.
sections.pullquote: Paid ads rent you a spot today. SEO builds an asset that keeps working long after the invoice is paid.

sections.h2: Which one fits your business right now
paragraphs: The right choice depends less on the tactics and more on where your business sits today. Cash flow, how fast you need leads and how competitive your trade is all point you in a direction.

As a rough guide, paid ads make sense when you need results now or you're testing a new offer. SEO makes sense when you're playing a longer game and want to stop paying for every single visitor. Many established businesses run both, but if the budget only stretches to one, be honest about your timeline.
bullets: **Lean towards paid ads if:** you've just launched, you have a time-sensitive offer, or you need enquiries within days.
**Lean towards SEO if:** you can wait a few months, you want to lower your cost per lead over time, or you rely on local searches like "emergency electrician near me".
**Consider both if:** you have steady cash flow and want ads covering you today while SEO builds your long-term foundation.

sections.h2: Why your website decides the outcome either way
paragraphs: Here's the part most guides skip: neither SEO nor paid ads works well if your website lets you down. Send paid traffic to a slow, confusing page and you're paying for clicks that bounce. Rank organically for a great keyword but bury your phone number, and those hard-won visitors leave without getting in touch.

Both channels funnel people to the same place, so a fast, clear, mobile-friendly site is the foundation under everything. Most local searches happen on a phone, often while someone is out and about, so the site has to load quickly and make the next step obvious. That's exactly what we focus on with [website design and build](/services/website-design): pages that turn visitors into enquiries, whichever channel sent them.

If you're weighing up spend, it's worth seeing our [transparent, fixed-price options](/pricing) first. Websites start at £477, with care plans from £27 a month, so you know the cost up front with no hourly surprises.

keyTakeaways: Paid ads buy speed and stop the moment you stop paying; SEO is slower but builds a lasting asset.
Pick based on your timeline, budget and how competitive your trade is, not on which sounds trendier.
Whichever you choose, a fast and clear website is what turns the traffic into actual enquiries.

bodyMarkdown:
Core Web Vitals sound like something only a developer needs to worry about. They are not. If you run a small business in the UK, these three simple measurements shape how many people stay on your site, pick up the phone, or quietly tap back to Google. Here are Core Web Vitals explained for small businesses, without the jargon.

## What are Core Web Vitals?

Core Web Vitals are three scores Google uses to measure how your website feels to a real visitor. Not how it looks in a designer's mockup, but how it behaves on an average phone with an average connection. There are three of them, and each one measures a different kind of frustration.

Put simply: does the page load quickly, respond quickly, and stay still while it does it. That is the whole idea.

- **Largest Contentful Paint (LCP)** — how long the main content takes to show up. A good score is under 2.5 seconds.
- **Interaction to Next Paint (INP)** — how quickly the page reacts when someone taps a button or opens a menu. Aim for under 200 milliseconds.
- **Cumulative Layout Shift (CLS)** — how much the page jumps around as it loads. You want a score below 0.1, so nothing moves under a visitor's thumb.

## Why Core Web Vitals matter for a small business

You might think this is a technical box-ticking exercise. It is not. Most local searches happen on a mobile, often outside on patchy data, and people decide in a second or two whether to wait. A slow or jumpy page costs you enquiries before anyone has read a word about you.

There is a search angle too. Google uses page experience as one of many ranking signals, so genuinely fast sites have a small edge over slow ones. It will not outrank strong, relevant content on its own, but when two plumbers or two cafes are otherwise even, the faster site tends to win the click. Good speed also supports the rest of your [SEO](/services/seo) work rather than quietly undermining it.

> A visitor who waits four seconds for your page to load has usually already tapped back to Google.

## How to check your own site

You do not need to pay anyone to find out where you stand. Google's PageSpeed Insights is free: type in your web address and it grades each of the three vitals, on mobile and desktop, using real-world data where it has it. If you have Google Search Console set up, its Core Web Vitals report shows which pages pass and which need work across your whole site.

Test on mobile first, because that is where most of your visitors are. And test a few different pages, not just the homepage, since a heavy service page or photo gallery often scores far worse than the front door.

## Why small-business sites usually fail these

When a local site scores badly, it is nearly always the same handful of culprits. The good news is that none of them are mysterious, and most are fixable.

- **Huge, unoptimised images** straight off a phone or camera, weighing several megabytes each.
- **Cheap, overloaded shared hosting** that responds slowly before your page has even started to load.
- **Too many plugins and tracking scripts** bolted on over the years, each one adding weight.
- **Images and ads with no set size**, so the layout jumps as they load in — the main cause of a poor CLS score.
- **Bloated page-builder templates** that ship far more code than your site actually uses.

## What you can actually do about it

You do not have to become a developer. Start with the cheap wins: compress your images before uploading, remove plugins and scripts you no longer use, and set a width and height on images so the page stops shifting. Those three alone move most sites in the right direction.

If your site is built on a tired template or a slow host, tidying at the edges only gets you so far. That is when a proper rebuild pays for itself — a clean, lightweight [website](/services/website-design) that is fast by design rather than patched after the fact. Keeping it fast then comes down to ongoing upkeep, which is exactly what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for. If you would rather see the numbers before spending anything, our [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and public, and you can always [talk it through with us](/contact) first.

## The quick wins

- Core Web Vitals are three scores — loading (LCP), responsiveness (INP) and visual stability (CLS) — that measure how your site feels on a real phone.
- Aim for LCP under 2.5 seconds, INP under 200 milliseconds and CLS under 0.1.
- Check yours free with Google PageSpeed Insights, then fix the usual causes: heavy images, slow hosting and layout that jumps as it loads.

## Frequently asked questions

### What are Core Web Vitals in plain English?

They are three scores Google uses to judge how your website feels to a visitor: how fast the main content loads (LCP), how quickly the page responds to a tap or click (INP), and how much it jumps around while loading (CLS). In short, is it quick, responsive and steady.

### Do Core Web Vitals affect my Google ranking?

They help, but they are only one of many ranking signals. A fast, stable site has a small edge, especially on mobile, but it will not outrank genuinely better, more relevant content on speed alone. Treat good vitals as support for your content, not a replacement for it.

### How much does it cost to fix a slow website?

It depends on whether your site needs tuning or a full rebuild. Our websites are fixed-price from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, so you know the cost up front with no hourly billing. Care plans to keep a site fast and maintained start at £27 a month, and redesigns or SEO work are quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build or rebuild a site?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you are never left on your own once it is live.

### Can I check my Core Web Vitals myself for free?

Yes. Go to Google PageSpeed Insights, enter your web address, and it will grade all three vitals on mobile and desktop. If you would like a plain-English walkthrough of what the results mean for your business, a free teardown is a short screen-recorded review of your current site.

description: Learn how to get more Google reviews and why they matter for local businesses, plus simple ways to ask customers without feeling awkward. Book a free teardown.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a local business, Google reviews are quietly deciding whether new customers choose you or the shop down the road. Learning how to get more Google reviews, and why they matter so much, is one of the cheapest ways to win more work without spending a penny on advertising. Yet most small businesses leave it to chance and hope a happy customer remembers to say something nice.

Good reviews are a habit, not a stroke of luck. Here is a plain-English guide to why they pull their weight, and exactly how to ask for them without feeling awkward or pushy.

## Why Google reviews matter more than you'd think

When someone searches for a plumber in Leeds or a dentist in Cardiff, Google shows a short list of nearby businesses with a row of gold stars under each name. Those stars are often the first thing people judge you on, long before they reach your website. A strong set of recent reviews quietly does three jobs at once.

Recency counts too. A dozen reviews from the last few months says far more than fifty reviews that dried up two years ago. That is why getting reviews needs to be an ongoing habit, not a one-off push.

- **Trust.** Most people read reviews before they call, and treat them a bit like a personal recommendation from a friend.
- **Visibility.** Reviews are one of the signals Google uses to decide who appears in the local map results, so they feed straight into your [local SEO](/services/seo).
- **Choice.** Faced with two similar businesses, people almost always pick the one with more reviews and a higher star rating.

> Reviews are the closest thing you have to word of mouth working for you around the clock.

## How to get more Google reviews without feeling pushy

Here is the part most guides skip. You will get far more reviews by making the ask simple, timely and personal. People are usually glad to help a small business they liked — they just need a gentle nudge and an easy route to the review form.

To find your link, open your Google Business Profile, choose 'Ask for reviews' and copy the short URL it gives you. Save it somewhere handy so anyone on your team can share it in seconds.

- **Ask at the right moment.** Just after you have finished a job well, or when a customer says thank you, is when goodwill is highest.
- **Make it one tap.** Send a direct link to your Google review form by text or email, rather than telling people to search for you.
- **Ask in person, then follow up.** A quick verbal request backed by a link a few minutes later converts best.
- **Keep it personal.** A short message from a real person beats a generic automated blast every time.
- **Add gentle reminders.** Put your review link in your email signature, on invoices, receipts and thank-you cards.
- **Never buy or bribe reviews.** Google can spot fake or incentivised reviews and may remove them or penalise your profile.

## Build asking into your everyday routine

The businesses with the best review profiles rarely have a clever trick. They have a habit. Reviews come in steadily because asking is baked into how they finish every job, rather than something they remember to do once a quarter when work is quiet.

Pick one or two natural moments in your day — handing over the keys, sending the final invoice, closing a project call — and attach the ask to them. Print a small QR code that points to your review form and keep it by the till or in the van. Brief everyone on the team so it doesn't rest on one person. Once it becomes routine, a handful of fresh reviews a month looks after itself.

## Reply to every review, good or bad

Reviews are a conversation, not a scoreboard. Thank people for the good ones by name, and answer the critical ones calmly and quickly. A measured reply to a one-star review often impresses future customers more than the complaint itself, because it shows you take problems seriously.

Replying also tells Google your profile is active and looked after, which never hurts. Aim to respond within a day or two while it is still fresh. If a complaint is fair, acknowledge it, put it right where you can, and say what you've changed. If it's unfair or fake, stay polite and factual — future readers can usually tell the difference, and a calm reply always wins.

## Turn your reviews into more customers

Reviews shouldn't just sit on Google. Feature your best ones where buyers are already deciding — on your homepage, service pages and quote forms. Seeing real, recent praise at the moment of choice reassures people and nudges them to get in touch. A short line under each review — the customer's first name and their town — makes it feel genuine without giving away private details.

For that to work, your site needs to be quick to update, so a [website that's easy to change](/services/website-design) earns its keep. Keeping your site and your Google profile in step is exactly the sort of thing a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is built to handle, so nothing goes stale.

## The quick wins

- Reviews shape trust, local search visibility and the final choice between you and a competitor.
- Ask at the right moment, make it one tap, and keep the request personal.
- Never buy reviews — build them steadily and reply to every one.
- Show your best reviews on your website, not just on Google.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is it against Google's rules to ask customers for reviews?

No. Google is happy for you to ask customers for honest reviews. What is not allowed is offering discounts or gifts in exchange for them, or only asking your happiest customers. Ask everyone, and let them say what they truly think.

### How many Google reviews do I need?

There is no magic number. What matters most is having more than your nearby competitors, keeping a healthy average, and adding fresh reviews regularly. A steady trickle beats a big one-off burst that then stops.

### How do I get my Google review link?

Sign in to your Google Business Profile, select 'Ask for reviews', and copy the short link. Share that link by text, email or a QR code on receipts so customers reach the review form in one tap.

### How much does it cost to improve my website and reviews?

Asking for reviews is free — it costs only a little consistency. If you want the wider picture handled, our websites are fixed-price from £477, care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO is quoted per project so you always know the cost up front. You can see the full [pricing](/pricing) before you decide anything.

### How long does it take to see results?

Reviews build over weeks and months as you make the ask a habit. If you are also rebuilding your site, most of our projects go live in three to six weeks, and a stronger site plus a steady stream of reviews tends to compound from there.

title: Keyword Research Basics for Local Businesses
description: Learn keyword research basics for local businesses: how to find the search terms customers use, the free tools to try, and how to turn them into enquiries.
metaTitle: Keyword Research Basics for Local Businesses
excerpt: Find the exact phrases your local customers type into Google, and turn them into a steady stream of the right enquiries.
targetKeyword: keyword research basics for local businesses

cta.heading: Not sure which keywords your site could win?
cta.sub: Book a free teardown and we'll show you where your website is missing local searches.

faq.q: What is keyword research, in plain English?
faq.a: It is the process of finding the words and phrases people type into Google when they are looking for a business like yours, then checking how often each is searched and how much competition it has. For a local business, the aim is to spot the specific, ready-to-buy searches, like 'emergency electrician Bristol', and make sure your website is built around them.

bodyMarkdown:
Keyword research basics for local businesses come down to one simple idea: find the exact words people type when they want what you sell, then make sure your website speaks their language. You don't need a marketing degree or costly software, just a bit of curiosity about your own customers and an afternoon to write things down.

Get this right and the rest of your marketing gets easier, because every page has a clear job to do. Get it wrong and you end up chasing words nobody searches, or fighting national brands for terms you will never win. Here is how to do the groundwork properly, without the jargon.

## What keyword research actually is

Keyword research is simply working out which phrases your potential customers use in Google, how often they use them, and how much competition there is for each one. A keyword rarely means a single word. The useful ones are short phrases like 'emergency plumber Leeds' or 'dog groomer near me'.

For a local business, the goal is not to chase the biggest numbers. A plumber in Sheffield does not need to rank for 'plumber' across the whole country; they need the handful of people in Sheffield with a leaking pipe right now. Those smaller, more specific searches are worth far more to you, because the people making them are close by and ready to book. That focus on nearby, ready-to-act searches is the heart of good [local SEO](/services/seo).

## Keyword research basics for local businesses, step by step

Here is a simple sequence you can work through in a single sitting:

None of this needs special training. You are already the expert on your own customers. This just turns what you know into a plan you can act on.

- **Brainstorm your services.** List everything you offer in plain words, the way a customer would say it rather than the way it reads on your invoice. 'Boiler repair', not 'domestic heating remediation'.
- **Add your locations.** Pair each service with the towns, neighbourhoods and postcodes you actually cover: 'boiler repair Wandsworth', 'boiler repair SW18'.
- **Think about intent.** Note whether someone using that phrase wants to buy now, compare prices, or just learn something. The ready-to-buy searches matter most.
- **Check the real numbers.** Use a free tool to see roughly how often each phrase is searched and how tough the competition looks.
- **Pick your winners.** Choose a shortlist that is specific enough to win but popular enough to be worth the effort.

## The searches worth chasing

Not every search is equal. The ones that turn into enquiries usually share three traits: they are specific, they are local, and they carry clear intent. 'Cheap haircut' is vague and could mean almost anything. 'Balayage salon Didsbury' tells you exactly who is searching and what they want.

These longer, more specific phrases are often called long-tail keywords. Each gets fewer searches on its own, but there are thousands of them, the competition is gentler, and the people using them are much closer to picking up the phone. Across the [industries we work with](/industries), from salons to trades, that pattern holds: the narrow searches quietly bring in the best work.

> The best local keyword is not the one with the most searches. It is the one your ideal customer types the moment they are ready to book.

## Free tools you can try this afternoon

You do not need a paid subscription to begin. A few free tools will get you most of the way, and an hour with a notepad will hand you more usable keywords than most businesses ever act on:

- **Google autocomplete.** Start typing a service into the search bar and note the suggestions. Those are real phrases real people use.
- **'People also ask' and 'Related searches'.** Scroll any results page and you will find dozens of question-style keywords for free.
- **Google Keyword Planner.** Free with a Google Ads account, it shows rough search volumes and related terms.
- **Google Business Profile insights.** Once you have claimed your listing, it reveals the search terms already bringing people to you.

## Turning keywords into pages that rank

Research only pays off when it shapes your website. Once you have a shortlist, give each important phrase a home. Your main service pages should target your biggest services, and a separate, genuinely written page for each town or service you cover helps you appear in more local searches. Copying a page and swapping the town name does the opposite, because Google treats near-identical pages as spam.

Weave your chosen phrases naturally into page titles, headings and opening paragraphs, then write for humans first. If the foundations are shaky, with slow pages, thin content or no clear structure, even perfect keywords will not save you, which is where solid [website design](/services/website-design) and keyword research work hand in hand. Get the groundwork right yourself, then [get in touch](/contact) when you would like a second pair of eyes on it.

## The quick wins

- Keyword research is just finding the phrases your customers actually type, and you can start today with free tools and no jargon.
- For local businesses, specific phrases like 'boiler repair Wandsworth' beat broad ones like 'plumber' every time.
- Give your best keywords a home on real, well-written pages, because research only pays off when it shapes your website.

## Frequently asked questions

### What is keyword research, in plain English?

It is the process of finding the words and phrases people type into Google when they are looking for a business like yours, then checking how often each is searched and how much competition it has. For a local business, the aim is to spot the specific, ready-to-buy searches, like 'emergency electrician Bristol', and make sure your website is built around them.

### Do I need paid tools to do keyword research?

No. You can get a long way with Google's autocomplete, the 'People also ask' and 'Related searches' boxes, the free Google Keyword Planner, and the insights inside your Google Business Profile. Paid tools add detail and save time, but the basics cost nothing but an afternoon.

### How many keywords should a local business target?

Start small. A handful of strong, specific phrases tied to your main services and locations will do more than a long list of vague ones. As you add well-written pages for each service and area you cover, you will naturally rank for hundreds of related searches over time, so there is no need to force it.

### How much does professional SEO and a website cost?

Doing the basics yourself is free. If you would like help, our websites are fixed-price from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project, so you always know the cost up front. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing page](/pricing).

### How long does keyword research take to pay off?

The research itself takes an afternoon. Seeing results in Google takes longer, because local SEO is a slow build measured in months rather than days, and no honest agency can guarantee a ranking. Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, and the keyword groundwork keeps working for years afterwards.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a small business, you have probably heard that on-page SEO matters, but nobody hands you a straight list of what to actually do. This on-page SEO checklist for small business websites is that list: no jargon, no 40-point audits you will never finish. Just the changes that move the needle for a plumber in Leeds, a café in Bristol or an accountant in Milton Keynes.

On-page SEO is everything you control on your own pages: the words, the structure, the images and the code behind the scenes. Get it right and Google understands what you offer and who to show it to. Get it wrong and even a lovely-looking site stays invisible. Here is how to fix it, one page at a time.

## What on-page SEO really means

On-page SEO is the work you do directly on a page to help it rank and to help visitors act. It is different from off-page SEO (things like reviews and links from other sites) and technical SEO (the plumbing that lets Google crawl your site). Most small businesses lose ground on the on-page basics, which is good news, because these are the parts you can change without waiting on anyone.

Think of each page as answering one clear question. Your emergency plumber page answers "who can fix my burst pipe tonight?" When the whole page (title, headings, copy and images) points at that single question, you rank for it. When it tries to be about five things at once, it ranks for none.

> A page that tries to be about everything ends up ranking for nothing.

## The on-page SEO checklist every small business page needs

Work through this list for each important page. Start with your home page, your main service pages and your contact page, then move down the rest.

Do this once per page and you are already ahead of most local competitors.

- **Title tag:** one clear, unique title per page with your main keyword near the front and, ideally, your town. Keep it under about 60 characters.
- **Meta description:** a one or two sentence summary that sells the click. It will not rank you directly, but a good one earns more clicks.
- **One H1 heading:** the visible headline. Say plainly what the page is about.
- **Logical H2s:** break the page into scannable sections that match how people search.
- **Keyword in the first 100 words:** mention your topic early and naturally, without stuffing.
- **Descriptive URL:** /emergency-plumber-leeds beats /page?id=27.
- **Internal links:** point visitors (and Google) to your related services and your [contact page](/contact).
- **Image alt text:** describe each image so search engines and screen readers understand it.

## Content that earns the click and the ranking

Google rewards pages that genuinely answer the question, so write for the person first. Use the words your customers use. If they search "boiler repair near me", say boiler repair, not "domestic heating solutions". Cover the obvious follow-up questions too: price ranges, the areas you cover, how fast you respond.

Length matters less than completeness. A tightly written 400-word service page that answers everything will beat a padded 1,500-word one. And every page needs a clear next step, a reason to call, book or enquire. Good [SEO](/services/seo) and good copywriting are really the same job done well.

## Speed, images and mobile: the basics that count

Most local searches happen on a phone, so your site has to load fast and work with a thumb. A slow, clunky page loses visitors before Google ever gets a say. The most common culprit is a huge image: a 4MB photo straight off a phone will drag a whole page down, so compress your images before you upload them, use modern formats, and size them to how they actually display.

Then test on a real device. Open your pages on your own phone using mobile data, not just office wifi. If a button feels fiddly to tap or text needs pinching to read, it feels that way for your customers too. A well-built [small business website](/services/website-design) handles most of this for you from day one, so you are not fighting the basics every time you add a page.

## Don't forget your local signals

For most small businesses the goal is not to rank across the country, it is to be the obvious choice in your town. So make your location impossible to miss. Put your town and county in your titles and copy where it reads naturally, and add your full business name, address and phone number in the footer of every page, matching your Google Business Profile exactly.

If you serve several areas, create a dedicated page for each one, genuinely written for that place rather than the same text with the town swapped, because Google treats near-identical pages as spam. And if you cover multiple trades or sectors, a clear [industries](/industries) structure helps both visitors and search engines see what you do.

## Where to start

You do not need to do all of this at once. Start with your most important page, usually a top service page, and work down the checklist: fix the title, tighten the copy, compress the images, add the local details, then move to the next page. Small, steady improvements compound over time.

If you would rather see exactly what is holding your site back, a free teardown is a short screen-recorded review of your current pages, pointing out the quick on-page wins in plain English. And because our [pricing](/pricing) is fixed and clear, you always know where you stand before anything starts.

## The quick wins

- On-page SEO is everything you control on your own pages, so start there before chasing links or ads.
- Give every important page one clear job, one strong title and one H1.
- Compress images and test on a real phone; speed is both a ranking and a sales issue.
- Add genuine local detail to win in your town, and never duplicate your area pages.

## Frequently asked questions

### What is on-page SEO in simple terms?

It is everything you can change on your own web pages to help them rank and to help visitors take action: your titles, headings, copy, URLs, internal links and images. Unlike reviews or links from other sites, these are fully in your control, which is why they are the best place for a small business to start.

### How much does on-page SEO cost for a small business?

It depends on whether you want a one-off improvement or an ongoing programme. We quote SEO per project after we have seen your site, and our websites are fixed-price from £477 (Starter), £1,170 (Growth) and £2,370 (Complete), with care plans from £27 a month if you want us to keep things maintained. You will always know the cost up front, with no hourly billing. The quickest way to a number is a free teardown.

### How long does on-page SEO take to work?

Google can pick up simple changes like a new title or improved copy within days to a few weeks, but competitive local terms usually take a few months of steady work to move. We never promise guaranteed rankings, because nobody honestly can. If we are building or redesigning your site, most projects go live in three to six weeks.

### Can I do on-page SEO myself, or do I need to hire someone?

You can absolutely handle the basics yourself using the checklist above, especially titles, headings and image sizes. Many owners do. People usually bring us in when they are short on time, unsure what to prioritise, or want a second pair of eyes before committing. A free teardown will tell you which jobs are worth doing yourself and which are not.

### How many pages should I optimise first?

Start with three to five: your home page, your top two or three service pages, and your contact page. These are the pages that earn enquiries, so improving them first gives you the fastest return. Once those are solid, work outward to the rest of the site at your own pace.
