bodyMarkdown:
Dark mode, animations and modern UX trends get plenty of airtime on design blogs and social feeds. But when you run a plumbing firm in Leeds, a café in Brighton or a dental practice in Bristol, the question is simpler than any trend report makes it sound: will any of this actually win you more customers? Some of it genuinely will. A fair amount of it won't, and a few bits could quietly cost you enquiries.

This is a plain-English look at what's worth your money and what's just fashion. No jargon, no chasing shiny features for their own sake — just the modern design choices that help a small-business website load fast, feel trustworthy and turn a visitor into a phone call.

## Dark mode: worth it, or just fashionable?

Dark mode means light text on a dark background, and a lot of people now have their phones set to switch to it automatically in the evening. It looks smart, it's easy on the eyes at night, and younger customers often expect the option. So should your site have it? For most local businesses, the honest answer is that it's a nice-to-have, not a must-have.

The trap is treating dark mode as your only look. Dark backgrounds can be harder to read for older customers, or for anyone standing outside your shop in bright sunlight trying to check your opening hours. A clean, high-contrast light design will never let you down. If you want to go further, the sensible middle ground is a site that quietly respects whatever setting the visitor's phone is already on, rather than a big toggle you have to build, test and maintain. Good [website design](/services/website-design) is about matching effort to payoff, and a full dark-mode build rarely earns its keep for a five-page local site.

## Animations that help, and the ones that annoy

Animation isn't good or bad in itself — it's about whether it helps someone get what they came for. Used well, small movements guide the eye and make a page feel considered. Used badly, they get in the way of the one thing that matters: your prices, your phone number, your booking form.

Here are the kinds of movement that genuinely help a visitor:

- Subtle hover effects on buttons and links, so it's obvious what people can tap or click
- A gentle fade as sections come into view, which makes a page feel calm rather than jumpy
- Small feedback moments — a tick when a form sends, a button that reacts when it's pressed
- A smooth, quick menu on mobile that opens without lag

> If an animation delays the moment someone can read your price or tap 'call', it isn't design — it's a cost.

## Dark mode, animations and modern UX trends: what actually matters

Strip away the fashion, and the modern UX trends that reliably help small businesses come down to a short list — none of it flashy. **Speed comes first**: a site that loads in a couple of seconds keeps far more visitors than a beautiful one that crawls. Then comes mobile-first design, because most people finding a local business are doing it on a phone, one-handed, in a hurry.

After that it's clarity. A visitor should see who you are, what you do and how to contact you without scrolling or squinting. Clear buttons, readable font sizes and an obvious next step beat any animation. These fundamentals also feed straight into how you rank — Google pays close attention to speed and mobile experience — so solid [local SEO](/services/seo) and good UX are really the same job done once.

The ones to be wary of are the show-offs: auto-playing sliders that move before anyone can read them, heavy scroll animations that delay your content, and anything that stutters on a three-year-old Android phone. Most local searches happen on mobile, often on average hardware and patchy signal, so every effect has to earn its place.

## Accessibility: the trend that quietly pays off

The most underrated modern trend isn't visual at all — it's making sure everyone can use your site. That means proper colour contrast, text you can read without zooming, buttons big enough to tap with a thumb, and respecting the 'reduce motion' setting for people who find animation uncomfortable or dizzying.

A good chunk of your customers benefit from this, not just a handful. And because clean, accessible code is also fast and well structured, it happens to make your site sturdier and easier to rank at the same time. It's rare to find a design choice that helps people and search engines at once, but this is one of them.

## How to decide what's worth building

You don't need every trend. You need the handful that suit your customers and your budget. Before adding any feature, ask a simple question: does this help someone contact us faster, or does it just look modern? If it's the second, it can usually wait.

Because we work to a fixed price, we'd rather spend your budget on the things that bring enquiries than on effects that impress other designers. You can see exactly where the money goes on our [pricing](/pricing) page, and a sensible [care plan](/services/care-plans) keeps the site quick and up to date long after launch — which is exactly when a lot of 'modern' sites start to sag.

## The quick wins

- Dark mode is a nice-to-have for most local sites — a clean, high-contrast light design matters far more.
- Animations should guide people towards your prices and phone number, never delay them.
- Speed, mobile-first layout and clear next steps beat any trend for winning enquiries.
- Accessibility helps more customers than you'd think, and quietly improves your ranking too.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do I really need dark mode on my small-business website?

For most local businesses, no. A clean, high-contrast light design is easier to read for all ages and in bright sunlight. If you want the modern feel, the smart option is a site that respects whatever setting the visitor's phone is already on, rather than building and maintaining a full toggle.

### Will animations slow my website down?

They can, if they're overdone. Heavy scroll effects and auto-playing sliders often hurt speed on average phones, and speed is one of the things that wins or loses enquiries. Small, purposeful movements — hover states, gentle fades, form feedback — add polish without the cost.

### How much does a website with modern UX cost?

We work to fixed prices, so you know the total up front. Websites start from £477 (Starter), £1,170 (Growth) and £2,370 (Complete), with care plans from £27/month. SEO and redesigns are quoted per project. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

### How long does it take to build?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how much content and how many pages are involved. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you're not left on your own once it launches.

### Is dark mode better for accessibility?

Not automatically. Some people find dark mode easier at night, but others read light backgrounds more comfortably. Real accessibility comes from good colour contrast, readable text sizes, tappable buttons and respecting reduced-motion settings — whichever colour scheme you use.

bodyMarkdown:
The letters GDPR still make a lot of small business owners wince. You picture legal fees, cookie pop-ups nobody reads and a stack of paperwork that eats a weekend you do not have. The good news: for a typical local business website, GDPR and your website compliance comes down to a handful of sensible, do-once tasks, not a mountain of red tape.

This is a plain-English guide to what actually matters. No scare tactics, no legalese. If you run a dental practice in Leeds, a plumbing firm in Bristol or a café in Manchester, you can work through this list and know where you stand.

## What GDPR actually means for a small business site

Since Brexit, the UK runs its own version of the rules, usually called UK GDPR, overseen by the Information Commissioner's Office (the ICO). The core idea has not changed: if your website collects personal data about people, you are responsible for handling it fairly, keeping it safe and being honest about what you do with it.

Personal data is anything that can identify someone. On most small-business sites that means the obvious stuff: names, email addresses and phone numbers from a contact form, plus quieter data like IP addresses and analytics identifiers. You do not need a data protection officer or a compliance department. You do need to know what you collect and why.

- A contact or enquiry form (name, email, message)
- A newsletter or mailing list sign-up
- Online booking or appointment requests
- Analytics such as Google Analytics
- Cookies from embedded maps, chat widgets or social feeds

## The privacy policy you genuinely need

Every site that collects data needs a privacy policy, and it has to be easy to find, usually linked in the footer on every page. This is not a place to copy a competitor's page and swap the name. Your policy should honestly describe what you collect, why, how long you keep it and who you share it with.

Write it in language a normal person understands. Say what happens when someone fills in your form, name the tools you use (your email provider, your booking system, your analytics) and tell people how to contact you if they want their data removed. A short, truthful policy beats a long, borrowed one every time. Good [copywriting](/services/website-design) here builds trust rather than burning it.

> A short, truthful privacy policy beats a long, borrowed one every single time.

## Cookies and consent without the annoying pop-up

Cookie banners have a bad reputation because most of them are done badly. The rule is straightforward: anything beyond strictly necessary cookies (the ones that make the site work) needs consent before it loads. That covers analytics and most marketing or tracking scripts.

In practice that means a simple banner letting people accept or reject non-essential cookies, and your tracking scripts holding off until they choose. The mistake to avoid is a banner with only an 'accept' button, or one that fires Google Analytics the moment the page loads. Both fall short of what the ICO expects. Done well, consent can be a two-click, unobtrusive part of the page rather than a wall between the visitor and your content.

## Forms, security and keeping data safe

Collecting data brings a duty to protect it. For a small-business site the essentials are refreshingly practical, and most are simply good web hygiene you would want anyway.

None of this is exotic. It is the baseline any decent build should include from day one, which is why we bake it into every [website we design](/services/website-design) rather than treating it as an add-on.

- A valid SSL certificate so the site runs on https and form data is encrypted
- Only asking for the information you actually need, nothing extra
- Keeping enquiries and contact details somewhere secure, not in a shared inbox forever
- Deleting old data you no longer have a reason to keep
- Keeping your site, plugins and hosting up to date so known holes get patched

## A quick compliance checklist

If you want a fast gut-check, run your site against the list below. Most local businesses can get the important parts right in an afternoon, especially with a tidy, well-built site behind them.

- You have a clear, honest privacy policy linked in the footer
- Non-essential cookies wait for consent before loading
- Every form runs over https with an SSL certificate
- You only collect data you have a genuine use for
- People can easily ask you to delete their details
- You know which third-party tools touch your visitors' data

## Where most small business sites slip up

In our experience reviewing sites, the same few gaps come up again and again: a privacy policy lifted wholesale from another business, a cookie banner that does nothing, analytics firing before consent, and forms sitting on an old site with no SSL. None of these are hard to fix, but they are easy to miss when you are busy running the actual business.

That is exactly what a free teardown is for. We record a short walkthrough of your current site and flag the practical issues, GDPR-related and otherwise, in plain English. If you would rather see the bigger picture first, our [services](/services) and transparent [pricing](/pricing) lay out how a fixed-price rebuild works, with no lock-in and no hourly surprises.

## The quick wins

- UK GDPR for a small site is mostly a privacy policy, honest cookie consent and basic security.
- You do not need a data protection officer or expensive legal advice for a typical local business website.
- Fixing the essentials is usually an afternoon's work on a well-built site, not a major project.

## Frequently asked questions

### Do I really need a privacy policy on a small business website?

Yes. If your site collects any personal data, even just names and emails from a contact form, UK GDPR requires a clear privacy policy that is easy to find. It should honestly explain what you collect, why, and how people can ask you to remove their data. It does not need to be long, but it does need to be true to how your site actually works.

### Are cookie banners a legal requirement?

If your site uses non-essential cookies, such as Google Analytics or marketing trackers, you need consent before they load, and a banner is the usual way to get it. Strictly necessary cookies that make the site function do not need consent. The key is giving people a genuine choice to accept or reject, not just an 'accept' button.

### Can GDPR problems actually get me fined?

For most small businesses the realistic risk is a complaint to the ICO rather than a large fine, and the ICO usually works with you to put things right first. That said, sloppy data handling erodes customer trust, which costs you more quietly. Getting the basics right is cheaper and simpler than dealing with a complaint later.

### How much does a compliant website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price so you know the cost up front, starting at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Every build includes SSL, a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so the compliance basics are covered from the start. Care plans to keep everything maintained start at £27 a month. You can see the full breakdown on our [pricing page](/pricing).

### How long does it take to build a new site?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on the size of the project and how quickly we get your content and photos. We are a small UK-based team you deal with directly, so there is no waiting on a call centre. A free teardown is the quickest way to get a realistic timeline for your project.

bodyMarkdown:
If your phone has gone quiet, the first instinct is usually to spend more on ads. Often the cheaper fix is the website itself. This is how a website redesign can double your enquiries: not through a clever trick, but by removing the small frictions that quietly push visitors towards a competitor. When your pages load quickly, say the right thing in the first few seconds and make the next step obvious, more of the people already finding you actually get in touch.

Doubling isn't a guarantee, and honest people won't promise it — it depends entirely on where you start. A dated site that hides your phone number and takes six seconds to load has plenty of room to improve; a tidy, modern one has less. But most small-business sites leak enquiries in the same handful of places, and those places are very fixable.

## A quiet website is usually a design problem, not a traffic problem

It's tempting to blame Google or a slow market when the work dries up. Before you accept that, look at what happens after someone lands on your site. If a hundred people visit this month and two get in touch, you may not need more visitors — you need more of those hundred to act. Nudging that from two in a hundred to four is, in plain terms, doubling your enquiries without spending another penny on being found.

This is why a redesign often beats a bigger ad budget. Paid clicks simply pour more people into the same leaky bucket. A [website redesign](/services/website-design) fixes the bucket, so every visit — paid, organic or word of mouth — is worth more. Suddenly your marketing works harder without costing you more.

## How a website redesign can double your enquiries

The mechanism is simple. Visitors decide within seconds whether a business looks trustworthy and whether getting in touch is worth the effort. A redesign works on both judgements at once: it makes you look credible, and it removes the effort. Do both well and the visitors you already have convert at a noticeably higher rate — which is where the extra enquiries come from.

Picture a plumber in Leeds whose old site opens with a stock photo and a vague welcome message. A visitor with a leaking tap has to scroll, hunt for a number, then guess whether their job is even covered. Replace that with a plain headline — what you do and where you do it — a number that taps to call on a phone, and a short enquiry form, and the same visitor is in touch in seconds. Nothing about the traffic changed. The site simply stopped getting in the way.

> Nothing about the traffic changes — the site simply stops getting in the way.

## Five changes that turn browsers into enquiries

You don't have to change everything. In our experience, five moves do most of the heavy lifting — and together they shift the visitor from 'I'll come back to this later' to 'I'll just send a quick message now':

- **A headline that answers 'can you help me?'** Say what you do and who you do it for in the first line, above the fold. Clever slogans can wait.
- **One obvious next step on every page.** A single, repeated call to action beats five competing links that pull people in different directions.
- **Proof you can trust.** Genuine reviews, recognisable logos, photos of real work and any accreditations. People buy from businesses that look like they've done the job before.
- **Contact details that follow the visitor.** A tap-to-call number in the header and a short form on every key page, so nobody has to go hunting.
- **A form that asks for less.** A name, a number and a line about the job is plenty; every extra field is another reason to give up.

## Speed, mobile and local search still decide who wins

Design tends to get the credit, but the plumbing matters just as much. Most local searches happen on a phone, often out and about on a patchy signal, so a page that takes several seconds to appear loses people before they read a word. A redesign is the natural moment to strip out the bloat, compress your images and get your pages loading quickly on mobile, where most of your visitors already are.

It's also the moment to sort out how you're found in the first place. Clear page structure, sensible headings and an up-to-date Google Business Profile all help you show up when someone nearby searches for what you offer. Pairing a redesign with proper [local SEO](/services/seo) means the better site actually gets seen, rather than sitting pretty on page three.

## What a redesign costs and how long it takes

A redesign should pay for itself, so the numbers need to be clear from the outset. We work to fixed prices rather than hourly billing, so you know the figure before anything starts — websites begin at £477, with most small-business redesigns sitting in the middle of our [fixed-price plans](/pricing). Larger rebuilds and SEO are quoted per project so the cost always fits the job.

Most sites go live in three to six weeks, and every build includes a simple admin panel so you can edit your own content, plus 30 days of aftercare. If you'd rather never touch it again, a [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month keeps everything updated, backed up and running smoothly. Throughout, you own the site, the domain and the data — there's no lock-in.

The quickest way to see where your current site leaks enquiries is to have someone walk through it with fresh eyes. That's exactly what a free teardown is: a short, screen-recorded review of your site, with the specific fixes that would make the biggest difference.

## The quick wins

- More enquiries usually come from converting the visitors you already have, not from buying more traffic.
- A clear headline, one obvious next step and easy contact details do most of the heavy lifting.
- Speed and mobile performance decide whether people stay long enough to enquire at all.
- Fixed-price redesigns start at £477 and most go live in three to six weeks, with no lock-in.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website redesign cost?

We work to fixed prices, not hourly billing, so you know the cost before we start. Websites begin at £477 for a Starter build, with Growth and Complete options for larger sites. Care plans start at £27 a month, and bigger rebuilds or SEO work are quoted per project so the figure always fits the job.

### How long does a website redesign take?

Most sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly content and photos come together. Every build includes a simple admin panel so you can make edits yourself, plus 30 days of aftercare once you're live.

### Will a redesign really double my enquiries?

Honestly, no one can guarantee that, and you should be wary of anyone who does. What we can say is that most small-business sites lose enquiries in the same few places — slow loading, an unclear message, a hidden phone number — and fixing those tends to lift how many visitors get in touch, sometimes substantially.

### Will I lose my Google rankings if I redesign?

Not if it's done carefully. Keeping your page structure sensible and putting proper redirects in place protects the rankings you already have, and a cleaner, faster site often performs better over time. Pairing the redesign with local SEO is the safest way to come out ahead rather than behind.

### Do I own the website after it's built?

Yes. You own the site, the domain and your data from day one — there's no lock-in and nothing held hostage. A care plan is available if you'd like us to handle updates and backups, but it's your choice, not a condition.

bodyMarkdown:
You paid for a website, but do you actually know if it's working? For a lot of UK small-business owners, the site goes live and then just sits there, a bit of a black box. Learning how to track your website's performance is analytics 101, and it's the difference between guessing and knowing. Once you can see what visitors actually do, you can fix what's costing you enquiries and put more into what's already working.

The good news is you don't need to be a data analyst or buy expensive software. A handful of free tools and a few numbers that genuinely matter will tell you most of what you need to know. Here's how to set it up, what to look at, and how to turn it into more phone calls and bookings.

## Start with the free tools that do the heavy lifting

You can track almost everything that matters with two free tools from Google, plus whatever comes built into your site. Between them they answer two different questions: how people find you, and what they do once they arrive.

- **Google Analytics 4 (GA4)** — the free standard for seeing how many people visit, which pages they land on, how long they stay and where they leave. It works on any website, big or small.
- **Google Search Console** — shows which search terms bring people to you, roughly where you rank for each, and any technical issues Google has flagged. It's the backbone of good [SEO](/services/seo).
- **Google Business Profile insights** — for local firms, this reveals how many people found you on Maps, called, or asked for directions. Often your biggest source of nearby customers.
- **Your form and phone log** — the numbers that pay the bills. Jot down where new enquiries heard about you, so you can tie website visits back to real work won.

## How to track your website's performance: analytics 101

At its heart, analytics is a simple loop: measure what's happening now, change one thing, then check whether the number moved. You don't need to understand every chart on the screen. Set a baseline, pick one goal — usually more enquiries — and follow the handful of metrics that feed it.

The single most useful thing you can do is set up conversion tracking. Tell Google Analytics to record when someone submits your contact form, taps your phone number or reaches a 'thank you' page. Now, instead of vanity figures like total visits, you can see how many visitors became real leads, and which pages and channels produced them.

## The numbers worth watching (and the ones to ignore)

Most dashboards throw dozens of metrics at you, and it's easy to feel buried. For a small business, only a few really earn your attention.

- **Conversions** — form submissions, calls and bookings. This is the single number that maps to money, so track it above all else.
- **Traffic sources** — are visitors arriving from Google search, social media, paid ads or word of mouth? This shows you where your effort is actually paying off.
- **Top landing pages** — which pages pull people in, and which of them turn browsers into enquiries. Your best performers usually deserve more of your attention, not less.
- **Engagement and bounce** — if people leave within seconds, the message, loading speed or [website design](/services/website-design) probably needs a rethink.
- **Mobile versus desktop** — most local searches happen on a phone, so a site that's fiddly on mobile is quietly turning customers away.

## Turn the numbers into decisions

Data is only useful when it changes what you do. Say your analytics show plenty of visitors landing on your services page but almost none filling in the form. That isn't a traffic problem, it's a conversion problem, and the fix might be a clearer heading, a photo of your team or a shorter form. If a slow page is quietly losing mobile visitors, speeding it up could win back enquiries you never knew you were missing.

Check in once a month rather than once a day. Trends over a few weeks tell a far more honest story than a single quiet Tuesday. Keeping a light [care plan](/services/care-plans) in place means someone is watching the numbers even when you're flat out running the business.

> Traffic tells you people are interested. Conversions tell you your website is doing its job.

## Keep it simple and act on what you see

It's easy to get lost building custom reports and never actually change anything. Don't. Pick two or three numbers, review them every month, and make one improvement at a time. Simple, consistent tracking beats a beautiful dashboard nobody reads. If you'd rather someone set this up properly and explain what it means in plain English, that's exactly the sort of thing our [team](/services) does, and because our prices are fixed you always know the cost up front.

## The quick wins

- You can track almost everything that matters with free tools: Google Analytics 4, Search Console and Google Business Profile.
- Conversions — calls, forms and bookings — matter far more than total visits.
- Review your numbers monthly and make one improvement at a time; consistency beats a fancy dashboard.

## Frequently asked questions

### What's the difference between Google Analytics and Search Console?

They answer different questions. Google Search Console shows how people find you — the search terms you appear for and roughly where you rank. Google Analytics 4 shows what they do once they arrive: which pages they visit, how long they stay and whether they get in touch. Most businesses want both.

### How do I know if my website is actually performing well?

Look past total visits. A site is performing when a healthy share of visitors take action — call you, fill in a form or book. Set up conversion tracking so you can see that number, then compare it month to month rather than day to day.

### How much does it cost to track my website's performance?

The essential tools are free. If you want tracking set up properly and watched over time, our care plans start at £27 a month. A full website with analytics and a simple admin panel built in starts at £477, with fixed pricing so there are no surprises — you can see the options on our [pricing](/pricing) page.

### How long does it take to see useful data, or to get a new site live?

Analytics needs a few weeks of visitors before the patterns mean anything, so give it about a month before drawing conclusions. If you need a new or redesigned site with tracking built in, most of our builds go live in three to six weeks.

title: Squarespace vs a Bespoke Website: Which Should You Choose?
description: Squarespace or a bespoke website for your UK small business? A fair, plain-English comparison of cost, SEO, design and ownership — plus how to choose.
metaTitle: Squarespace vs a Bespoke Website: Honest Guide
excerpt: A fair, jargon-free comparison of Squarespace and a bespoke website — cost, SEO, control and ownership — to help you choose the right one.

cta.heading: Not sure which is right for you?
cta.sub: Get a free teardown — a short screen-recorded review of your current site, with honest, practical pointers on what to fix and what to keep.

faq.q: Is Squarespace bad for SEO?
faq.a: No, Squarespace can rank, especially for less competitive searches. But its templates add code you don't need, and you are limited by what the platform allows. For competitive local searches, a leaner bespoke build gives you more control over speed and structure, which makes optimising far easier.

faq.q: How much does a bespoke website cost?
faq.a: Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front. They start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or redesigns are quoted per project. You own everything, with no lock-in.

faq.q: How long does it take to build a bespoke website?
faq.a: Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you are supported once you are live.

faq.q: Can I move my Squarespace site to a custom build?
faq.a: Yes. Your domain and content can transfer, and a careful migration protects the search rankings you have already built. We handle the technical side so you don't lose traffic in the switch.

faq.q: Which is better for a small local business?
faq.a: If your website drives real enquiries or bookings, a bespoke build usually pays off through better speed, SEO and design. If you are testing an idea on a tight budget, Squarespace is a sensible start. The right answer depends on your stage.

bodyMarkdown:
If you run a local business, the choice often comes down to this: build it yourself on Squarespace, or commission a studio for a bespoke website. Both can look smart. Both can bring in enquiries. But they suit different businesses at different stages, and the wrong call can cost you months of lost search traffic. Here is a fair, plain-English look at Squarespace vs a bespoke website, so you can choose with your eyes open.

## What Squarespace does well

Let's start by being fair to Squarespace, because it has real strengths. For a brand-new business testing an idea, it is quick, tidy and affordable. You get attractive templates, hosting bundled in, and an editor you can learn in an afternoon. If you sell a handful of products or just need a simple presence online, it will do the job without a big upfront bill.

For a lot of side projects, market traders and early-stage founders, that is exactly the right call. There is no sense commissioning a custom build for something you are still shaping. Squarespace lets you get online this week and change your mind next month, and that flexibility has genuine value when nothing is settled yet.

Where Squarespace starts to strain is when your ambitions grow. The templates that made setup easy become the ceiling you keep bumping into, and the monthly fee keeps ticking over whether the site is earning its keep or not.

## Where a bespoke website earns its keep

A [bespoke website](/services/website-design) is built around your business, not squeezed into someone else's template. That difference shows up exactly where it matters to a serious local business: how fast your pages load, how well they rank, and how easily the site handles the specific way you win customers.

Think of a plumber who gets most of his work from 'emergency plumber near me', or a dental practice competing with three others on the same high street. Winning those searches depends on clean code, fast pages and a structure built for [local SEO](/services/seo). Template builders get you part of the way, but you are always working within what the platform allows. A custom build gives you the room to do it properly.

There is also the matter of standing out. When a dozen firms in your town all pick from the same popular templates, your site starts to look like everyone else's. A design made for your brand, with copy written around the services you actually want to sell, does quiet work every day to make you the obvious choice.

> A bespoke site is built around how your business actually wins customers — not around a template's limits.

## Squarespace vs a Bespoke Website: The Trade-offs

No option wins on everything. Here is how the two stack up on the things local business owners tell us matter most:

- **Upfront cost**: Squarespace is cheaper to start; a bespoke build is a larger one-off investment that you own outright.
- **Ongoing cost**: Squarespace charges a subscription for as long as you use it; a custom site runs on low-cost hosting, with an optional [care plan](/services/care-plans) only if you want hands-off support.
- **Speed and SEO**: templates carry code you'll never use, which can slow pages; a hand-built site is leaner and simpler to optimise.
- **Design**: templates look polished but familiar; a bespoke design reflects your brand and can be shaped around your best services.
- **Ownership**: on Squarespace you rent the platform; with a custom build you own the site, the domain and the data, with no lock-in.
- **Room to grow**: booking systems, memberships or unusual features are simple to add to a bespoke site and often awkward on a template.

## The real cost, over time

It is tempting to compare only the upfront price, but the honest comparison is what each option costs over three or four years. Squarespace looks cheaper on day one, then keeps charging every month for as long as your site exists. A bespoke build is a bigger cheque up front, after which your running costs can be modest — hosting and, if you want it, a maintenance plan.

For a hobby site, the subscription barely registers. For a growing business, those monthly fees add up while you are still boxed in by the platform's limits. When your website is winning you customers, paying once for something you own and control often works out both cheaper and more profitable in the long run.

## So which should you choose?

The honest answer is that it depends on where your business is right now.

If you are testing an idea, watching every pound, and happy to build it yourself, start with Squarespace. There is no shame in it, and you can always move up later.

If your website is a real source of income — if enquiries, bookings or sales genuinely depend on it — a bespoke build usually pays for itself. You get a faster site, stronger search performance and a design that sets you apart from competitors using the very same templates. You can compare our [fixed-price packages](/pricing) to see what that costs, or browse the [types of business we build for](/industries).

Still unsure? That is normal — most owners are weighing budget against ambition. The useful question is not 'which is better?' but 'which is right for where my business is heading over the next two years?'

## Can you switch later?

Plenty of the owners we work with started on a DIY builder and outgrew it, so this is not a decision you are locked into forever. Your domain and content can come with you, and a well-planned migration protects the rankings you have already earned. Moving sooner rather than later simply means less to redo — and less time spent limited by a template while your competitors pull ahead.

## The quick wins

- Squarespace is a strong, low-cost start for testing an idea or running a simple online presence.
- A bespoke website wins on speed, SEO control, design and true ownership — which matters once the site drives real income.
- Choose by stage: DIY while you are figuring it out, custom when enquiries and bookings depend on your site.
- Compare cost over three to four years, not just day one — subscriptions add up while a site you own keeps running cheaply.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is Squarespace bad for SEO?

No, Squarespace can rank, especially for less competitive searches. But its templates add code you don't need, and you are limited by what the platform allows. For competitive local searches, a leaner bespoke build gives you more control over speed and structure, which makes optimising far easier.

### How much does a bespoke website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the cost up front. They start at £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or redesigns are quoted per project. You own everything, with no lock-in.

### How long does it take to build a bespoke website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare, so you are supported once you are live.

### Can I move my Squarespace site to a custom build?

Yes. Your domain and content can transfer, and a careful migration protects the search rankings you have already built. We handle the technical side so you don't lose traffic in the switch.

### Which is better for a small local business?

If your website drives real enquiries or bookings, a bespoke build usually pays off through better speed, SEO and design. If you are testing an idea on a tight budget, Squarespace is a sensible start. The right answer depends on your stage.

intro: If you run a local business, the choice often comes down to this: build it yourself on Squarespace, or commission a studio for a bespoke website. Both can look smart. Both can bring in enquiries. But they suit different businesses at different stages, and the wrong call can cost you months of lost search traffic. Here is a fair, plain-English look at Squarespace vs a bespoke website, so you can choose with your eyes open.

sections.h2: What Squarespace does well
paragraphs: Let's start by being fair to Squarespace, because it has real strengths. For a brand-new business testing an idea, it is quick, tidy and affordable. You get attractive templates, hosting bundled in, and an editor you can learn in an afternoon. If you sell a handful of products or just need a simple presence online, it will do the job without a big upfront bill.

For a lot of side projects, market traders and early-stage founders, that is exactly the right call. There is no sense commissioning a custom build for something you are still shaping. Squarespace lets you get online this week and change your mind next month, and that flexibility has genuine value when nothing is settled yet.

Where Squarespace starts to strain is when your ambitions grow. The templates that made setup easy become the ceiling you keep bumping into, and the monthly fee keeps ticking over whether the site is earning its keep or not.

sections.h2: Where a bespoke website earns its keep
paragraphs: A [bespoke website](/services/website-design) is built around your business, not squeezed into someone else's template. That difference shows up exactly where it matters to a serious local business: how fast your pages load, how well they rank, and how easily the site handles the specific way you win customers.

Think of a plumber who gets most of his work from 'emergency plumber near me', or a dental practice competing with three others on the same high street. Winning those searches depends on clean code, fast pages and a structure built for [local SEO](/services/seo). Template builders get you part of the way, but you are always working within what the platform allows. A custom build gives you the room to do it properly.

There is also the matter of standing out. When a dozen firms in your town all pick from the same popular templates, your site starts to look like everyone else's. A design made for your brand, with copy written around the services you actually want to sell, does quiet work every day to make you the obvious choice.
sections.pullquote: A bespoke site is built around how your business actually wins customers — not around a template's limits.

sections.h2: Squarespace vs a Bespoke Website: The Trade-offs
paragraphs: No option wins on everything. Here is how the two stack up on the things local business owners tell us matter most:
bullets: **Upfront cost**: Squarespace is cheaper to start; a bespoke build is a larger one-off investment that you own outright.

**Ongoing cost**: Squarespace charges a subscription for as long as you use it; a custom site runs on low-cost hosting, with an optional [care plan](/services/care-plans) only if you want hands-off support.
**Speed and SEO**: templates carry code you'll never use, which can slow pages; a hand-built site is leaner and simpler to optimise.
**Design**: templates look polished but familiar; a bespoke design reflects your brand and can be shaped around your best services.
**Ownership**: on Squarespace you rent the platform; with a custom build you own the site, the domain and the data, with no lock-in.
**Room to grow**: booking systems, memberships or unusual features are simple to add to a bespoke site and often awkward on a template.

sections.h2: The real cost, over time
paragraphs: It is tempting to compare only the upfront price, but the honest comparison is what each option costs over three or four years. Squarespace looks cheaper on day one, then keeps charging every month for as long as your site exists. A bespoke build is a bigger cheque up front, after which your running costs can be modest — hosting and, if you want it, a maintenance plan.

For a hobby site, the subscription barely registers. For a growing business, those monthly fees add up while you are still boxed in by the platform's limits. When your website is winning you customers, paying once for something you own and control often works out both cheaper and more profitable in the long run.

sections.h2: So which should you choose?
paragraphs: The honest answer is that it depends on where your business is right now.
If you are testing an idea, watching every pound, and happy to build it yourself, start with Squarespace. There is no shame in it, and you can always move up later.

If your website is a real source of income — if enquiries, bookings or sales genuinely depend on it — a bespoke build usually pays for itself. You get a faster site, stronger search performance and a design that sets you apart from competitors using the very same templates. You can compare our [fixed-price packages](/pricing) to see what that costs, or browse the [types of business we build for](/industries).

Still unsure? That is normal — most owners are weighing budget against ambition. The useful question is not 'which is better?' but 'which is right for where my business is heading over the next two years?'

bodyMarkdown:
Building a business website used to mean hiring someone and waiting weeks. Now you can drag, drop and publish one yourself before lunch. That is exactly why the DIY website vs hiring an agency pros and cons question matters so much: the wrong call costs you either real money or wasted months. This guide weighs both routes fairly, then makes the honest case for a professional build when your website has a real job to do.

There is no single right answer. A weekend market stall and a growing dental practice have very different needs and very different budgets. What follows is the version most sales pages skip — the genuinely good and the slightly awkward parts of each option — so you can choose with your eyes open.

## The pros and cons of a DIY website

Website builders like Wix, Squarespace and Shopify have come a long way. For a brand-new business testing an idea, a DIY site can be a sensible first step. You keep costs low, you stay in control, and you can have something live the same week. Those are real advantages, and it would be dishonest to pretend otherwise.

The catch is what a builder quietly leaves to you. Your time is not free, and hours spent wrestling with templates are hours away from paying customers. Most builder templates also look like thousands of others, so you blend in exactly when you want to stand out. And the parts that actually win customers — fast loading, a clean mobile layout, local SEO and clear copy — are the parts DIY tools make easiest to skip.

- Low upfront cost — you pay a monthly subscription rather than a project fee.
- Speed off the mark — a simple one-page site can go live in an afternoon.
- Full hands-on control — you tweak your own text and images whenever you like.
- A gentle learning curve — you get to understand how your own website works.

## What you are really paying an agency for

Hiring a studio is not just buying a better-looking website. You are buying judgement, time back, and someone who answers the phone when something breaks. A good agency turns your vague ideas into a site built to bring in enquiries, not just to exist. That is the real difference you are paying for.

With us, it also means you own everything — your site, your domain and your data — with no lock-in. Most builds go live in three to six weeks, and every one includes a simple admin panel and thirty days of aftercare while you settle in.

- A design shaped around your customers and your industry, not a stock template.
- Copy written to turn visitors into enquiries, plus proper on-page [SEO](/services/seo).
- A simple admin panel so you can update the site yourself without breaking it.
- Fixed pricing up front, so you know the cost before anyone starts work.
- Aftercare and optional [care plans](/services/care-plans) that keep the site fast and secure.

## DIY website vs hiring an agency: the real trade-offs

Strip away the marketing and the choice comes down to three things: cost, time and results. DIY wins on upfront cost. It can also win on speed if all you need is a simple placeholder. But an agency build tends to win on results — a faster site, better local visibility and a design built to convert the visitors you work hard to attract. Cheap is only cheap if the website actually earns its keep.

It is also worth being honest about the hidden costs of DIY. The subscription, the premium template, the stock photos, the plugins — and above all your own time — add up quietly. You can compare that against our transparent [pricing](/pricing) and decide which is genuinely better value for your business.

> A DIY site saves you money up front. A well-built one earns it back every month it brings in enquiries.

## Where DIY quietly loses local customers

Most of your customers are searching on a phone, often for something nearby and urgent — 'emergency dentist near me', 'plumber open now', 'best Sunday roast in town'. Turning up in those searches takes more than a tidy homepage. It takes a fast, mobile-first site, sensible page structure, a well-kept Google Business Profile and content that answers what local people actually type.

DIY builders can technically do all of this, but they rarely do it well by default, and it is the first thing that slips when you are busy running a business. This is usually where a professional build earns its money — not in the design, but in being found at all.

## Which route fits your business?

If you are testing an idea, running a side project or working to a tiny budget, a DIY website is a perfectly reasonable place to start. There is no shame in it, and you can always upgrade later. The key is to be clear about what you are actually trying to achieve.

But if your website is a genuine source of customers — a dentist, a builder, a solicitor, a restaurant — the sums change quickly. One or two extra enquiries a month can cover a professional build many times over. At that point a custom [website design](/services/website-design) usually pays for itself, and your time is far better spent running the business than editing a template.

Not sure which camp you are in? The fastest way to find out is to let a fresh pair of eyes look at your current site. We offer a free teardown — a short, screen-recorded review of what is working, what is costing you enquiries and what to fix first. No pressure and no jargon; just [get in touch](/contact) and we will take a look.

## The quick wins

- A DIY website is a fair starting point for a new idea or a very tight budget.
- An agency build pays off when your website is a real source of customers.
- The true cost of DIY includes your time, subscriptions and lost enquiries.
- With a fixed-price build you own your site, domain and data, with no lock-in.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is a DIY website ever good enough?

Yes. For a brand-new idea, a side project or a very tight budget, a DIY website is a sensible first step. It gets you online cheaply while you test whether the business works. The time to move on is when the site becomes a real source of enquiries and its limits start costing you customers.

### How much does a professional website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the figure before we start. Starter builds begin at £477, Growth at £1,170 and Complete at £2,370. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO work or redesigns are quoted per project. No hourly billing and no surprises.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly you can get us your content. Every build includes a simple admin panel and thirty days of aftercare once you are live.

### If an agency builds my site, do I own it?

Yes. You own your site, your domain and your data, with no lock-in. If you ever decide to move on, everything comes with you — which is not always true of DIY platforms, where your content can be tied to their system.

### Can I start with a DIY website and switch to an agency later?

Absolutely, and many businesses do exactly that. Starting DIY is a fine way to test the water. When you are ready to grow, we can rebuild it properly and carry over what already works — usually with a much clearer idea of what your customers respond to.

description: Shopify vs custom e-commerce compared fairly for UK small businesses — see which online shop fits your budget, products and your plans to grow.

bodyMarkdown:
If you sell online — or you're about to — sooner or later you reach the same fork in the road: Shopify vs custom e-commerce, and what's best for you. It's a genuinely good question, and the honest answer is that it depends on your products, your margins and how much you plan to grow. So let's walk through both options fairly, in plain English, so you can choose with your eyes open.

We build [online shops](/services/website-design) for UK small businesses, so we've watched both approaches succeed and both come unstuck. This isn't a pitch for one side over the other. It's the same conversation we have with clients before a single line of code gets written.

## What Shopify gets right

Shopify is a hosted platform, which is a tidy way of saying someone else looks after the plumbing. You pay a monthly fee and the hosting, security, software updates and payment setup are handled for you. For a lot of businesses that trade-off is exactly the right one.

If you're testing a new idea, selling a handful of products, or you simply want the lightest possible setup, Shopify is a sensible, respectable choice. Plenty of thriving UK shops run on it, and there's no shame in starting there while you find your feet.

- You can be up and selling quickly, often within days.
- Hosting, security certificates and PCI payment compliance are taken care of.
- It copes well with sudden spikes in traffic, like a Black Friday rush.
- There's an app store for almost any feature you can think of.
- The monthly cost is predictable and easy to budget for.

## Where a custom build pulls ahead

A custom e-commerce site is built around your business rather than the other way round. Instead of bending your shop to fit a template and a stack of monthly apps, the site is shaped to how you actually sell.

That matters most when your products are unusual, your checkout needs to work in a particular way, or you lean on other systems — an EPOS till, a booking calendar, your accountant's software. A custom build can connect to those directly. You also get full control over design, page speed and the technical details that feed into [good search rankings](/services/seo), which is harder to fine-tune on a locked-down template.

Say you run a Yorkshire farm shop that also opens a weekend café and sells hampers at Christmas. A custom site can handle stock, table bookings and seasonal bundles in one place, rather than three separate apps bolted awkwardly together.

> The real question isn't which platform is cheaper this month — it's which one still fits when you've doubled your orders.

## The questions that actually decide it

Forget the brand names for a moment. The right answer usually falls out of a few honest questions about your business, not out of a feature list.

If your answers lean towards simple, few products and a tight budget, a hosted platform tends to win. If they lean towards distinct products, connected systems and real growth plans, custom tends to pay you back.

- How many products do you sell, and how often do they change?
- Do you need to connect the shop to a till, booking system or accounting software?
- What are your margins — can you comfortably absorb transaction fees and monthly app subscriptions?
- Do you need full control of your design and checkout, or is a smart template genuinely fine?
- Where do you expect the business to be in two or three years?

## The costs people forget to add up

The sticker price is never the whole story on either side. With Shopify, the monthly plan is only the start. Transaction fees can apply if you don't use its own payment system, and the apps that add reviews, subscriptions or product bundles each carry their own monthly charge. They stack up quietly, and you're renting the platform rather than owning it.

A custom build usually costs more up front and needs looking after — that's what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for. But there's no per-sale platform tax beyond your payment processor, and no app subscriptions creeping onto the bill every month. Over a few years, the sums often look very different from how they look in month one. You can see [our fixed prices](/pricing) rather than guess at a moving total.

## Shopify vs custom e-commerce: our honest take

Here's where we come down, and we'll not pretend otherwise. If you're a serious local business — a shop, a studio, a maker with a brand that matters and orders you want to grow — a professional build usually earns its keep. You own the site, the domain and your data, so you're never locked in. There's no monthly app creep, and it's yours to shape as the business changes.

That said, we'd tell you plainly if Shopify were the smarter call for where you are right now. Most of our shops go live in three to six weeks, at a fixed price agreed before we start, with 30 days of aftercare and a simple admin panel so you can manage products yourself. If you're not sure which side of the line you're on, a free teardown of your current setup is the quickest way to find out — a short, screen-recorded review with honest, specific advice and no obligation. You can tell us where you're stuck over on the [contact page](/contact).

## The quick wins

- Shopify is fast, hosted and low-hassle — ideal for testing an idea or selling a few products.
- A custom build wins on ownership, connected systems and long-term control as you grow.
- Add up the real costs: monthly apps and transaction fees on one side, upkeep on the other.
- The best choice depends on your products, margins and growth plans, not the brand name.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does an e-commerce website cost?

Our websites are fixed-price, so you know the total before we start: from **£477** for a Starter site, **£1,170** for Growth and **£2,370** for Complete. Care plans start at **£27 a month**, and SEO or a redesign of an existing shop is quoted per project.

### How long does it take to build?

Most of our sites go live in **three to six weeks**, depending on how many products you have and how quickly we get your content and images. We agree the timeline with you up front, so there are no surprises.

### Is Shopify cheaper than a custom site?

In the first month, usually yes. Over two or three years the gap narrows, because monthly plans, paid apps and transaction fees add up, while a custom build has no per-sale platform cut beyond your payment processor. It comes down to your sales volume and how many apps you'd otherwise need.

### Can I move from Shopify to a custom site later?

Yes. Your products, customers and orders can be exported and brought across. Because a custom build means you own your site, domain and data outright, you're never locked in — which is exactly the freedom a hosted platform can't fully give you.

### Which is better for SEO?

Both can rank well, but a custom build gives you more control over page speed, structure and technical detail, all of which feed into search. Whichever route you take, good local SEO and clear, genuinely useful content matter more than the platform badge.

It's a genuinely good question, and the honest answer is that it depends on your products, your margins and how much you plan to grow. So let's walk through both options fairly, in plain English, so you can choose with your eyes open.

We build [online shops](/services/website-design) for UK small businesses, so we've watched both approaches succeed and both come unstuck. This isn't a pitch for one side over the other. It's the same conversation we have with clients before a single line of code gets written.

sections.h2: What Shopify gets right
paragraphs: Shopify is a hosted platform, which is a tidy way of saying someone else looks after the plumbing. You pay a monthly fee and the hosting, security, software updates and payment setup are handled for you. For a lot of businesses that trade-off is exactly the right one.

If you're testing a new idea, selling a handful of products, or you simply want the lightest possible setup, Shopify is a sensible, respectable choice. Plenty of thriving UK shops run on it, and there's no shame in starting there while you find your feet.
bullets: You can be up and selling quickly, often within days.
Hosting, security certificates and PCI payment compliance are taken care of.
It copes well with sudden spikes in traffic, like a Black Friday rush.
There's an app store for almost any feature you can think of.
The monthly cost is predictable and easy to budget for.

sections.h2: Where a custom build pulls ahead
paragraphs: A custom e-commerce site is built around your business rather than the other way round. Instead of bending your shop to fit a template and a stack of monthly apps, the site is shaped to how you actually sell.

That matters most when your products are unusual, your checkout needs to work in a particular way, or you lean on other systems — an EPOS till, a booking calendar, your accountant's software. A custom build can connect to those directly. You also get full control over design, page speed and the technical details that feed into [good search rankings](/services/seo), which is harder to fine-tune on a locked-down template.

Say you run a Yorkshire farm shop that also opens a weekend café and sells hampers at Christmas. A custom site can handle stock, table bookings and seasonal bundles in one place, rather than three separate apps bolted awkwardly together.
sections.pullquote: The real question isn't which platform is cheaper this month — it's which one still fits when you've doubled your orders.

sections.h2: The questions that actually decide it
paragraphs: Forget the brand names for a moment. The right answer usually falls out of a few honest questions about your business, not out of a feature list.

If your answers lean towards simple, few products and a tight budget, a hosted platform tends to win. If they lean towards distinct products, connected systems and real growth plans, custom tends to pay you back.
bullets: How many products do you sell, and how often do they change?
Do you need to connect the shop to a till, booking system or accounting software?
What are your margins — can you comfortably absorb transaction fees and monthly app subscriptions?
Do you need full control of your design and checkout, or is a smart template genuinely fine?
Where do you expect the business to be in two or three years?

sections.h2: The costs people forget to add up
paragraphs: The sticker price is never the whole story on either side. With Shopify, the monthly plan is only the start. Transaction fees can apply if you don't use its own payment system, and the apps that add reviews, subscriptions or product bundles each carry their own monthly charge. They stack up quietly, and you're renting the platform rather than owning it.

A custom build usually costs more up front and needs looking after — that's what a [care plan](/services/care-plans) is for. But there's no per-sale platform tax beyond your payment processor, and no app subscriptions creeping onto the bill every month. Over a few years, the sums often look very different from how they look in month one. You can see [our fixed prices](/pricing) rather than guess at a moving total.

sections.h2: Shopify vs custom e-commerce: our honest take
paragraphs: Here's where we come down, and we'll not pretend otherwise. If you're a serious local business — a shop, a studio, a maker with a brand that matters and orders you want to grow — a professional build usually earns its keep. You own the site, the domain and your data, so you're never locked in. There's no monthly app creep, and it's yours to shape as the business changes.

That said, we'd tell you plainly if Shopify were the smarter call for where you are right now. Most of our shops go live in three to six weeks, at a fixed price agreed before we start, with 30 days of aftercare and a simple admin panel so you can manage products yourself. If you're not sure which side of the line you're on, a free teardown of your current setup is the quickest way to find out — a short, screen-recorded review with honest, specific advice and no obligation. You can tell us where you're stuck over on the [contact page](/contact).

keyTakeaways: Shopify is fast, hosted and low-hassle — ideal for testing an idea or selling a few products.
A custom build wins on ownership, connected systems and long-term control as you grow.
Add up the real costs: monthly apps and transaction fees on one side, upkeep on the other.
The best choice depends on your products, margins and growth plans, not the brand name.

title: Freelancer vs Web Design Agency: Which to Hire?
description: Freelancer or web design agency? An honest, plain-English comparison of cost, risk and results to help your small business choose the right web partner.
metaTitle: Freelancer vs Web Design Agency: Which to Hire?
excerpt: A freelancer or a web design agency can both build a good site, but one suits a serious local business better. Here's how to choose.
targetKeyword: freelancer vs web design agency which to hire

cta.heading: Still weighing it up?

faq.q: How long does it take to build a website?

bodyMarkdown:
You have decided your business needs a proper website, and now comes the fork in the road: hire a freelancer, or hire a web design agency. The freelancer vs web design agency decision comes down to far more than the headline price, and choosing wrong can cost you months as well as money. This guide lays out both options fairly, so you can work out which to hire for your particular business.

There is no single right answer for everyone. A one-chair barber and a growing plumbing firm have different needs, different budgets and a different appetite for risk. What follows is the honest version, written by a small UK studio that has rebuilt plenty of sites left behind by freelancers and agencies alike.

## What a freelancer actually gives you

A freelancer is one person, usually working from home or a small office, who takes your project from start to finish. The good ones are a pleasure to work with. You speak to the person doing the actual work, decisions happen quickly, and because their overheads are low, a simple brochure site can come in at a very fair price.

The trade-off is that one person is one point of failure. Holidays, illness, a bigger client or simply a full calendar can all stall your project. And no single person is brilliant at design, development, copywriting and SEO at once, so something usually gets less attention than it deserves.

- Lower rates and less overhead, so a small, well-defined site often costs less up front.
- A direct relationship with the maker, with no account manager in the middle.
- Fast to get moving when the brief is clear and the job is small.

## What a web design agency actually gives you

An agency is a team. That usually means a designer, a developer, someone who writes the words and someone who understands search, all under one roof. The obvious benefit is cover and range: if one person is away, the work carries on, and each part of your [web design and build](/services/website-design) is handled by someone who does that thing every day.

The trade-offs are cost and distance. Agencies carry more overhead, so you pay more, and at larger firms you often deal with an account manager rather than the people actually building your site. A few also tie you to their own platform or hosting, which feels convenient until the day you want to leave.

## Freelancer vs web design agency: which to hire

So, freelancer vs web design agency, which to hire? Price is the wrong place to start. Begin with what your business actually needs from the site over the next few years, then be honest about how much risk you can carry. These questions sort most people quickly:

If your answers point to a simple site, a tight budget and a short timescale, a trusted freelancer may be exactly right. If they point to something more involved, or you simply cannot afford for the project to stall, the reliability of a team earns its keep.

- How complex is the site? A simple brochure is a different job from online booking, payments or a large product catalogue.
- Do you need more than one skill? Great design, sharp copy and solid SEO rarely live in the same single person.
- What happens if your one contact goes quiet for a fortnight in the middle of the build?
- Do you want a one-off build, or someone to keep the site updated and secure afterwards?
- At the end, who owns the site, the domain and the content, you or the person you paid?

## The costs nobody puts in the quote

The cheapest quote rarely tells the whole story. It is worth asking what happens after launch, because that is where hidden costs hide. Who fixes it when a plugin breaks? What does a change to your opening hours cost? Can you edit the site yourself, or do you pay every time? Our own [fixed-price plans](/pricing) exist for exactly this reason, you know the number before any work starts, with no hourly meter running.

Ownership matters just as much. You should walk away owning your site, your domain and your data outright, with **no lock-in**. If a provider cannot say that plainly, treat it as a warning sign.

> The cheapest quote and the cheapest project are rarely the same thing.

## Where a small studio sits between the two

There is a middle path, and it is where we deliberately sit. A small studio is personal like a freelancer, you deal with us directly and we pick up the phone, but structured like an agency, so no single holiday or illness stops your project. Every build is fixed-price, most sites go live in three to six weeks, and each one comes with a simple admin panel and thirty days of aftercare so you are never left stuck.

**You own everything at the end**, and if you want us to keep the site fast, secure and updated, a [care plan](/services/care-plans) starts from £27 a month. Need to be found locally as well as online? We handle [local SEO](/services/seo) too. You can see the kind of work this produces across our [recent projects](/work).

Whichever way you lean, do not choose on price alone. If you would like a second opinion first, a free teardown is a short screen-recorded review of your current site, with clear, honest pointers you can use whether you hire us or not.

## The quick wins

- Freelancers can be excellent value for small, simple sites, but you are relying on one person's time, skills and availability.
- Agencies bring a full team and process, at a higher cost and sometimes at arm's length from the people doing the work.
- For a serious local business the deciding factors are usually ownership, reliability and ongoing support, not the headline price.

## Frequently asked questions

### Is a freelancer cheaper than an agency?

Usually a freelancer costs less up front, because the overheads are lower. But the cheapest quote is not always the cheapest project. Factor in revisions, ongoing fixes and what happens if the work stalls, and the gap often narrows. Compare the total cost of getting a finished, owned and maintained site, not just the day rate.

### How much does a small-business website cost?

We work to fixed prices, so you know the figure before we start. Our websites are £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete, with care plans from £27 a month. SEO and full redesigns are quoted per project, because every one is different.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live within three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly we get your content and photos. A simple brochure site is quicker; booking systems and larger catalogues take a little longer.

### Will I own my website if I use an agency?

You should, but always ask before you sign. With us, you own your site, your domain and your data outright, with no lock-in, so you are free to take everything elsewhere if you ever choose to. Not every provider offers that, so it is worth checking.

### Can a small studio handle SEO as well as the build?

Yes. A good build is only useful if people can find it, so search is part of the plan from the start, not an afterthought bolted on later. That covers your Google Business Profile and being found for local searches too.

title: Template vs Custom Website Design: Which Is Right for Your Business?
description: Template vs custom website design, compared fairly for UK firms: cost, speed, SEO and control. See which fits your goals, then book a free teardown.

bodyMarkdown:
If you are weighing up a template vs custom website design for your business, you have probably heard the two extremes. One camp swears a twenty-pound-a-month template is all any small business ever needs. The other insists anything short of a bespoke build is money down the drain. Neither is quite true, and the honest answer depends on what you actually need your website to do.

This is a fair look at both sides. Templates are fast and cheap for a reason, and for some businesses they are the sensible choice. But if your website is meant to bring in enquiries, get found in your local area and grow with you, a professional build usually earns its keep. Here is how to tell which side of the line you are on.

## Template vs custom website design: the honest difference

A template is a pre-made design you drop your words and photos into, think Wix, Squarespace or a themed WordPress layout. You are renting a structure that thousands of other businesses also use. A custom build starts from your goals and your brand instead: the layout, the copy and the way the pages fit together are designed around how your customers actually decide to buy.

Both can look smart on launch day. The difference tends to show up later, in how easily you can change things, how well the site gets found on Google, and how much control you really have over your own business online.

## Where templates genuinely make sense

For plenty of businesses, a template is a perfectly good starting point. If you are testing an idea, running a side project, or you simply need a tidy presence online, there is no shame in it at all.

A template tends to work well in a handful of situations:

If most of those describe you, a template will do the job for now. The trouble usually starts when a business quietly outgrows one and only notices after the enquiries have stalled.

- You need something live this week and the budget is genuinely tight.
- Your site is mainly a brochure, a few pages with no bookings or shop.
- You are comfortable building and editing it yourself.
- You do not yet rely on the website to bring in customers.
- You are happy to move to something stronger once the business grows.

## Where a custom build pays off

A [custom website design](/services/website-design) is worth it when the site has a real job to do. For a local dentist, tradesperson or restaurant, that job is usually the same: be found by nearby customers and turn those visits into phone calls and bookings.

A professional build gives you a few things a template rarely does well:

- A structure built around your services and your area, so [local SEO](/services/seo) has something solid to work with.
- Faster loading, because the code is not padded with features you never use, and most local searches happen on a phone where speed matters.
- Copy written to answer the questions your customers actually ask before they buy.
- A simple admin panel, so you can change prices, hours and photos without touching code.
- Room to add booking, quotes or a shop later, without rebuilding from scratch.

> A template asks you to fit your business into its shape. A custom build fits the site around how your customers already decide.

## The costs people forget to compare

On paper a template looks far cheaper, and up front it is. But a fair comparison counts the whole picture, not just the monthly fee.

Templates carry costs that are easy to miss: the hours you spend wrestling with the builder, the paid add-ons needed for basic features, and the ceiling you hit when the design simply cannot do what you need next. Many owners end up paying twice, once for the template and again for the proper rebuild it was always going to need.

A good studio should be upfront about all of this. Our websites start from £477 and come at [fixed prices](/pricing), so you know the full cost before we begin, no hourly billing and no surprise invoices later.

## Control and ownership: the part nobody mentions

There is one more difference that rarely makes the sales pitch: who is actually in charge of your website. With many template platforms you are renting space on their terms. Prices can rise, features can change, and moving away can mean rebuilding from scratch because your content is locked inside their system.

With a properly built site you should own the lot, the design, the domain and the data. That means you can change host, switch agency or hand it to someone in-house whenever you like, with no permission needed. It is your shopfront, so you should be the one holding the keys.

## How to choose for a serious local business

Ask yourself one honest question: is your website a nice-to-have, or is it meant to bring in work? If it is the second, treat it as the investment it is rather than the cheapest box to tick.

Be practical about the ongoing side too. A site is never truly finished on launch day. It needs updates, security patches and the odd tweak, which is where a [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month keeps everything ticking over without you having to think about it. Whichever route you choose, go in with your eyes open about both the up-front price and the years that follow.

## The quick wins

- Templates suit tight budgets, simple brochure sites and businesses still testing an idea.
- Custom builds pay off when the site must be found locally and turn visitors into enquiries.
- Compare the whole cost, not just the monthly fee, because templates are often rebuilt later.
- You should own your site, domain and data outright, whichever route you take.

## Frequently asked questions

### How much does a website cost with Nexus Lab Systems?

Our websites are fixed-price and start from £477 for a Starter site, £1,170 for Growth and £2,370 for Complete. Care plans start at £27 a month, and SEO or redesigns are quoted per project. You always know the full cost before any work begins.

### How long does it take to build a website?

Most of our sites go live in three to six weeks, depending on how many pages you need and how quickly you can get us your content. Every build includes a simple admin panel and 30 days of aftercare once you are live.

### Can I start with a template and move to a custom site later?

Yes, and plenty of businesses do exactly that. The main thing is to keep ownership of your domain and content so the move stays painless. When enquiries start to matter more than the monthly saving, that is usually the moment to switch.

### Is a custom website worth it for a small local business?

If your website is meant to bring in enquiries, it usually is. A build designed around your services and area gives local search something solid to work with and turns more visitors into calls and bookings, which a template often cannot do as well.

### Do I own the website if you build it?

Yes. You own your site, domain and data outright, with no lock-in. We are a small UK team you deal with directly, and if you ever want to move on, everything is yours to take with you.

With a properly built site you should own the lot, the design, the domain and the data. That means you can change host, switch agency or hand it to someone in-house whenever you like, with no permission needed. It is your shopfront, so you should be the one holding the keys.

sections.h2: How to choose for a serious local business
paragraphs: Ask yourself one honest question: is your website a nice-to-have, or is it meant to bring in work? If it is the second, treat it as the investment it is rather than the cheapest box to tick.

Be practical about the ongoing side too. A site is never truly finished on launch day. It needs updates, security patches and the odd tweak, which is where a [care plan](/services/care-plans) from £27 a month keeps everything ticking over without you having to think about it. Whichever route you choose, go in with your eyes open about both the up-front price and the years that follow.

keyTakeaways: Templates suit tight budgets, simple brochure sites and businesses still testing an idea.
Custom builds pay off when the site must be found locally and turn visitors into enquiries.
Compare the whole cost, not just the monthly fee, because templates are often rebuilt later.
You should own your site, domain and data outright, whichever route you take.
